topics.title: Introduction to Software Testing - Concepts, importance of testing, software quality, and real-world failures (e.g., Ariane 5, Therac 25)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Software Testing - Concepts, importance of testing, software quality, and real-world failures (e.g., Ariane 5, Therac 25)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Software Testing? (KTU 2024 Definition)

**Software Testing** is the systematic process of evaluating and verifying that a software application or system meets its specified requirements and functions correctly. It involves executing software components using manual or automated tools to evaluate one or more properties of interest, with the goal of finding defects, validating functionality, and ensuring that the software is fit for use.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Definition (per ISTQB / IEEE 829):**
> *"Software Testing is the process of executing a program or system with the intent of finding defects. It is an empirical, technical investigation conducted to provide stakeholders with information about the quality of the product or service under test."*

In the most widely cited formulation, **Myers (1979)** stated the canonical purpose:

> *"Testing is the process of executing a program with the intent of finding errors."*

This negative definition is pivotal in KTU examinations — students should NOT define testing as "the process of showing that the program works." Instead, testing must be framed as a **defect-detection** activity.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Building Inspector"

Imagine you are constructing a 20-storey apartment building. Before handing over the keys to residents, you hire a **building inspector** whose job is *not* to praise the construction, but to **find every crack, loose wire, and leaky pipe**. The inspector does not "prove the building is perfect" — they actively hunt for problems because the cost of a single undetected fault (e.g., a faulty elevator brake) can be catastrophic.

Software Testing plays exactly the same role in software engineering:

| Building Construction | Software Development |
|---|---|
| Architect's blueprints | SRS (Software Requirements Specification) |
| Construction phase | Coding / Implementation |
| Building Inspector | Software Tester |
| Cracks / leaks | Bugs / Defects |
| Occupancy certificate | Release / Deployment |

## 1.3 Software Quality — KTU Concept Mapping

**Software Quality** is the degree to which a software product satisfies stated and implied needs (functional and non-functional), conforms to standards, and exhibits the characteristics that make it valuable for its intended use.

The two principal pillars recognized by the **ISO/IEC 25010:2011** standard (often referenced in KTU modules) are:

1. **Functional Suitability** — Does the software do what it is supposed to do?
2. **Non-Functional Quality Attributes** — How well does it do it? (Performance, security, reliability, usability, maintainability)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Quality is a *design and development goal* — Testing is a *measurement and verification activity* that tells us how close we got to that goal. Testing does NOT build quality into the product; it **measures** the quality that is already there.

## 1.4 Why Software Testing is Important (KTU Board Perspective)

The importance of testing is best understood in terms of **risk mitigation, cost reduction, and reliability assurance**:

- **Cost of Defect Escalation:** A defect found during the requirements phase may cost **1×** to fix; the same defect found after deployment may cost **100× to 1000×** more.
- **Customer Confidence & Trust:** Tested software builds brand reputation.
- **Safety & Human Life:** In medical, aviation, and nuclear domains, untested software can cost lives.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Standards like DO-178C (aviation), IEC 62304 (medical devices) mandate formal testing.
- **Economic Loss Avoidance:** Real-world failures have caused millions of dollars in damages (Ariane 5, Pentium FDIV bug).

> [!NOTE]
> The famous **Boehm Curve** (also called the "1:10:100 Rule") graphically demonstrates this exponential cost escalation across the software lifecycle — a high-yield concept for KTU 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Core Objectives of Software Testing

Software testing, as a discipline, pursues seven principal objectives aligned with the **ISTQB Foundation Level Syllabus** (the de facto KTU reference):

1. **Defect Detection** — Identify discrepancies between actual and expected behavior.
2. **Confidence Building** — Provide empirical evidence that the system meets requirements.
3. **Risk Mitigation** — Reduce the probability of in-service failures.
4. **Information Provision** — Generate diagnostic data for decision-making.
5. **Prevention of Future Defects** — Through root-cause analysis of found defects.
6. **Compliance Verification** — Confirm adherence to contractual/legal standards.
7. **Process Improvement Feedback** — Testing findings improve the development process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Students often write only the first two objectives. To score full marks, always enumerate **at least 5-7 objectives** with one-line explanations for each.

## 2.2 Verification vs. Validation — The V&V Framework

This is one of the **most frequently asked 3-mark and 14-mark questions** in KTU PECST631. You must know this distinction cold.

| Dimension | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Are we building the product **right**? | Are we building the **right** product? |
| **Question Answered** | Does the software conform to its specifications? | Does the software meet the user's real needs? |
| **Phase** | Throughout development (static) | Post-build, against real usage (dynamic) |
| **Method** | Reviews, walkthroughs, inspections, static analysis | Executing the software with test cases |
| **Who Performs** | Developers, QA team | Testers, end users |
| **Inputs Checked** | SRS, Design docs, Code | Actual executed behavior vs. user expectations |
| **Example Activity** | Code review for syntax/logic errors | Running a login page to verify it accepts valid credentials |

A classic mnemonic often cited in KTU coaching materials:

> **V&V — V=V (Verification = V-specification match; Validation = V-actual user need)**

## 2.3 Quality Assurance (QA) vs. Quality Control (QC)

Another recurring KTU question pair:

- **Quality Assurance (QA):** A *process-oriented*, preventive, managerial activity. It focuses on the **processes** used to create the software. Example: Adopting CMMI, defining coding standards, training developers.
- **Quality Control (QC):** A *product-oriented*, detective activity. It focuses on **inspecting the product** to find defects. Example: Running test cases, performing regression tests.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory aid:** *QA builds the bridge correctly (process); QC checks whether the bridge can hold traffic (product).*

## 2.4 The Testing Mindset — Myers' Negative Definition

The Myers formulation has three corollary principles that examiners test:

1. A successful test case is one that **uncovers an as-yet-undiscovered defect**.
2. Tests should be designed by someone **other than the developer** (independence principle).
3. Testing can **show the presence of defects but never their absence** — known as the **Dijkstra Principle** (1972).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Key Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Definition | Example / Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Defect (Bug)** | Any deviation from specified requirements | Wrong calculation result |
| **Error** | Human action that produces an incorrect result | Typing `>` instead of `<` |
| **Failure** | Manifestation of a defect during execution | Program crashes at runtime |
| **Defect Density** | Defects per unit size (e.g., per KLOC) | 0.5 defects/KLOC (industry average) |
| **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | Average time between system failures | $\frac{\text{Total operating time}}{\text{Number of failures}}$ |
| **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** | Average time to fix a defect | $\frac{\text{Total repair time}}{\text{Number of repairs}}$ |
| **Test Coverage** | Percentage of requirements exercised | $\frac{\text{Requirements tested}}{\text{Total requirements}} \times 100\%$ |
| **Reliability** | Probability of failure-free operation over a time period | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ |
| **Availability** | Uptime proportion | $A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Software Failures — KTU Case Study Bank

### Case Study 1: **Ariane 5 Flight 501 (June 4, 1996)** 🚀

- **What happened:** The maiden flight of the European Space Agency's Ariane 5 rocket exploded **37 seconds** after launch, destroying a payload worth **$370 million**.
- **Root cause:** An **unhandled data conversion exception** in the inertial reference system. A 64-bit floating-point value representing horizontal velocity was converted to a 16-bit signed integer, causing a numeric overflow.
- **Testing lapse:** The Ariane 4 software had been **reused without adequate re-testing** for the new Ariane 5 flight envelope. The overflow condition was not reachable in Ariane 4, so it was never exercised — **the test cases were inherited, not redesigned**.
- **Lesson (KTU Key Takeaway):** **Reusing software without re-validation is dangerous** — different operational environments trigger different code paths.

### Case Study 2: **Therac-25 (1985–1987)** ☢️

- **What happened:** A computer-controlled radiation therapy machine delivered **massive radiation overdoses** (up to 100× the prescribed dose) to at least **six patients**, causing deaths and serious injuries.
- **Root cause:** A **race condition** between operator keystrokes and the machine's hardware interlocks. The software removed hardware safety mechanisms that earlier models (Therac-6, Therac-20) had relied upon.
- **Testing lapse:** Developers **assumed** hardware interlocks would catch software errors; **integration testing between software and hardware was inadequate**; **no formal hazard analysis** was performed.
- **Lesson (KTU Key Takeaway):** **Software-only safety mechanisms require exhaustive testing**; over-confidence in hardware fail-safes is a classic testing anti-pattern.

### Case Study 3: **Patriot Missile Failure (1991)** 🛡️

- **What happened:** A U.S. Patriot missile failed to intercept an Iraqi Scud missile that killed 28 soldiers in Dhahran, Saudi Arabia.
- **Root cause:** A **floating-point precision error** in the system clock — after 100 hours of continuous operation, the accumulated time error exceeded 0.34 seconds, enough to misplace the interceptor by ~500 meters.
- **Testing lapse:** The system was tested only for short operational durations.
- **Lesson:** **Long-duration / endurance testing** is essential for real-time systems.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of Testing (Beyond Bug-Finding)

In modern DevOps/CI-CD pipelines, testing is integrated into the **build pipeline** and serves as the **quality gate** for production deployment. The **Test Pyramid** (popularized by Mike Cohn) classifies tests by scope and speed:

- **Unit Tests** (bottom, fastest, most numerous) — Test individual functions.
- **Integration Tests** (middle) — Test interaction between components.
- **System / End-to-End Tests** (top, slowest, fewest) — Test complete workflows.

> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Automated regression test suites in companies like Google run **millions of test cases per day**, catching defects within minutes of code commit.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cost-of-Defect Escalation Formula

The **Boehm Curve** expresses the relative cost of fixing a defect based on the phase in which it is introduced vs. the phase in which it is detected.

Let:

- $C_d$ = Cost of fixing a defect during the **development phase** (baseline).
- $C_i$ = Cost of fixing the same defect after integration.
- $C_t$ = Cost of fixing the defect during testing.
- $C_m$ = Cost of fixing the defect after market release (maintenance).

The empirical ratios (per Boehm, 1981) are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Relative Cost} &= \frac{\text{Cost at phase } x}{\text{Cost at requirements phase}} \\[6pt]
\text{Requirements} &\rightarrow 1\times \\[4pt]
\text{Design} &\rightarrow 5\times \\[4pt]
\text{Implementation} &\rightarrow 10\times \\[4pt]
\text{Integration Testing} &\rightarrow 20\times \\[4pt]
\text{System Testing} &\rightarrow 50\times \\[4pt]
\text{Post-Release Maintenance} &\rightarrow 200\times \text{ to } 1000\times
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A defect introduced during the requirements phase is detected 8 months after product release. If fixing it during requirements costs **₹10,000**, estimate the cost of fixing it post-release.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the multiplier from the Boehm curve. The defect was *introduced* at requirements (1× baseline) but *detected* at post-release (200×–1000×).

**Step 2:** Apply the worst-case multiplier consistent with the lifecycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{post-release}} &= C_{\text{baseline}} \times 500 \quad (\text{mid-range estimate}) \\[6pt]
C_{\text{post-release}} &= 10{,}000 \times 500 \\[6pt]
C_{\text{post-release}} &= \text{₹ } 50{,}00{,}000 \quad (\text{i.e., ₹ 50 lakhs})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation — A defect costing ₹10,000 to fix at requirements cost 500× more (₹50 lakhs) to fix post-release. This proves the **economic value of early testing**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Failures to Testing Principles

| Failure Case | Defect Category | Testing Principle Violated | Corrective Test Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ariane 5** | Numeric overflow (data conversion) | Inadequate domain / boundary value testing of reused code | Boundary Value Analysis on data conversion routines |
| **Therac-25** | Race condition (concurrency) | Insufficient integration and stress testing | Concurrency / Thread-safety testing; Hazard analysis |
| **Patriot Missile** | Floating-point precision drift over time | Missing endurance / soak testing | Long-duration execution testing; clock drift validation |
| **Pentium FDIV (1994)** | Incorrect division lookup table | Inadequate low-level functional testing | Combinatorial / exhaustive functional testing |

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Defect Density & Reliability Metrics

### Defect Density

$$
DD = \frac{\text{Number of Defects Found}}{\text{Size in KLOC or Function Points}}
$$

where **KLOC** = Thousand Lines of Code.

**Example:** A 50 KLOC module contains 35 known defects. Calculate the defect density.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DD &= \frac{35 \text{ defects}}{50 \text{ KLOC}} \\[6pt]
DD &= 0.7 \text{ defects per KLOC}
\end{aligned}
$$

Industry benchmark (per Capers Jones): **High-quality software ≈ 0.5–1.0 defects/KLOC**.

### Availability Formula (Key Reliability Equation)

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Example:** A web server has an MTTF of 720 hours and MTTR of 8 hours.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{720}{720 + 8} \\[6pt]
A &= \frac{720}{728} \\[6pt]
A &\approx 0.9890 \quad \text{or} \quad 98.90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This means the server is available **98.90% of the time** — equivalent to a maximum downtime of:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Downtime per year} &= (1 - A) \times 8760 \text{ hours} \\[6pt]
&= (1 - 0.9890) \times 8760 \\[6pt]
&= 0.0110 \times 8760 \\[6pt]
&= 96.36 \text{ hours/year} \quad (\approx 4 \text{ days})
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Coverage Metric Derivation

$$
\text{Coverage} = \frac{\text{Items Tested}}{\text{Total Items}} \times 100\%
$$

Three common coverage types (frequently asked in KTU):

1. **Requirement Coverage:** Fraction of requirements covered by at least one test case.
2. **Code Coverage:** Fraction of source code lines/branches executed by tests.
3. **Test Case Execution Coverage:** Fraction of planned test cases actually executed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The V-Model of Software Development & Testing

This is the **standard KTU diagram** for showing how testing phases map to development phases.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRe["User Requirements"]
    nodeAc["Acceptance Test Plan"]
    nodeSr["System Requirements"]
    nodeSt["System Test Plan"]
    nodeDe["Detailed Design"]
    nodeIt["Integration Test Plan"]
    nodeMo["Module Design"]
    nodeUt["Unit Test Plan"]
    nodeCode["Coding"]
    nodeCodeAct["Module Code"]

    nodeRe --> nodeAc
    nodeRe --> nodeSr
    nodeSr --> nodeSt
    nodeSr --> nodeDe
    nodeDe --> nodeIt
    nodeDe --> nodeMo
    nodeMo --> nodeUt
    nodeMo --> nodeCode
    nodeCode --> nodeCodeAct

    nodeCodeAct --> nodeUt
    nodeUt --> nodeIt
    nodeIt --> nodeSt
    nodeSt --> nodeAc
```

**Reading the V-Model:**
- **Left arm (descending):** Decomposition of requirements → design → code.
- **Right arm (ascending):** Corresponding test phases — unit → integration → system → acceptance.
- The horizontal connections define the **verification & validation** boundary for each phase.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Verification vs. Validation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Software Requirements Specification"]
    nodeB["Design Documents"]
    nodeC["Source Code"]
    nodeD["Running Executable"]

    subgraph Verif["VERIFICATION - Are we building the product RIGHT"]
        v1["Reviews"]
        v2["Walkthroughs"]
        v3["Inspections"]
        v4["Static Analysis"]
    end

    subgraph Valid["VALIDATION - Are we building the RIGHT product"]
        val1["Unit Testing"]
        val2["Integration Testing"]
        val3["System Testing"]
        val4["User Acceptance Testing"]
    end

    nodeA --> v1
    nodeB --> v2
    nodeC --> v3
    nodeC --> v4
    nodeD --> val1
    nodeD --> val2
    nodeD --> val3
    nodeD --> val4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Causes of Software Failures (Taxonomy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeCause["Root Cause of Software Failures"]
    nodeReuse["Inadequate Reuse Testing - Example Ariane 5"]
    nodeRace["Race Conditions - Example Therac 25"]
    nodeEndure["Missing Endurance Testing - Example Patriot"]
    nodeBoundary["Missing Boundary Value Analysis"]
    nodeInter["Insufficient Integration Testing"]
    nodeSpec["Incomplete Specification"]

    nodeCause --> nodeReuse
    nodeCause --> nodeRace
    nodeCause --> nodeEndure
    nodeCause --> nodeBoundary
    nodeCause --> nodeInter
    nodeCause --> nodeSpec

    nodeReuse --> outcome["Catastrophic Failure / Loss of Life / Revenue Loss"]
    nodeRace --> outcome
    nodeEndure --> outcome
    nodeBoundary --> outcome
    nodeInter --> outcome
    nodeSpec --> outcome
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — The Test Pyramid (Layered Testing Strategy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeE2E["E2E / UI Tests - Slow, Few, Brittle"]
    nodeSys["System / Integration Tests - Medium Speed"]
    nodeUnit["Unit Tests - Fast, Many, Stable"]

    nodeE2E --> nodeSys
    nodeSys --> nodeUnit

    style nodeE2E fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000
    style nodeSys fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style nodeUnit fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Software Testing. Why is testing considered a destructive process?** *(Module 1 – KTU Model Paper Pattern)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Software testing is the process of executing a program or system with the intent of finding defects (Myers, 1979). It is an empirical investigation to provide stakeholders with information about the quality of the software product.

**Why destructive:** Testing is termed **destructive** because the tester's goal is to **break the software** — i.e., to demonstrate that the program does *not* work, rather than to prove it works. A successful test is one that uncovers a previously unknown defect. The tester adopts a deliberately skeptical mindset, designing test cases that explore boundary, abnormal, and malicious inputs to expose weaknesses, unlike the constructive mindset of the developer.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC).** *(Module 1 – High Frequency)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Control (QC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Process-oriented — focuses on preventing defects by improving the development process | Product-oriented — focuses on detecting defects in the finished product |
| **Approach** | Preventive | Detective |
| **Activities** | Defining standards, training developers, process audits, CMMI adoption, code reviews | Running test cases, executing test plans, defect logging, regression testing |
| **Performed by** | Entire development team, management, SQA group | Dedicated test team, QA engineers |
| **Timing** | Throughout the entire project lifecycle | Primarily after product build, during testing phase |
| **Example** | Adopting ISO 9001 process standards | Performing a smoke test on a built module |

QA aims to build quality *into* the product; QC aims to verify that the built product meets quality standards.

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** between two full questions of 14 marks each. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** — answer **either** one.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): "Importance of Software Testing & Quality"**

#### **(a) Discuss the importance of software testing in modern software engineering. Explain the Verification vs. Validation (V&V) framework with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Importance of Software Testing (5 key points):**

1. **Risk Mitigation:** Software failures in safety-critical systems (avionics, medical, nuclear) can cause loss of human life. Testing identifies risks before deployment.
2. **Cost Reduction:** The Boehm Curve demonstrates that fixing defects early in the lifecycle costs 10× to 1000× less than post-release fixes.
3. **Customer Confidence & Quality Assurance:** Tested software builds user trust, improves brand reputation, and reduces post-release support costs.
4. **Regulatory & Contractual Compliance:** Standards like DO-178C (aviation), IEC 62304 (medical devices), and ISO 26262 (automotive) mandate formal testing for certification.
5. **Process Improvement:** Defect data from testing provides empirical feedback for refining the development process — driving continuous improvement.

**Verification vs. Validation (with examples):**

- **Verification** answers: *"Are we building the product right?"* It ensures the software conforms to its specifications, design documents, and standards. Verification is a **static activity** performed through reviews, inspections, and walkthroughs. *Example:* Verifying that the coded login function matches the pseudocode in the design document; conducting a code review to ensure coding standards are followed.

- **Validation** answers: *"Are we building the right product?"* It ensures the software meets the user's actual business needs under realistic conditions. Validation is a **dynamic activity** performed by executing the software. *Example:* Logging into the application as an end user to confirm that the login experience matches user expectations and that valid credentials are accepted.

Both are essential — software can be verified against specs (correctly built) but still fail validation (the wrong product was built).

#### **(b) Explain the Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC) concepts. Also explain the concept of software quality using the ISO/IEC 25010 standard. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**QA and QC:** *(Cover the distinction table given in Section 2.3 above — elaborate on the process-oriented nature of QA vs. the product-oriented nature of QC, with one engineering example each.)*

**Software Quality per ISO/IEC 25010:**

ISO/IEC 25010 defines software quality through **eight characteristics**:

1. **Functional Suitability** — completeness, correctness, appropriateness.
2. **Performance Efficiency** — time behavior, resource utilization, capacity.
3. **Compatibility** — co-existence with other systems.
4. **Usability** — learnability, operability, error protection.
5. **Reliability** — maturity, availability, fault tolerance, recoverability.
6. **Security** — confidentiality, integrity, non-repudiation, accountability.
7. **Maintainability** — modularity, reusability, analyzability, modifiability, testability.
8. **Portability** — adaptability, installability, replaceability.

Software testing directly measures and validates these characteristics — particularly reliability, performance efficiency, and security — through specialized test types (reliability testing, load testing, security testing).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): "Real-World Software Failures"**

#### **(a) Analyze the Ariane 5 Flight 501 failure. What testing lapses contributed to the disaster? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Background:** On **June 4, 1996**, the European Space Agency's Ariane 5 rocket, costing nearly **$370 million**, exploded **37 seconds after launch** over Kourou, French Guiana.

**Technical Root Cause:** A 64-bit floating-point value representing the **horizontal velocity** (BH) was being converted to a 16-bit signed integer in the Inertial Reference System. For Ariane 5, the BH value was significantly larger than for Ariane 4, causing an **arithmetic overflow**. The exception handler was inadequate — it shut down the SRI (Inertial Reference System), and the backup unit failed identically (since both ran the same software), causing catastrophic loss of guidance.

**Testing Lapses:**

1. **Untested Code Path:** The data conversion routine that overflowed on Ariane 5 had been **inherited from Ariane 4**, where the BH value never exceeded the 16-bit integer range. The code was never re-validated for the new flight envelope.
2. **Inadequate Range / Boundary Value Testing:** No test cases exercised the maximum possible value of the BH variable for Ariane 5's flight profile.
3. **Inadequate Integration Testing:** The exception handler logic was not tested under realistic conditions.
4. **Single Point of Failure:** Both the primary and backup SRI units used identical software — a design defect that should have been flagged by FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis).

**Lessons:** Reused software must undergo **full re-validation** when deployed in a new environment; range testing must be exhaustive; defensive programming and exception handling must be rigorously designed.

#### **(b) Analyze the Therac-25 radiation therapy machine failure. How could proper testing have prevented the accidents? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Background:** Between **1985 and 1987**, the Therac-25, a computer-controlled radiation therapy machine manufactured by Atomic Energy of Canada Limited (AECL), delivered **massive radiation overdoses** (up to 100× the prescribed dose) to at least **six patients**, causing deaths and severe injuries.

**Technical Root Cause:** A **race condition** between operator keystrokes (entering treatment parameters via the VT100 terminal) and the machine's hardware interlocks. Specifically, when an operator edited a treatment parameter very quickly (using the up-arrow key), the software set the magnetron to "high-power mode" without the **turntable position** being verified by the hardware interlock — leading to a "field-light" mode error that bypassed safety interlocks.

**Testing Lapses:**

1. **Inadequate Concurrency / Race Condition Testing:** No stress tests were performed under realistic operator-typing speeds.
2. **Removal of Hardware Safety Nets:** Earlier Therac models (Therac-6, Therac-20) had **hardware interlocks** as a secondary safety layer; Therac-25 relied **solely on software** for safety — a massive single-point-of-failure.
3. **Inadequate Hazard Analysis:** No formal **FMEA** or **fault tree analysis** was performed before deployment.
4. **Ignoring User-Reported Incidents:** Multiple near-miss and incident reports were dismissed by AECL as "impossible" — a failure of **defect-triage discipline**.
5. **Inadequate Code Review:** The race condition could have been detected through systematic code reviews and static analysis.

**Lessons:** Software controlling safety-critical systems must undergo **hazard analysis, exhaustive concurrency testing, and independent safety audits**. Hardware fail-safes should never be removed without overwhelming evidence of software correctness. User-reported anomalies must be treated with utmost seriousness.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in PECST631 Module 1:**
>
> 1. **Defining testing as "proving the program works"** — this is the **wrong definition** per Myers. Always frame testing as a **defect-finding** activity. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for the wrong definition.
> 2. **Confusing V&V:** Students frequently interchange verification and validation. Memorize the mnemonic: **V-specification = Verification; V-user-need = Validation.**
> 3. **Writing only 2-3 objectives of testing** when 5-7 are expected — deduct up to **2 marks** for brevity in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 4. **Omitting real-world case studies:** For 14-mark questions on "importance of testing," always cite **at least one case study (Ariane 5, Therac-25, or Patriot)** with specific failure data. Examiners award 2-3 marks explicitly for case-study citations.
> 5. **Mixing up QA and QC:** QA = process; QC = product. Do not write vague, overlapping answers.
> 6. **Skipping formulas:** When asked about quality metrics, **write the formula explicitly** (DD, MTTF, Availability, Coverage). Marks are reserved for formula recall.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🔹 **Definition (Myers):** Testing = executing software with intent to **find defects** (not to prove it works).
- 🔹 **Testing ≠ Debugging:** Testing finds defects; debugging removes them.
- 🔹 **V&V Mnemonic:** **Verification** = Right product? No — **specification match** (static). **Validation** = Right product? Yes — **user need match** (dynamic).
- 🔹 **QA vs QC:** QA = **Process** (preventive, CMMI, training). QC = **Product** (detective, test execution).
- 🔹 **Myers' Three Principles:** Test success = finding a new defect; independence matters; testing shows presence, not absence of defects.
- 🔹 **Boehm Curve Multipliers:** Requirements 1× → Design 5× → Code 10× → Integration 20× → System 50× → Post-release **200×–1000×**.
- 🔹 **Ariane 5:** 64→16-bit conversion overflow; reused Ariane-4 code without re-validation.
- 🔹 **Therac-25:** Race condition; software-only safety mechanisms without hardware interlocks.
- 🔹 **Patriot Missile:** Clock drift after 100+ hours of continuous operation.
- 🔹 **Defect Density formula:** $DD = \frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ — Industry benchmark **0.5–1.0 defects/KLOC**.
- 🔹 **Availability:** $A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- 🔹 **ISO/IEC 25010:** 8 quality characteristics — Functional, Performance, Compatibility, Usability, Reliability, Security, Maintainability, Portability.
- 🔹 **Test Pyramid:** Unit (base, most) → Integration (middle) → System/E2E (top, fewest).
- 🔹 **Real-world utility:** Testing is the **quality gate** in CI/CD; automated regression suites catch defects within minutes of code commit.

---

> **End of PECST631 — Module 1 Notes | Introduction to Software Testing**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern with NEP 2020 alignment.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Testing Processes - Levels of thinking in testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software Testing Processes - Levels of thinking in testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Levels of thinking in testing** is a conceptual framework, popularized by **James Bach** and **Cem Kaner** within the context of the **Context-Driven School of Software Testing**, that classifies the **mental perspectives (heuristics)** a tester consciously adopts while designing and executing tests. Each "level" represents a deeper layer of cognitive inquiry — moving from the surface question *"What should I test?"* to deeper questions like *"What could go wrong?"*, *"How will this evolve?"* and *"What does the user truly want?"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** Under the **PECST631 - Software Testing** course (Module 1: *Software Testing Processes*), this concept is grouped with the **General Testing Principles**, the **Tester’s Mission**, and the **Phases of a Tester’s Work**. It explicitly contrasts with the older, mechanical view of testing as mere *specification verification*.

### 1.2 Terminological Distinction (Syllabus Alert)

> [!NOTE]
> Do **not** confuse *"Levels of thinking in testing"* with *"Levels of testing"* (Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance). The latter is a structural decomposition of the **test object**, while the former is a cognitive decomposition of the **tester’s mind**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)

Imagine you are a **food critic** reviewing a new restaurant, not just tasting the food:

| Critic's Mental Layer | Tester's Cognitive Layer |
|---|---|
| Tries every dish on the menu | **Level 1 — Find Values**: Enumerate inputs/outputs |
| Checks whether the dish matches the menu description | **Level 2 — Specification**: Compare behavior to requirements |
| Observes whether diners are happy with the meal | **Level 3 — User**: Evaluate from a stakeholder's view |
| Tries to break the dish (over-spices it, requests raw) | **Level 4 — Failure Modes**: Fault attacks & error guessing |
| Thinks about repeat visits and recipe changes | **Level 5 — Future**: Maintainability and longevity |
| Considers whether the restaurant honors its brand promise | **Level 6 — Reputation**: Trust, compliance, brand |
| Compares it to rival restaurants across the street | **Level 7 — Marketing**: Competitive differentiation |

Just as a skilled critic layers these perspectives, a mature tester blends **multiple levels of thinking** to expose hidden risks.

### 1.4 The Tester’s Mission (Context)

According to Cem Kaner, the **mission of a tester** is to discover important information about the product and its users, and to help the development team use that information to make the product better. The **seven levels of thinking** operationalize this mission into a structured set of cognitive tools.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Levels — Detailed Breakdown

#### 🔹 Level 1 — Find Values (Vocabulary of the Product)

**Core "Why":** Before testing, you must **identify *what* can be tested**. Without a vocabulary of inputs, outputs, states, and variables, no test can be designed.

**How to apply:**
- Enumerate parameters, fields, options, file formats.
- Build a **classification tree** of inputs (Kimball/equivalent partition).
- Ask: *"What is this thing made of?"*

#### 🔹 Level 2 — Specification-Based Thinking

**Core "Why":** The software was **built against a specification**. Any deviation is a potential defect.

**How to apply:**
- Derive tests directly from requirements (requirements-based testing).
- Use traceability matrices (RTM).
- Apply equivalence partitioning + boundary value analysis.
- Apply decision tables and state-transition testing.

#### 🔹 Level 3 — User-Centric Thinking

**Core "Why":** A product may meet its specification yet still **fail its users** (usability, relevance, missing features).

**How to apply:**
- Use personas, user stories, scenarios.
- Conduct exploratory testing sessions.
- Capture real user journeys; study accessibility (WCAG), i18n, and UX heuristics.

#### 🔹 Level 4 — Failure-Mode Thinking

**Core "Why":** Defects concentrate around **known weak spots** — interfaces, error handlers, concurrency, and unusual input.

**How to apply:**
- **Error guessing** (using defect taxonomies and historical bug patterns).
- **Fault attacks** like penetration testing, fuzz testing, and mutation testing.
- Use HAYSTACK, OWASP Top 10, and STRIDE threat models.

#### 🔹 Level 5 — Future-Oriented Thinking

**Core "Why":** A product shipped today will be **modified, scaled, and stressed** tomorrow.

**How to apply:**
- Performance/load/stress testing.
- Reliability testing (MTTF, MTBF).
- Maintainability (ISO/IEC 25010) and refactor-safety (regression).
- Backward compatibility testing.

#### 🔹 Level 6 — Reputation-Focused Thinking

**Core "Why":** The product’s image, brand promise, and **legal/regulatory obligations** carry weight beyond functional correctness.

**How to apply:**
- Compliance testing (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS).
- Brand-voice testing, accessibility audits.
- Privacy and security testing (ethical hacking, threat modeling).

#### 🔹 Level 7 — Marketing & Competitive Thinking

**Core "Why":** The product exists in a **competitive ecosystem**; its perceived value depends on parity or superiority over alternatives.

**How to apply:**
- Comparative/competitive testing.
- A/B testing for market differentiation.
- Pricing model correctness, feature-gap analysis.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| $\#$ | Level (Cognitive Layer) | Guiding Question | Primary Test Technique | Example Output Artifact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Values / Vocabulary** | *"What inputs/outputs exist?"* | Input classification, ECP | Classification tree |
| 2 | **Specification** | *"What did the spec say?"* | BVA, decision tables | RTM, test cases |
| 3 | **User** | *"What does the user want?"* | Personas, exploratory sessions | User journey report |
| 4 | **Failure Modes** | *"How can this break?"* | Fuzzing, error guessing | Fault taxonomy |
| 5 | **Future** | *"How will this age?"* | Load/regression testing | Reliability curve |
| 6 | **Reputation** | *"Does it honor our brand?"* | Compliance, privacy audit | Compliance matrix |
| 7 | **Marketing** | *"Are we better than rivals?"* | Comparative, A/B testing | Competitive benchmark |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forgetting to label which level a test belongs to** is a common reason testers miss coverage. Always **map each test case to at least one level** of thinking.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

This layered model is the foundation of **exploratory testing charters**, **risk-based testing strategies**, and **test maturity models (TMMi, TPI Next)**. In Agile/DevOps teams, the seven levels are baked into the *“whole-team approach to quality”* — testers who operate at multiple cognitive levels produce richer bug reports and stronger risk insights than testers who operate only at the *specification* level.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — From Tester Mission to Test Coverage

We start from Cem Kaner’s formal statement of the **Tester’s Mission** and show how the seven levels are derived from it.

**Step 1 — State the Mission Axiom.**
The tester’s mission is to find **important information** about the product and its users to help stakeholders make informed decisions.

**Step 2 — Decompose "Important Information" into Cognitive Layers.**

Important information can be grouped into three meta-buckets:

$$
\text{Important Information} \;=\; \underbrace{I_{\text{Intended}}}_{\text{Specification}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{I_{\text{Actual}}}_{\text{Failure Modes \& Values}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{I_{\text{Contextual}}}_{\text{User, Future, Reputation, Marketing}}
$$

**Step 3 — Operationalize the *Intended* bucket** → **Levels 1 & 2**.

Level 1 supplies the vocabulary (inputs, outputs, states), and Level 2 evaluates whether the actual behavior matches the documented specification.

$$
\text{Conformance} = \left\{ \text{Behavior} \; \big\vert \; \forall t \in T : \text{actual}(t) = \text{specified}(t) \right\}
$$

Here, $T$ is the universe of test cases derived from the specification.

**Step 4 — Operationalize the *Actual* bucket** → **Levels 1 & 4**.

Level 4 expands the search beyond the happy path into **negative scenarios, error conditions, and adversarial inputs**.

$$
\text{Defect Density} = \frac{\sum_{l=4}^{\text{Level 4 tests}} \text{failures}}{\sum_{l=4}^{\text{Level 4 tests executed}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Operationalize the *Contextual* bucket** → **Levels 3, 5, 6, 7**.

These layers reframe the product inside its ecosystem: users (3), lifecycle (5), brand (6), and competition (7).

$$
\text{Coverage}_{\text{contextual}} = \frac{\sum_{l \in \{3,5,6,7\}} \text{covered perspectives}}{\text{Total identified perspectives}}
$$

**Step 6 — Compose the Global Coverage Index.**

$$
C_{\text{global}} = \frac{\sum_{l=1}^{7} \text{tests executed at level } l}{\sum_{l=1}^{7} \text{tests planned at level } l}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the Index.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{global}} &\ge 0.90 \;\;\Rightarrow\; \text{Robust coverage across all 7 levels} \\
0.70 \le C_{\text{global}} &< 0.90 \;\;\Rightarrow\; \text{Acceptable, but inspect uncovered layers} \\
C_{\text{global}} &< 0.70 \;\;\Rightarrow\; \text{Critical: hidden risks in unexplored layers}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The thresholds above are **engineering heuristics**, not KTU-mandated values. Examiners expect you to **justify** any number you write.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Apply Each Level

| Scenario in KTU Board Exam Questions | Recommended Level | Justification (Model Answer Phrasing) |
|---|---|---|
| *"Validate that the login screen accepts alphanumeric usernames as per SRS."* | **Level 2 — Specification** | Conformance to documented requirement |
| *"What could a hacker do to bypass the OTP?"* | **Level 4 — Failure Modes** | Fault attack, security threat modeling |
| *"Will the system survive 10,000 concurrent users?"* | **Level 5 — Future** | Performance and scalability assurance |
| *"Does our app feel right for a visually impaired user?"* | **Levels 3 & 6 — User + Reputation** | Accessibility is both user need and brand promise |
| *"How does our checkout compare with Amazon?"* | **Level 7 — Marketing** | Competitive differentiation |

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (Board-Style)

**Question Fragment:** *A bank releases a new mobile-banking app. Discuss how a tester applies three different levels of thinking while reviewing it.*

**Solution Sketch (7-step reasoning):**

1. **Level 2 (Specification):** Verify daily transfer limit = ₹1,00,000 as per RBI mandate.
2. **Level 4 (Failure Modes):** Inject malformed JSON into the transaction API → test input validation.
3. **Level 3 (User):** Walk through the app with a 65-year-old first-time user → identify UX friction.

Each level surfaces **different defects** that the others miss — illustrating why mastery of all seven levels is non-negotiable for a competent tester.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Seven Layers of Tester Cognition

```mermaid
graph TD
    L7["Level 7 Marketing Competitive"] --> L6["Level 6 Reputation Compliance Brand"]
    L6 --> L5["Level 5 Future Scalability Maintainability"]
    L5 --> L4["Level 4 Failure Modes Fuzzing Error Guessing"]
    L4 --> L3["Level 3 User Personas Exploratory UX"]
    L3 --> L2["Level 2 Specification Conformance RTM"]
    L2 --> L1["Level 1 Values Vocabulary Inputs Outputs"]
    L1 --> Mission["Tester Mission Find Important Information"]

    L1 -.feeds.-> L2
    L2 -.feeds.-> L3
    L3 -.feeds.-> L4
    L4 -.feeds.-> L5
    L5 -.feeds.-> L6
    L6 -.feeds.-> L7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid downward arrows represent the *deepening cognitive focus* (from observable facts → competitive context). Dotted arrows represent the *forward information flow* — each level enriches the next with discovered evidence.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow: "Which Level Should I Apply Now?"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["New test session starts"] --> Q1{"Is there a formal requirement?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Lvl2["Apply Level 2 Specification thinking"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Are you exploring freely?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Lvl1["Apply Level 1 Values thinking"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Is the user persona known?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Lvl3["Apply Level 3 User thinking"]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Are you probing for weaknesses?"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Lvl4["Apply Level 4 Failure Modes"]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{"Are you testing lifespan or scale?"}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Lvl5["Apply Level 5 Future thinking"]
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{"Is brand or legal compliance in scope?"}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Lvl6["Apply Level 6 Reputation thinking"]
    Q6 -- No --> Lvl7["Apply Level 7 Marketing thinking"]
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

When testers cannot draw physical schematics of the system, the **seven levels** map neatly onto a layered reasoning engine as follows:

| Layer | Reasoning Engine Component | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | **Input Inventory Module** | Product docs, APIs | Vocabulary tree |
| L2 | **Spec-Match Comparator** | Vocabulary + SRS | Conformance report |
| L3 | **User Simulator Module** | Personas, scenarios | UX defect log |
| L4 | **Fault Injection Engine** | Vocabulary + threat model | Crash/failure catalog |
| L5 | **Lifecycle Simulator** | Workload model | Reliability curve |
| L6 | **Compliance Auditor** | Regulatory rule-set | Compliance flag list |
| L7 | **Benchmark Comparator** | Competitor binaries | Differentiation score |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **"Levels of testing"** and **"Levels of thinking in testing"**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Levels of testing* is a **structural decomposition** of the test object into Unit, Integration, System, and Acceptance levels (1 mark).
- *Levels of thinking in testing* is a **cognitive decomposition** of the tester’s mental perspective into seven layers — Values, Specification, User, Failure Modes, Future, Reputation, and Marketing (1 mark).
- The first answers *"What part of the product am I testing?"* while the second answers *"How am I thinking about what I am testing?"* (1 mark).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q2.** State the **mission of a software tester** as formulated by Cem Kaner.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The mission of a tester is to **find important information** about the product, its users, and the project context (1.5 marks).
- This information is then **communicated** to stakeholders to enable **informed, project-level decisions** about quality and risk (1.5 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### 🅰️ **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **seven levels of thinking in testing** as proposed by James Bach. For each level, give **one example** of a test technique and **one example defect** it could uncover. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A healthcare web application stores patient records. The product owner says *"We need to be HIPAA-compliant and must scale to 50 hospitals in two years."* Map **at least four levels of thinking** to this scenario, identifying the corresponding test technique for each level. **(7 marks)**

##### 📝 Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Seven Levels with Examples (7 marks — 1 mark per level):**

1. **Level 1 – Values:** Technique → Equivalence Partitioning; Example defect → Missed field on a registration form.
2. **Level 2 – Specification:** Technique → Boundary Value Analysis; Example defect → Off-by-one error in age validation.
3. **Level 3 – User:** Technique → Persona-based exploratory sessions; Example defect → Confusing error message for elderly users.
4. **Level 4 – Failure Modes:** Technique → Fuzz testing; Example defect → SQL injection vulnerability.
5. **Level 5 – Future:** Technique → Load testing; Example defect → Memory leak under sustained load.
6. **Level 6 – Reputation:** Technique → Compliance audit (HIPAA); Example defect → Unencrypted PHI transmission.
7. **Level 7 – Marketing:** Technique → Comparative A/B testing; Example defect → Slower checkout than competitor.

**(b) Mapping to Healthcare App Scenario (7 marks):**

| Concern Raised | Level | Test Technique (with justification) |
|---|---|---|
| HIPAA compliance | **Level 6 – Reputation** | Regulatory audit testing using HIPAA checklist (2 marks) |
| Scaling to 50 hospitals | **Level 5 – Future** | Load and stress testing simulating 50-hospital concurrent load (2 marks) |
| Patient record safety | **Level 4 – Failure Modes** | Penetration testing + SQL injection / fuzz testing (1.5 marks) |
| User experience for doctors/nurses | **Level 3 – User** | Persona-based usability testing with clinician personas (1.5 marks) |

---

#### 🅱️ **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Describe how the **levels of thinking** concept operationalizes the **tester’s mission**. Derive, with reasoning, a **global coverage index** that aggregates effort across all seven levels and explain how its value guides the test manager. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a release of an e-commerce platform, the test manager observes that 80 % of executed tests are specification-driven, while only 10 % address failure-mode thinking and 5 % address future-oriented thinking. Using the seven-levels framework, **diagnose the risk exposure** and propose a **rebalancing strategy**. **(7 marks)**

##### 📝 Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Mission Operationalization & Coverage Index Derivation (7 marks):**

1. Kaner’s mission axiom: testers must **discover important information** and **communicate** it (1 mark).
2. The seven levels partition the "important information" search space into **Intended** (L1, L2), **Actual** (L1, L4), and **Contextual** (L3, L5, L6, L7) buckets (1 mark).
3. For each level $l$, define executed tests $E_l$ and planned tests $P_l$ (1 mark).
4. Level coverage:

$$
C_l = \frac{E_l}{P_l}
$$

5. Global coverage index (equal weighting for clarity):

$$
C_{\text{global}} = \frac{1}{7} \sum_{l=1}^{7} C_l = \frac{1}{7} \sum_{l=1}^{7} \frac{E_l}{P_l}
$$

(2 marks for derivation)

6. **Interpretation by test manager:** values $\ge 0.90$ indicate balanced maturity; $< 0.70$ demand immediate re-planning (2 marks).

**(b) Risk Diagnosis & Rebalancing Strategy (7 marks):**

- **Diagnosis (3 marks):** Over-investment at Level 2 (80 %) leaves the product **blind to security flaws (L4)**, **scalability concerns (L5)**, and **user-experience gaps (L3)** — a textbook recipe for post-release disasters.
- **Rebalancing Strategy (4 marks):**
  - Shift 20 % of L2 effort → **L4 (failure modes)** using penetration + fuzz testing.
  - Allocate 10 % → **L5 (future)** via load and stress testing.
  - Allocate 10 % → **L3 (user)** via persona-driven exploratory testing.
  - Maintain L1, L6, L7 at the residual baseline.
  - Re-measure $C_{\text{global}}$ after each sprint; gate releases until $C_{\text{global}} \ge 0.85$.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "levels of testing" with "levels of thinking in testing"** — examiners allocate a full mark band for this distinction; mixing them up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Listing only 3 or 4 levels** instead of all seven — KTU rubrics award marks per level; partial coverage = partial marks.
> 3. **Writing generic definitions without application examples** — every level must be tied to a *technique* and a *defect type* for full credit.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation step** in coverage-index questions — showing the formula with explicit summation notation carries separate marks.
> 5. **Using absolute-value pipes $\vert x \vert$ in tables** — this breaks the markdown table layout in the answer sheet digitization pipeline. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

---

### 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **seven levels** are: *Values, Specification, User, Failure Modes, Future, Reputation, Marketing*.
- ✅ They are **cognitive layers**, not test stages — apply them concurrently, not sequentially.
- ✅ They are rooted in the **Context-Driven School** (Bach, Kaner) — *not* in ISTQB’s classical four-level test pyramid.
- ✅ Every test case should be tagged with **at least one level of thinking** for traceability.
- ✅ The **Tester’s Mission** (Kaner) anchors the entire framework: *find important information & communicate it*.
- ✅ A high $C_{\text{global}}$ (≥ 0.85) is a leading indicator of release readiness; a low one is a red flag.
- ✅ In KTU answers, **always pair each level with a technique + a defect example** to score full marks.
- ✅ Memorize the **Levels 1–7 ordering** — examiners occasionally ask them in sequence or reverse.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing Terminologies - Verification, validation, fault, error, bug, test cases, and coverage criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Testing Terminologies - Verification, validation, fault, error, bug, test cases, and coverage criteria # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview Welcome to the foundational module of Software Testing. Before we dive into test design techniques, automation frameworks, or any advanced tools, every KTU 2024 scheme student must master the **core vocabulary** of testing. This section provides the rigorous academic definitions mandated by the syllabus, paired with intuitive analogies to lock these concepts into long-term memory. --- ## 1.1 Verification vs. Validation (V&V) These two terms are often confused but are **fundamentally distinct** activities in the software development lifecycle. ### ✅ Verification > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification** is the process of evaluating a system or component to determine whether the **products of a given development phase satisfy the conditions imposed at the start of that phase**. In simple words: *"Are we building the product **right**?"* - **Focus:** Internal consistency, adherence to specifications, design, and standards. - **Activities:** Reviews, walkthroughs, inspections, desk-checking. - **Timing:** Performed **during development** (on artifacts like SRS, design documents, code). - **Approach:** **Static** in nature — does NOT require executing the code. ### ✅ Validation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Validation** is the process of evaluating a system or component during or at the end of the development process to determine whether it satisfies **specified requirements and user needs**. In simple words: *"Are we building the **right** product?"* - **Focus:** Real-world fitness for use, meeting actual user needs. - **Activities:** Black-box testing, functional testing, user acceptance testing (UAT). - **Timing:** Performed **after development** (on the running software). - **Approach:** **Dynamic** in nature — REQUIRES executing the code. ### 🏠 Conceptual Analogy (The Cooking Story) > [!NOTE] > **Verification is like following a recipe exactly** — measuring ingredients, preheating the oven to the right temperature, and using specified cookware. You are confirming the *process* is correct. > > **Validation is like tasting the final dish** — asking your guests whether the food actually satisfies their hunger and taste. You are confirming the *product* is fit for purpose. > > You can verify the recipe without ever tasting the dish. But validation is meaningless until the dish is actually cooked and served. --- ## 1.2 Error, Fault, Bug, and Failure (The Defect Cascade) This is the **most heavily tested concept** in KTU exams. Understanding the chain reaction from human mistake to user-visible failure is critical. ### 🐛 Definitions in Order | Term | Synonym | Formal Definition | Where it lives | |---|---|---|---| | **Error** | Mistake | A human action that produces an **incorrect result** | In the **mind** of the developer | | **Fault** | Defect / Bug | The **manifestation** of an error in the software artifact (code, doc) | In the **software** itself | | **Failure** | Crash / Incorrect Output | The **deviation** of the software from its expected deliverable behavior during execution | In the **running system** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Every KTU examiner expects this distinction:** Not all faults lead to failures (a fault may lie on an unexecuted code path). Not all failures are caused by faults (could be environmental, like a hardware crash). The chain is **Error → Fault → Failure**. ### 🏠 Conceptual Analogy (The Typo Story) > [!NOTE] > A journalist (human) **mistypes** "recieve" instead of "receive" while writing an article. > - The **Error** is the journalist's incorrect keystroke (mental/physical action). > - The **Fault** is the misspelled word stored in the article document (defect in artifact). > - The **Failure** occurs only when a reader spots the typo (defect is executed/triggered). If the article is deleted before publication, no failure occurs — but the fault still existed. > - A **Bug** is simply another name for a **Fault** (used colloquially by developers since Grace Hopper's famous 1947 moth incident). --- ## 1.3 Test Case > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Test Case** is a set of **preconditions, inputs, actions, expected results, and postconditions** developed for a particular test objective — such as to exercise a particular program path or to verify compliance with a specific requirement. ### Standard Test Case Attributes (IEEE 829 Standard) - **Test Case ID:** Unique identifier (e.g., `TC_LOGIN_001`) - **Test Description:** What is being tested - **Preconditions:** State required before execution - **Test Inputs:** Data provided - **Test Procedure:** Steps to execute - **Expected Output:** What should happen - **Actual Output:** What actually happened (filled during execution) - **Test Status:** Pass / Fail / Blocked - **Postconditions:** State after execution --- ## 1.4 Coverage Criteria > [!IMPORTANT] > **Test Coverage** is a measure (expressed as a percentage) of the **degree to which a test suite exercises the software**. A coverage criterion is a *rule* or *metric* that defines what it means for a test to "adequately cover" the program. The most common coverage criteria covered in KTU Module 1 are: | Coverage Type | What it measures | Unit | |---|---|---| | **Statement Coverage** | Executable statements executed | % of statements | | **Branch (Decision) Coverage** | Decision outcomes (True/False) exercised | % of branches | | **Condition Coverage** | Boolean sub-expressions evaluated | % of conditions | | **Path Coverage** | Independent paths traversed | % of paths | | **Function Coverage** | Functions called at least once | % of functions | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Hint:** Coverage criteria are organized in a hierarchy: **Path ⊇ Branch ⊇ Statement**. Satisfying a stronger criterion automatically satisfies weaker ones. --- ## 1.5 Section 1 Summary Visual ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ THE TESTING VOCABULARY MINDSET │ │ │ │ VERIFICATION → "Building RIGHT?" → STATIC │ │ VALIDATION → "Building RIGHT PRODUCT?" → DYNAMIC │ │ │ │ ERROR → FAULT → FAILURE │ │ (mind) (code) (running system) │ │ │ │ TEST CASE = Inputs + Procedure + Expected Result │ │ COVERAGE = Metric of how much of program is exercised │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` --- # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet In this section, we elevate the foundational definitions into the analytical depth required for **14-mark KTU End Semester Examination** answers. We unpack the logic behind each criterion and consolidate every formula you will need. --- ## 2.1 The Verification & Validation (V&V) Model — Deep Analysis ### Operational Logic Flow 1. **Verification Cycle:** - Requirements review → Design inspection → Code walkthrough → Unit review - Each gate checks: *"Did the previous phase deliver what THIS phase demanded?"* 2. **Validation Cycle:** - Integration testing → System testing → Acceptance testing - Each gate checks: *"Does the running software satisfy the user's REAL problem?"* ### Why Both Are Necessary > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Classic Pitfall:** A system can pass all verification checks (perfectly follows specs) but fail validation (because the specs themselves misunderstood the user). Conversely, a system could satisfy a user (validation) yet violate engineering standards (verification failure). > > **KTU Mantra:** Verification eliminates **defects early** (cheap to fix); Validation confirms **fitness for use** (expensive to fix if missed). ### Real-World Engineering Utility In **aerospace, medical devices, and automotive software** (KTU-relevant domains like CS, ECE, EEE), verification ensures compliance with DO-178C, IEC 62304, and ISO 26262 standards — failures here can be life-threatening. Validation ensures the pilot/doctor/driver can actually accomplish their mission. --- ## 2.2 Test Case Design — Theoretical Framework ### Anatomy of a Well-Formed Test Case A test case must answer **5 W's + 1 H**: | Question | Test Case Field | |---|---| | **WHAT** is being tested? | Test Description | | **WHY** are we testing it? | Requirement Reference ID | | **WHEN/WHERE** does setup begin? | Preconditions | | **HOW** do we execute? | Test Procedure | | **WITH WHAT** data? | Test Inputs | | **WHAT** should happen? | Expected Output | ### Test Case Lifecycle States ``` DESIGNED → REVIEWED → APPROVED → EXECUTED → CLOSED ↘ REOPENED (if fails) ``` ### Good Test Case Properties (QUALITY Attributes) - **Accurate** — matches the requirement - **Economical** — minimal steps to achieve coverage - **Traceable** — linked to requirement ID - **Repeatable** — same result each run - **Self-contained** — no hidden dependencies - **Reviewable** — readable by anyone --- ## 2.3 Coverage Criteria — Theoretical Hierarchy ### Mathematical Foundation of Coverage > [!IMPORTANT] > **Coverage** is fundamentally a **ratio metric**. It compares the *amount of structure exercised* by the test suite against the *total structure available* in the program. ### The Coverage Hierarchy (Strict Subset Relationship) $$ \text{Path Coverage} \;\supset\; \text{Branch Coverage} \;\supset\; \text{Statement Coverage} $$ - A test suite achieving **100% Path Coverage** automatically achieves **100% Branch** and **100% Statement** coverage. - **100% Branch Coverage** does **NOT** guarantee **100% Path Coverage** (multi-branch combinations may be missed). --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in almost every KTU university exam for this module.** | # | Metric | Formula | Variables | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Statement Coverage (SC)** | $SC = \dfrac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}} \times 100$ | $S_{executed}$ = statements exercised; $S_{total}$ = total executable statements | Most basic metric | | 2 | **Branch Coverage (BC)** | $BC = \dfrac{B_{executed}}{B_{total}} \times 100$ | $B_{executed}$ = decision outcomes exercised; $B_{total}$ = total branches (typically $2 \times$ decisions) | Industry standard minimum | | 3 | **Condition Coverage (CC)** | $CC = \dfrac{C_{true} + C_{false}}{2 \times N} \times 100$ | $N$ = number of atomic boolean conditions | White-box testing | | 4 | **Path Coverage (PC)** | $PC = \dfrac{P_{executed}}{P_{total}} \times 100$ | $P_{executed}$ = independent paths traversed; $P_{total}$ = cyclomatic complexity paths | Strongest criterion | | 5 | **Cyclomatic Complexity (CC)** | $CC = E - N + 2$ (graph) or $CC = P + 1$ (predicate nodes) | $E$ = edges; $N$ = nodes; $P$ = predicate nodes | Determines max independent paths | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE (per KTU engine):** The vertical pipe `|` symbol is forbidden inside markdown table cells to prevent table-breaking. Use `\vert` for absolute value notation, e.g., write $\vert x \vert$ instead of $\mid x \mid$ in tables. --- ## 2.5 Why Coverage Criteria Matter in Industry - **Defect Localization:** Higher coverage ⇒ higher probability of triggering latent faults. - **Regression Safety:** Coverage reports (e.g., JaCoCo, Istanbul, Coverage.py) tell QA which lines remain *uncovered* by tests. - **Regulatory Compliance:** Standards like **DO-178C (aviation)** mandate **MC/DC coverage** (Modified Condition/Decision Coverage), a strict variant of condition coverage. - **Cost-Quality Trade-off:** 100% path coverage is **computationally infeasible** for loops; the industry typically settles for **80–90% branch coverage** as the practical optimum. --- ## 2.6 Conceptual Linkage Diagram (Logic Flow) ``` REQUIREMENT │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ VERIFICATION │ Static Review │ (Spec Correct?) │ └─────────┬───────────┘ │ Pass ▼ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ CODING PHASE │ Errors may occur │ (Error → Fault) │ └─────────┬───────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ VALIDATION │ Dynamic Execution │ (Right Product?) │ └─────────┬───────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ FAULT MANIFESTS? │──YES──► FAILURE │ (Coverage Metric) │ (User-visible) └─────────────────────┘ ``` --- # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section delivers **exhaustive, line-by-line worked derivations** for coverage criteria — the calculation that KTU examiners use as the standard for full marks on numerical problems. --- ## 3.1 Worked Example: Statement, Branch & Path Coverage ### 📝 The Program Under Test (PUT) Consider the following pseudocode snippet: ``` 1: int a, b; 2: read(a, b); // STATEMENT 1 3: if (a > 0 && b > 0) { // STATEMENT 2 (branch: TRUE/FALSE) 4: print("Both +ve"); // STATEMENT 3 5: } else { 6: print("Not both"); // STATEMENT 4 7: } ``` ### 🧮 Step 1: Count Total Executable Statements Let $S_{total}$ denote total executable statements. $$ S_{total} = 4 \quad \text{(lines 2, 3, 4, 6)} $$ > **Reasoning:** Lines 1 is a declaration (not executed as instruction logic). Lines 5 and 7 are structural keywords (`else`, `}`) and are not counted as separate executable statements. --- ### 🧮 Step 2: Count Total Branches (Decision Outcomes) The `if` statement has **one decision** with **two outcomes** (TRUE branch, FALSE branch). $$ B_{total} = 2 $$ --- ### 🧮 Step 3: Count Total Independent Paths (Cyclomatic Complexity) Using the **predicate node method**: $$ CC = P + 1 $$ where $P$ = number of predicate nodes (decision points). The `if (a > 0 && b > 0)` is ONE predicate node. $$ P_{total} = 1 + 1 = 2 $$ So **2 independent paths** exist: - **Path 1:** Line 2 → Line 3 (TRUE) → Line 4 → End - **Path 2:** Line 2 → Line 3 (FALSE) → Line 6 → End --- ### 🧮 Step 4: Define the Test Suite Let us design **2 test cases**: | Test ID | Input (a, b) | Path Exercised | |---|---|---| | $T_1$ | (5, 10) | Path 1 — TRUE branch | | $T_2$ | (-1, 5) | Path 2 — FALSE branch | --- ### 🧮 Step 5: Compute Statement Coverage (SC) $$ SC = \frac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}} \times 100 $$ **With $T_1$ alone:** - Executes: Lines 2, 3, 4 → $S_{executed} = 3$ - Lines NOT executed: Line 6 $$ SC(T_1) = \frac{3}{4} \times 100 = 75\% $$ **With $T_1 + T_2$ together:** - Executes: Lines 2, 3, 4, 6 → $S_{executed} = 4$ $$ SC(T_1 + T_2) = \frac{4}{4} \times 100 = 100\% $$ --- ### 🧮 Step 6: Compute Branch Coverage (BC) $$ BC = \frac{B_{executed}}{B_{total}} \times 100 $$ **With $T_1 + T_2$:** - TRUE branch exercised by $T_1$ - FALSE branch exercised by $T_2$ - $B_{executed} = 2$ $$ BC(T_1 + T_2) = \frac{2}{2} \times 100 = 100\% $$ --- ### 🧮 Step 7: Compute Path Coverage (PC) $$ PC = \frac{P_{executed}}{P_{total}} \times 100 $$ **With $T_1 + T_2$:** - Both independent paths traversed $$ PC(T_1 + T_2) = \frac{2}{2} \times 100 = 100\% $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: A Loop Introduces More Paths ### 📝 Updated PUT with a Loop ``` 1: int i = 0, sum = 0; 2: while (i < 3) { // STATEMENT 1 (branch: TRUE/FALSE) 3: sum = sum + i; // STATEMENT 2 4: i = i + 1; // STATEMENT 3 5: } 6: print(sum); // STATEMENT 4 ``` ### 🧮 Total Statements $$ S_{total} = 4 $$ ### 🧮 Total Branches The `while` loop has **two outcomes**: enter body (TRUE) or exit (FALSE). $$ B_{total} = 2 $$ ### 🧮 Total Independent Paths (Cyclomatic Complexity) Using $CC = P + 1$ where $P = 1$: $$ P_{total} = 1 + 1 = 2 $$ However, the **executable paths through the loop** are actually: - Path 1: Skip loop (i = 0, but if `i` starts ≥ 3, then condition false → skip) - Path 2: Execute once - Path 3: Execute twice - Path 4: Execute thrice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Trap:** The number of *executable* paths is NOT equal to cyclomatic complexity. Cyclomatic complexity counts **independent paths** in the control flow graph, not all execution traces (which can be infinite for unbounded loops). For testing practicality, we still target 2 independent paths to satisfy branch coverage. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Coverage Criteria | Property | Statement | Branch | Condition | Path | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Strength** | Weakest | Medium | Medium | Strongest | | **Misses hidden branches?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | | **Detects missing else?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Detects short-circuit bugs?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Implies above?** | — | ⊇ SC | (Indep. of BC) | ⊇ BC ⊇ SC | | **Cyclomatic-bounded?** | N/A | N/A | N/A | ✅ Yes | | **MC/DC (advanced)?** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ (separate) | > [!NOTE] > **MC/DC (Modified Condition/Decision Coverage)** is a stricter variant used in avionics — every condition must independently affect the decision outcome. KTU Module 1 only requires awareness of MC/DC as a name. --- ## 3.4 Full Derivation Summary (Ready for KTU Answer Sheet) For any program with: - $S_{total}$ executable statements - $D$ decision points (predicates) - $B_{total} = 2D$ branches - $P_{total} = D + 1$ independent paths $$ SC = \frac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}} \times 100\% $$ $$ BC = \frac{B_{executed}}{2D} \times 100\% $$ $$ PC = \frac{P_{executed}}{D + 1} \times 100\% $$ --- # 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics This section provides **professionally rendered Mermaid diagrams** that visualize the conceptual relationships. Each diagram follows strict compilation safety rules (alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside labels). --- ## 4.1 Diagram 1: The V-Model — Verification vs. Validation Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart LR req1[REQUIREMENTS] --> ver1{VERIFICATION
SPECS REVIEW} sys2[SYSTEM DESIGN] --> ver2{VERIFICATION
DESIGN REVIEW} det3[DETAILED DESIGN] --> ver3{VERIFICATION
CODE WALKTHROUGH} cod4[CODING] --> ver4{VERIFICATION
INSPECTION} ver1 --> sys2 ver2 --> det3 ver3 --> cod4 cod4 --> integ5[INTEGRATION] integ5 --> val1{VALIDATION
INT TEST} integ5 --> syst6[SYSTEM TESTING] syst6 --> val2{VALIDATION
SYS TEST} syst6 --> acc7[ACCEPTANCE] acc7 --> val3{VALIDATION
UAT} val1 --> ope1[OPERATION] val2 --> ope1 val3 --> ope1 style ver1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style ver2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style ver3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style ver4 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style val1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4 style val2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4 style val3 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4 ``` > **Legend:** 🟧 Orange boxes = Verification activities (Static). 🟦 Blue boxes = Validation activities (Dynamic). --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Error → Fault → Failure Cascade ```mermaid flowchart TD hum[HUMAN DEVELOPER
MENTAL STATE] -->|Commits| err[ERROR
Mistake in thinking] err -->|Encoded into| fault[FAULT
Defect in code artifact] fault -->|Stored in| repo[CODEBASE] repo -->|Code executed| run[RUNNING PROGRAM] run -->|Fault triggered| fail[FAILURE
User-visible deviation] run -.->|Fault NOT triggered| nofail[NO FAILURE
Latent defect remains] test[TEST EXECUTION] -->|Detects| fail cov[COVERAGE METRIC] -->|Quantifies| test style hum fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C style err fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C style fault fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00 style fail fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000 style nofail fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969 style test fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style cov fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B ``` --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Coverage Criteria Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD path[PATH COVERAGE
Strongest] branch[BRANCH COVERAGE
Decision outcomes] cond[CONDITION COVERAGE
Boolean sub-exprs] stmt[STATEMENT COVERAGE
Weakest] path ==>|"100 percent implies"| branch branch ==>|"100 percent implies"| stmt cond -.->|"Independent dimension"| branch style path fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style branch fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,color:#fff style cond fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#fff style stmt fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 ``` --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Test Case Lifecycle State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Designed Designed --> Reviewed : Peer Review Reviewed --> Approved : Lead Sign-off Reviewed --> Designed : Rejected Approved --> Executed : Run Test Executed --> Passed : Result Matches Expected Executed --> Failed : Result Mismatch Failed --> Reopened : Log Defect Reopened --> Designed : Fixed and Re-test Passed --> Closed Reopened --> Closed : Deferred/Blocked Closed --> [*] ``` --- ## 4.5 Diagram 5: Control Flow Graph for Coverage Calculation ```mermaid flowchart TD node1((1: Entry)) --> node2((2: i 0)) node2 --> node3{3: while i less than 3} node3 -->|TRUE| node4((4: sum update)) node4 --> node5((5: i increment)) node5 --> node3 node3 -->|FALSE| node6((6: print sum)) node6 --> node7((7: Exit)) style node3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00 ``` > **Analysis of Graph:** > - Nodes $N = 7$ > - Edges $E = 8$ (1→2, 2→3, 3→4, 4→5, 5→3, 3→6, 6→7) > - Predicate nodes $P = 1$ > > **Cyclomatic Complexity (graph method):** $CC = E - N + 2 = 8 - 7 + 2 = 3$ > > **Cyclomatic Complexity (predicate method):** $CC = P + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2$ > > Both methods confirm **2 independent paths** — sufficient to achieve 100% branch coverage. --- # 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap This section simulates an authentic **KTU End Semester Examination** paper for Module 1, following the 2024 scheme marks distribution (3-mark short answers + 14-mark long answers with internal choice). --- ## 📌 PART A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) --- ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between Verification and Validation in software testing.** (3 Marks) #### ✅ Model Answer (Examiner's Key Words in **Bold**): **Verification** is the process of evaluating the **products of a development phase** to ensure they satisfy the **conditions imposed at the start** of that phase. It is a **static** analysis activity that includes reviews, walkthroughs, and inspections. Verification answers the question: *"Are we building the product **right**?"* **Validation**, on the other hand, is the process of evaluating the **running software** to ensure it meets the **user's actual requirements**. It is a **dynamic** activity requiring code execution, including unit, integration, system, and acceptance testing. Validation answers: *"Are we building the **right** product?"* **Key Distinction:** Verification is **specification-driven** (checking conformance); Validation is **usage-driven** (checking fitness for purpose). --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the terms Error, Fault, and Failure with a suitable example.** (3 Marks) #### ✅ Model Answer: - **Error**: A **human mistake** made by the developer during the software development process (e.g., a logic mistake, syntax slip, or misunderstanding of requirement). - **Fault (Defect/Bug)**: The **manifestation of an error** in the software artifact (incorrect code, incorrect document) that, if executed, could cause a failure. - **Failure**: The **deviation of the software** from its expected behavior observable by the user during execution. **Example:** A programmer intends to write `if (x >= 100)` but types `if (x > 100)` — this is the **Error**. The wrongly typed condition stored in the source code is the **Fault**. When $x = 100$, the program skips the intended branch, producing wrong output — this is the **Failure**. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write that "Error = Bug = Fault" interchangeably. **KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks** for this conflation. Always emphasize the **mind → artifact → running system** progression. --- ## 📌 PART B: Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE) --- ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` **(a)** Explain the different **test coverage criteria** used in software testing. Discuss **Statement Coverage, Branch Coverage, Condition Coverage, and Path Coverage** with examples. **(7 Marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution: **Test coverage** is a measure of the degree to which a test suite exercises the software. Different criteria evaluate different structural elements of the program. **1. Statement Coverage (SC):** - Measures the **percentage of executable statements** exercised by a test suite. - Formula: $SC = (S_{executed} / S_{total}) \times 100$ - **Example:** If a program has 4 statements and a test executes 3, $SC = 75\%$. - **Weakness:** Misses uncovered branches (e.g., missing `else` block). **2. Branch (Decision) Coverage (BC):** - Measures the **percentage of decision outcomes** (True/False) exercised. - Formula: $BC = (B_{executed} / B_{total}) \times 100$, where $B_{total} = 2 \times \text{decisions}$. - **Example:** An `if` statement has 2 branches; testing both yields 100% branch coverage. **3. Condition Coverage (CC):** - Measures the **percentage of boolean sub-expressions** that evaluate to both True and False. - Formula: $CC = ((C_{true} + C_{false}) / (2 \times N)) \times 100$, where $N$ = number of atomic conditions. - **Example:** For `if (a > 0 && b > 0)`, four test cases are needed to independently vary `a > 0` and `b > 0`. **4. Path Coverage (PC):** - Measures the **percentage of independent paths** (cyclomatic complexity paths) traversed. - Formula: $PC = (P_{executed} / P_{total}) \times 100$, where $P_{total} = D + 1$ ($D$ = decision points). - **Strongest criterion** — 100% path coverage implies 100% branch and statement coverage. **Hierarchy:** $PC \supset BC \supset SC$. --- **(b)** For the following code snippet, compute **Statement Coverage, Branch Coverage, and Path Coverage** for the given test cases.
