Identify if any criterion is not satisfied. **(7 Marks)** ``` 1: int x, y; 2: read(x, y); 3: if (x > 10) { 4: y = y + 1; 5: } else { 6: y = y - 1; 7: } 8: if (y > 0) { 9: print("Positive"); 10: } else { 11: print("Non-positive"); 12: } ``` **Test Cases:** - $T_1$: $x = 15, y = 5$ - $T_2$: $x = 5, y = -3$ - $T_3$: $x = 15, y = -3$ #### ✅ Model Solution: **Step 1: Identify Structure** - Executable statements: Lines 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 9, 11 → **$S_{total} = 7$** - Decision points: Two `if` statements ($D = 2$) - Total branches: **$B_{total} = 2 \times 2 = 4$** - Independent paths: **$P_{total} = D + 1 = 3$** Wait — recalculating paths. With 2 binary decisions, the **maximum independent paths** are **$2 \times 2 = 4$** (TT, TF, FT, FF combinations), though the cyclomatic count by graph formula gives 3. For testing purposes, we consider all combinations: **$P_{total} = 4$**. **Step 2: Execute Each Test** | Test | Path | Line 3 (x>10) | Line 4 | Line 6 | Line 8 (y>0) | Line 9 | Line 11 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $T_1$: (15, 5) | T-T | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | | $T_2$: (5, -3) | F-F | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | $T_3$: (15, -3) | T-F | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | **Step 3: Aggregate Statement Execution** - Lines executed across $T_1, T_2, T_3$: 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 9, 11 (all 7) → $S_{executed} = 7$ $$ SC = \frac{7}{7} \times 100 = 100\% $$ **Step 4: Aggregate Branch Execution** - Branches: - Line 3 TRUE: ✅ ($T_1, T_3$) - Line 3 FALSE: ✅ ($T_2$) - Line 8 TRUE: ✅ ($T_1$) - Line 8 FALSE: ✅ ($T_2, T_3$) All 4 branches executed → $B_{executed} = 4$ $$ BC = \frac{4}{4} \times 100 = 100\% $$ **Step 5: Aggregate Path Execution** - Paths executed: T-T ($T_1$), F-F ($T_2$), T-F ($T_3$) - Path F-T: ❌ Not executed (no test where $x \leq 10$ AND $y > 0$) $$ PC = \frac{3}{4} \times 100 = 75\% $$ **Step 6: Conclusion** - **Statement Coverage = 100%** ✅ Satisfied - **Branch Coverage = 100%** ✅ Satisfied - **Path Coverage = 75%** ❌ Not satisfied (Path F-T missing) **Missing Test Case Required:** $T_4$: $x = 5, y = 5$ to complete F-T path. --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Verification and Validation (V&V) Model** in software engineering. How does it relate to the waterfall model? **(7 Marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution: **Verification and Validation Model:** The V&V Model is a **systems development lifecycle** where each development phase has a corresponding testing phase, forming a **V-shape**. Verification phases (left arm) run **in parallel** with validation phases (right arm). **Left Arm — Verification (Static, Done Before Execution):** 1. **Requirements Specification** → reviewed for completeness, consistency, and feasibility 2. **System Design** → reviewed for architectural correctness 3. **Detailed Design** → reviewed for module-level correctness 4. **Coding** → inspections, walkthroughs, and static analysis **Right Arm — Validation (Dynamic, Done After Coding):** 1. **Unit Testing** → validates individual modules against detailed design 2. **Integration Testing** → validates module interactions against system design 3. **System Testing** → validates complete system against requirements 4. **Acceptance Testing** → validates against user needs (UAT) **Relationship with Waterfall:** The V&V Model is essentially the **Waterfall Model with explicit testing linkage**. Each development phase cascades downward (Waterfall behavior), but at the end of coding, testing phases cascade upward, with each test level validating the corresponding design level. **Advantages:** - Defects caught **early** (cheaper to fix) - Clear traceability between requirement and test - Supports **regulatory compliance** (avionics, medical devices) **Limitations:** - Rigid — late changes are expensive - No iterative prototyping support *(Diagram already provided in Section 4.1)* --- **(b)** Define **Test Case**. List and explain **any six attributes** of a well-designed test case. **(7 Marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution: **Definition:** A **Test Case** is a set of **preconditions, inputs, actions, expected results, and postconditions** developed for a particular test objective, to exercise a specific program path or verify compliance with a requirement. **Six Attributes of a Good Test Case:** 1. **Accuracy:** The test case must correctly test what it claims to test. An inaccurate test gives misleading pass/fail signals. 2. **Economy:** Each test case should achieve maximum coverage with minimum steps — avoiding redundant or overlapping cases saves time and effort. 3. **Traceability:** Every test case must be linked to its originating **requirement ID** (e.g., REQ_LOGIN_005). This ensures all requirements are covered and supports impact analysis during change requests. 4. **Repeatability:** Running the same test case multiple times must yield the same result, given the same preconditions. Non-deterministic tests are unreliable. 5. **Self-Containment:** A test case must include all necessary preconditions and setup steps within its documentation — it should not depend on undocumented prior states. 6. **Reviewability:** The test case must be written in clear, plain language so that any team member (developer, tester, manager) can execute it without ambiguity. **Additional Attribute (Optional):** 7. **Independence:** Each test case should be runnable independently of others, allowing parallel execution and isolated defect diagnosis. --- ## 📚 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam — Module 1** - ✅ **Verification** = Static, checks conformance to specs, *"Building right?"* - ✅ **Validation** = Dynamic, checks fitness for use, *"Building right product?"* - ✅ **Error → Fault → Failure** is a **one-way cascade** (Mind → Artifact → Running System) - ✅ A **Bug** is **synonymous** with a **Fault** (not with Failure) - ✅ **Not every fault causes a failure** — only when executed and triggered - ✅ **Test Case** has 5 components: Preconditions, Inputs, Procedure, Expected Output, Postconditions - ✅ **IEEE 829** is the standard for test documentation - ✅ **Coverage Hierarchy:** Path ⊇ Branch ⊇ Statement (strict subset) - ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity** (McCabe) = $E - N + 2$ (graph) OR $P + 1$ (predicates) - ✅ **Independent Paths** = $D + 1$ where $D$ = decision points - ✅ **100% Branch Coverage** is the **industry standard minimum**, not 100% Statement - ✅ **MC/DC** is for safety-critical systems (avionics, medical) — beyond Module 1 scope but name-memorize it - ✅ **Test Case Attributes to memorize:** Accuracy, Economy, Traceability, Repeatability, Self-Containment, Reviewability --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 FINAL EXAMINER'S WARNING:** > 1. Do NOT confuse **Verification** with **Validation** — they differ in **timing** (development vs. execution), **method** (static vs. dynamic), and **focus** (specs vs. user needs). > 2. In coverage problems, **always state your assumptions clearly**: count only *executable* statements, count branches as $2 \times$ decisions, and count paths using cyclomatic complexity. > 3. For V&V diagram questions, **draw the V-shape** — students who draw it as a straight waterfall line lose **2–3 marks** immediately. ---

topics.title: Types of Testing - Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance, Performance (stress, usability, regression), and Security Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Testing - Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance, Performance (stress, usability, regression), and Security Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Types of Testing"?

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECST631 (Software Testing)**, the term *Types of Testing* refers to a **taxonomic classification of software test activities** designed to validate and verify different quality attributes of a software system at distinct levels of granularity, ranging from the smallest individual code units to the entire deployed application under adversarial and load conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> *Software testing types* are the **distinct categories of test activities** (Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance, Performance, and Security Testing) executed across the **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** to uncover defects, validate functional and non-functional requirements, and ensure the delivered product conforms to user expectations and contractual obligations.

These types are **NOT mutually exclusive** — they are **complementary layers** stacked on top of each other, often visualized as the famous **"Test Pyramid"** introduced by Mike Cohn.

---

## 1.2 The Seven Major Testing Types — At a Glance

| # | Testing Type | What It Verifies | Who Performs It | Stage in SDLC |
|---|--------------|------------------|-----------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Unit Testing** | Individual functions/methods | Developer | Coding |
| 2 | **Integration Testing** | Interaction between modules | Developer / Tester | Post-unit |
| 3 | **System Testing** | Complete integrated system | Independent QA Team | Pre-release |
| 4 | **Acceptance Testing** | Business/user requirements | End User / Client | Pre-deployment |
| 5 | **Performance Testing** | Speed, stability under load | Performance Engineer | System stage |
| 6 | **Regression Testing** | Stability after changes | QA Team | After every change |
| 7 | **Security Testing** | Confidentiality, integrity, auth | Security Specialist | System stage |

---

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — Building a House 🏠

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are a civil engineer constructing a **10-storey apartment building**.
>
> - **Unit Testing** → You check **each brick, each beam, each tile** individually before placing it.
> - **Integration Testing** → You check whether the **door fits the frame**, the **window opens smoothly in the wall**, the **plumbing connects correctly to the bathroom fixtures**.
> - **System Testing** → You check the **entire building**: Does the elevator work? Does the water reach the 10th floor? Does the wiring handle all loads?
> - **Acceptance Testing** → The **building inspector and the buyer** walk through — does it match the brochure? Do they sign the handover form?
> - **Performance Testing** → You simulate a **festival-day crowd of 5,000 people** using the lobby, stairs, and parking.
> - **Security Testing** → You hire an **ethical burglar** to try to break in and check if the CCTV, locks, and alarm system are robust.

This same layered philosophy applies directly to software — only instead of bricks, we test **functions, classes, APIs, databases, and UI screens**.

---

## 1.4 Why Are These Types Necessary?

A single "test everything at once" approach is **practically impossible** because:

1. **Defect Localization** — Finding a bug in 1 million lines of monolithic code is impossible; testing small units first makes bugs easy to locate.
2. **Cost of Fixing** — A defect caught at *unit* stage costs **~\$100**; the same defect at *acceptance* stage can cost **~\$10,000** (Boehm's Cost-of-Fix curve).
3. **Risk Reduction** — Each test type addresses a specific **risk category** (functional, performance, security, usability).

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer-by-Layer Theoretical Breakdown

### 🔹 2.1.1 Unit Testing (Module / Component Testing)

- **Definition:** The testing of the **smallest testable parts** of an application, called *units*, in isolation from the rest of the system.
- **Granularity:** Function, method, procedure, class, or object.
- **Technique Used:** **White-box testing** (developer has access to source code).
- **Frameworks:** JUnit (Java), pytest (Python), NUnit (.NET), GoogleTest (C++).
- **Stub/Driver:** A *driver* simulates a calling module; a *stub* simulates a called module.

**Step-by-step logic flow of a typical unit test:**

1. **Arrange** — Initialize the unit and its inputs.
2. **Act** — Invoke the unit's function under test.
3. **Assert** — Compare actual output with expected output.
4. **Cleanup** — Release any external resources.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners LOVE asking: *"Why is unit testing not sufficient to ensure software quality?"* Answer: Because it **cannot detect integration defects, environmental defects, or end-to-end business logic failures** that arise only when units interact.

---

### 🔹 2.1.2 Integration Testing

- **Definition:** Testing the **interaction between integrated modules** to expose faults in the interaction between integrated units.
- **Approaches:**
  * **Big-Bang Integration:** All modules integrated at once and tested together *(high risk, hard to isolate failures)*.
  * **Incremental Integration:** Modules added one at a time.
    - **Top-Down:** Higher-level modules tested first using stubs for lower-level.
    - **Bottom-Up:** Lower-level modules tested first using drivers for higher-level.
    - **Sandwich (Hybrid):** Combines both top-down and bottom-up.

| Approach | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|----------|-----------|--------------|
| Big-Bang | Quick, simple | Hard defect localization |
| Top-Down | Early prototype possible | Lower modules tested late |
| Bottom-Up | Easier to verify lower-level | Top-level defects found late |
| Sandwich | Balanced | Requires more manpower |

---

### 🔹 2.1.3 System Testing

- **Definition:** Testing the **complete and integrated software system** as a whole against specified requirements.
- **Type:** **Black-box testing** (tester need not know internal code).
- **Categories:**
  * **Functional System Testing** — Does each feature work?
  * **Non-Functional System Testing** — Performance, usability, security, load.
  * **Recovery Testing** — Can the system recover from crashes?
  * **Compatibility Testing** — Does it work across browsers/OS/hardware?

---

### 🔹 2.1.4 Acceptance Testing

- **Definition:** The **final level of testing** conducted to determine whether the system meets the **acceptance criteria** and is ready for delivery.
- **Sub-types:**
  * **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)** — Performed by end users.
  * **Business Acceptance Testing (BAT)** — Performed against business objectives.
  * **Contract Acceptance Testing (CAT)** — Performed against contract specs.
  * **Regulation Acceptance Testing (RAT)** — Performed against legal/regulatory standards.
  * **Alpha Testing** — Performed at the developer's site by internal users.
  * **Beta Testing** — Performed at the customer's site by a limited external audience.

---

### 🔹 2.1.5 Performance Testing

A **non-functional** test type that determines the system's performance in terms of **responsiveness and stability** under a workload.

**Sub-categories explicitly mentioned in the KTU 2024 syllabus:**

| Sub-type | Objective | Key Metric |
|----------|-----------|------------|
| **Load Testing** | Behavior under expected load | Response time at normal load |
| **Stress Testing** | Behavior under extreme load (beyond limits) | Breaking point, throughput at peak |
| **Endurance/Soak Testing** | Behavior under sustained load over time | Memory leaks, degradation |
| **Spike Testing** | Behavior under sudden load spikes | Recovery rate |
| **Volume Testing** | Behavior with large data volumes | DB query time |
| **Usability Testing** | How easy and user-friendly is the UI? | Task completion time, error rate, satisfaction score (SUS) |
| **Regression Testing** | Does new code break old functionality? | Pass-rate of existing test cases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regression Testing vs. Re-Testing** — a classic KTU question:
> - **Re-Testing** → Executing a *failing test case again* after the defect is fixed.
> - **Regression Testing** → Executing *all existing test cases* to ensure the fix (or new feature) has **NOT broken** any previously working functionality.

---

### 🔹 2.1.6 Security Testing

- **Definition:** Testing that uncovers **vulnerabilities, threats, and risks** in a software application, ensuring the system is secure from intruders and malicious attacks.
- **Core security properties validated (CIA Triad):**
  * **C**onfidentiality — Only authorized users access data.
  * **I**ntegrity — Data is not altered by unauthorized parties.
  * **A**vailability — System is accessible when needed.

**Common techniques:**

| Technique | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| **Penetration Testing** | Simulating an actual attack to find exploitable weaknesses |
| **Vulnerability Scanning** | Automated scan for known CVEs (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) |
| **Security Auditing** | Code and architecture review against security standards (OWASP, ISO 27001) |
| **Ethical Hacking** | Authorized attempt to breach security |
| **SQL Injection Testing** | Verify inputs are sanitized |
| **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Testing** | Verify output encoding |
| **Authentication/Authorization Testing** | Verify access controls |

> [!NOTE]
> The **OWASP Top 10** is the most commonly referenced security-testing standard worldwide. KTU examiners may expect awareness of categories like *Injection*, *Broken Authentication*, *Sensitive Data Exposure*, etc.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Testing Metrics)

Although testing is largely qualitative, **exam-ready metrics calculations** appear regularly in KTU papers. Master these:

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|--------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | **Test Coverage** | $C = \frac{T_{\text{covered}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of requirements covered |
| 2 | **Defect Density** | $D = \frac{N_{\text{defects}}}{S}$ | Defects per KLOC (thousand lines of code) |
| 3 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $\text{DRE} = \frac{D_{\text{pre-release}}}{D_{\text{pre-release}} + D_{\text{post-release}}} \times 100\%$ | Effectiveness of testing phase |
| 4 | **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Where $\lambda$ = failure rate |
| 5 | **Test Effectiveness** | $E = \frac{T_{\text{failed}}}{T_{\text{executed}}} \times 100\%$ | Higher = more defects caught |
| 6 | **Availability** | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures; MTTR = Mean Time To Repair |
| 7 | **Reliability Growth** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential decay model |
| 8 | **Requirement Coverage** | $RC = \frac{R_{\text{tested}}}{R_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Business requirements coverage |
| 9 | **Cost of Testing** | $C_{\text{test}} = C_{\text{tooling}} + C_{\text{people}} + C_{\text{infrastructure}}$ | Total QA cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NOTE on Table Symbols:** Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE markdown safety rules, the table above uses `\` to escape formulas and does not use the vertical pipe `$\vert$` symbol inside cell contents to prevent table-rendering errors.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-Commerce sites** (e.g., Flipkart) — Run **stress + load + spike** tests before Diwali sales.
- **Banking software** (e.g., SBI NetBanking) — **Security + penetration** testing is mandatory by RBI.
- **Medical devices** (e.g., pacemakers) — **Unit + integration + acceptance** testing is required by FDA standards.
- **Avionics** (Boeing, Airbus) — All **seven test types** are mandatory, with traceability to DO-178C standards.

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Test Coverage Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> *A software module has 240 test cases written for it. Out of these, 186 test cases have been executed. Of the executed ones, 24 failed. Compute (a) Test Coverage, (b) Test Effectiveness, and (c) the number of test cases still pending execution.*

### Given Data
- Total test cases $T_{\text{total}} = 240$
- Executed test cases $T_{\text{executed}} = 186$
- Failed test cases $T_{\text{failed}} = 24$

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### (a) Test Coverage

Test coverage is sometimes expressed in two ways — by **execution coverage** or by **requirement coverage**. Here we use execution coverage.

$$
C = \frac{T_{\text{executed}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the values.

$$
C = \frac{186}{240} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2:** Perform the division.

$$
\frac{186}{240} = 0.775
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to percentage.

$$
C = 0.775 \times 100\% = 77.5\%
$$

**Result:** Test Coverage = **77.5%**

---

#### (b) Test Effectiveness

$$
E = \frac{T_{\text{failed}}}{T_{\text{executed}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute.

$$
E = \frac{24}{186} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2:** Divide.

$$
\frac{24}{186} = 0.1290
$$

**Step 3:** Convert.

$$
E \approx 12.90\%
$$

**Result:** Test Effectiveness = **12.90%**

---

#### (c) Pending Test Cases

$$
T_{\text{pending}} = T_{\text{total}} - T_{\text{executed}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{pending}} = 240 - 186 = 54
$$

**Result:** **54 test cases** are still pending.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Defect Density & DRE

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> *A software project of 30 KLOC was tested. The QA team found 45 defects before release. After release, customers reported 5 more defects. Compute (a) Pre-release Defect Density, (b) Post-release Defect Density, (c) Defect Removal Efficiency.*

### Given Data
- Size $S = 30$ KLOC
- Pre-release defects $D_{\text{pre}} = 45$
- Post-release defects $D_{\text{post}} = 5$

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### (a) Pre-release Defect Density

$$
D_{\text{pre}} = \frac{D_{\text{pre}}}{S}
$$

$$
D_{\text{pre}} = \frac{45}{30} = 1.5 \; \text{defects/KLOC}
$$

#### (b) Post-release Defect Density

$$
D_{\text{post}} = \frac{D_{\text{post}}}{S}
$$

$$
D_{\text{post}} = \frac{5}{30} \approx 0.167 \; \text{defects/KLOC}
$$

#### (c) Defect Removal Efficiency

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{D_{\text{pre}}}{D_{\text{pre}} + D_{\text{post}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 1:** Add pre + post defects.

$$
D_{\text{pre}} + D_{\text{post}} = 45 + 5 = 50
$$

**Step 2:** Compute ratio.

$$
\frac{45}{50} = 0.9
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to percentage.

$$
\text{DRE} = 0.9 \times 100\% = 90\%
$$

**Result:** DRE = **90%** (Industry benchmark: ≥ 95% is considered excellent).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Test Type

| Scenario / Trigger Event | Recommended Primary Test Type | Secondary Test Type |
|--------------------------|-------------------------------|---------------------|
| New module coded | Unit Testing | Code Review |
| Two modules connected for the first time | Integration Testing | Unit Testing |
| Build released to QA | System Testing | Regression Testing |
| Build released to client | Acceptance Testing (UAT) | System Testing |
| Patch applied to fix a bug | Re-Testing + Regression Testing | Integration Testing |
| Banking app with login feature | Security Testing | Functional Testing |
| E-commerce app before Black Friday | Performance Testing (Load + Stress) | Security Testing |
| New version released with new features | Full Regression Testing | Smoke Testing |
| Medical software for FDA approval | Acceptance Testing (RAT) | System Testing |

---

## 3.4 Mapping Test Types to V-Model SDLC Phases

| SDLC Phase (Development Side) | Corresponding Test Type (Verification Side) |
|-------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Requirements Analysis | Acceptance Test Plan |
| System Design | System Test Plan |
| Module / Detailed Design | Integration Test Plan |
| Coding | Unit Test Plan |
| (no direct V-model pair) | Performance + Security (cross-cutting) |

# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Software Testing Pyramid (Mike Cohn)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram below is the **classical Test Pyramid** that visualizes the **relative number of tests at each level** — wide base = many small fast unit tests, narrow top = few slow end-to-end tests.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[E2E Acceptance Tests FEW SLOW]
    B0[System Tests MODERATE]
    C0[Integration Tests MANY]
    D0[Unit Tests NUMEROUS FAST]
    A0 --> B0
    B0 --> C0
    C0 --> D0
```

> **Interpretation (Board-Examiner Friendly):**
> - **Bottom layer (Unit)** — Fastest, cheapest, most numerous. Foundation of the pyramid.
> - **Middle layer (Integration)** — Fewer in count; tests module-to-module data flow.
> - **Upper-middle (System)** — Even fewer; tests the whole integrated system.
> - **Top (Acceptance)** — Fewest; tests real business scenarios; slow and expensive.

---

## 4.2 Hierarchical Test-Process Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    req[Requirements Spec] --> tc[Test Case Design]
    tc --> ut[Unit Testing]
    ut --> it[Integration Testing]
    it --> syst[System Testing]
    syst --> pt[Performance Testing]
    syst --> st[Security Testing]
    syst --> rt[Regression Testing]
    syst --> at[Acceptance Testing]
    at --> release[Production Release]
```

> Each block is a **test stage**. The arrows represent the **execution order**. Note that *Performance, Security, and Regression* branches originate at the **System stage** because they require a fully integrated system.

---

## 4.3 Integration Testing Strategies — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    int[Integration Testing] --> bb[Big Bang]
    int --> inc[Incremental]
    inc --> td[Top Down]
    inc --> bu[Bottom Up]
    inc --> san[Sandwich Hybrid]
    td --> stub[Uses Stubs for lower modules]
    bu --> drv[Uses Drivers for higher modules]
```

---

## 4.4 Security Testing — CIA Triad Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    c[Confidentiality] -->|ensures| data[Data Accessible only to authorized]
    i[Integrity] -->|ensures| data
    a[Availability] -->|ensures| data
    data --> sec[Secure Software System]
    sec --> pt[Penetration Testing]
    sec --> vs[Vulnerability Scanning]
    sec --> sa[Security Auditing]
    sec --> eh[Ethical Hacking]
```

> The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model for security testing. Each technique listed in the bottom row helps validate one or more pillars of the triad.

---

## 4.5 Performance Testing Sub-Types — Functional Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    perf[Performance Testing] --> ld[Load Testing]
    perf --> str[Stress Testing]
    perf --> end[Endurance Testing]
    perf --> spk[Spike Testing]
    perf --> vol[Volume Testing]
    perf --> usab[Usability Testing]
    perf --> reg[Regression Testing]
```
