## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Black-Box Testing and White-Box Testing. List any two techniques used in each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Black-Box Testing | White-Box Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Tests functionality without knowledge of internal code | Tests internal structure with full code access |
| **Focus** | Input–Output behavior, requirements validation | Internal paths, branches, conditions, statements |
| **Tester Perspective** | End-user viewpoint | Developer's viewpoint |
| **Test Basis** | SRS, use cases, user stories | Source code, design documents |
| **Techniques (2 each)** | (i) Equivalence Partitioning, (ii) Boundary Value Analysis | (i) Statement Coverage, (ii) Cyclomatic Complexity |

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition 1M + Focus/Basis 1M + Two techniques each 1M.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is Grey-Box Testing? Give two real-world scenarios where it is applied.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Grey-Box Testing is a hybrid software testing method in which the tester has **partial knowledge** of the internal structure of the application. Test cases are designed using a combination of black-box functional inputs and white-box awareness of internal data structures, algorithms, or environmental constraints.

**Real-World Scenarios (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Integration Testing of Microservices** — Tester knows the API contracts (partial internal) but tests the functional interaction between services.
2. **Penetration Testing** — An ethical hacker knows the OS version, IP range, and web server type (partial internal) but not the application's source code, while testing for security vulnerabilities.
3. *(Optional 3rd example)* **Database Testing** — Tester knows the schema (table structures) but not the stored procedures or triggers.

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question has **internal choice** between **Question A** and **Question B**, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

## ⭐ **QUESTION A — (14 Marks)**

### **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the **Cyclomatic Complexity** metric in White-Box Testing. Describe all three methods to compute it. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following pseudo-code. Draw the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)**, compute the **Cyclomatic Complexity** using all three methods, and identify the **independent basis paths**. **(7 Marks)**

```c
1: int Authenticate(int userId, char password[]) {
2:     if (userId > 0)                              // Decision 1
3:         if (strcmp(password, "valid") == 0)      // Decision 2
4:             return 1;                            // Authorized
5:         else
6:             return -1;                           // Wrong password
7:     else if (userId == 0)
8:         return 0;                                // Guest mode
9:     else
10:        return -2;                               // Invalid user
11: }
```

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Cyclomatic Complexity, proposed by **Thomas J. McCabe (1976)**, is a software metric that quantitatively measures the **number of linearly independent paths** through a program's source code. It defines the **minimum number of test cases** required to achieve **branch coverage** of the program.

**Three Methods of Calculation (5 Marks):**

**Method 1 — Edge-Node Formula:**
$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$
Where $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components (usually 1).

**Method 2 — Predicate Node Formula:**
$$
V(G) = P + 1
$$
Where $P$ = number of predicate (decision) nodes containing conditional logic.

**Method 3 — Region Formula:**
$$
V(G) = R
$$
Where $R$ = number of enclosed regions in the planar CFG (including the outer region).

**Utility:** Independent of programming language, identifies risky modules, guides test design, and predicts maintenance effort. A $V(G) > 10$ indicates high complexity and refactoring is advised.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

#### Step 1 — Construct the CFG

**Nodes (N = 10):**
- N1 = START
- N2 = Predicate `userId > 0`
- N3 = Predicate `strcmp(...) == 0`
- N4 = `return 1`
- N5 = `return -1`
- N6 = Predicate `userId == 0`
- N7 = `return 0`
- N8 = `return -2`
- N9 = END
- *(N10 = implicit join node)* → For simplicity, N = 9 in standard count

**Edges (E = 11):**
- N1→N2, N2→N3 (T), N2→N6 (F), N3→N4 (T), N3→N5 (F), N4→N9, N5→N9, N6→N7 (T), N6→N8 (F), N7→N9, N8→N9

**Predicates (P = 3):** N2, N3, N6
**Regions (R = 4):** 3 enclosed + 1 outer

#### Step 2 — Calculate Cyclomatic Complexity

**Method 1 (Edge-Node):**
$$
V(G) = 11 - 9 + 2(1) = 4
$$

**Method 2 (Predicate):**
$$
V(G) = 3 + 1 = 4
$$

**Method 3 (Regions):**
$$
V(G) = 4
$$

All three methods give $V(G) = 4$, confirming the result.

#### Step 3 — Independent Basis Paths

| Path | Description | Test Input (userId, password) | Expected Return |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P1** | N1→N2(T)→N3(T)→N4→N9 | `(101, "valid")` | `1` |
| **P2** | N1→N2(T)→N3(F)→N5→N9 | `(101, "wrong")` | `-1` |
| **P3** | N1→N2(F)→N6(T)→N7→N9 | `(0, "anything")` | `0` |
| **P4** | N1→N2(F)→N6(F)→N8→N9 | `(-1, "anything")` | `-2` |

**Minimum Test Cases Required = 4** (equal to $V(G)$).

---

## ⭐ **QUESTION B — (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

### **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain **Equivalence Partitioning** and **Boundary Value Analysis** as Black-Box testing techniques. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bank ATM allows cash withdrawal for amounts between **Rs. 500 and Rs. 20,000**, in multiples of **Rs. 100**. Design test cases using EP and BVA techniques. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**Equivalence Partitioning (EP) (3.5 Marks):**
- Divides the input domain into groups (equivalence classes) such that all values in a group are expected to behave identically.
- The system is assumed to treat all members of a class the same way.
- One representative test case per partition suffices.
- *Example:* For an input range [1, 100], the partitions are: < 1, [1, 100], > 100.

**Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) (3.5 Marks):**
- A refinement of EP that focuses on the **boundary edges** of equivalence partitions.
- Defects cluster at boundaries (off-by-one errors).
- For a valid range $[a, b]$, the test points are: $a-1, a, a+1, b-1, b, b+1$.
- *Example:* For range [1, 100], the BVA test points are: 0, 1, 2, 99, 100, 101.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Constraints:**
- Valid Range: $500 \leq \text{amount} \leq 20{,}000$
- Multiple of Rs. 100
- Input must be a positive integer

#### Step 1 — Equivalence Partitions (3.5 Marks)

| Partition | Class Description | Example Value |
|---|---|---|
| EP1 (Valid) | $500 \leq \text{amount} \leq 20{,}000$ and amount % 100 == 0 | `Rs. 5000` |
| EP2 (Invalid) | amount < 500 | `Rs. 200` |
| EP3 (Invalid) | amount > 20,000 | `Rs. 25,000` |
| EP4 (Invalid) | Not a multiple of 100 | `Rs. 5,050` |
| EP5 (Invalid) | Negative or zero | `Rs. -500`, `Rs. 0` |

#### Step 2 — Boundary Value Analysis (3.5 Marks)

Valid range: $[500, 20{,}000]$. Critical BVA points: $\{499, 500, 501, 19{,}999, 20{,}000, 20{,}001\}$

| Test ID | Input (Rs.) | Multiples of 100? | Expected Result | Technique |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TC1 | 499 | Yes | INVALID (below min) | BVA (lower-1) |
| TC2 | 500 | Yes | VALID (min) | BVA (lower) |
| TC3 | 501 | No | INVALID (not multiple) | BVA (lower+1) |
| TC4 | 5,000 | Yes | VALID (representative) | EP |
| TC5 | 19,999 | No | INVALID (not multiple) | BVA (upper-1) |
| TC6 | 20,000 | Yes | VALID (max) | BVA (upper) |
| TC7 | 20,001 | No | INVALID (above max) | BVA (upper+1) |
| TC8 | 0 | — | INVALID (zero) | EP |
| TC9 | -1000 | — | INVALID (negative) | EP |
| TC10 | 5,050 | No | INVALID (not multiple) | EP |

> **Coverage:** All 5 equivalence classes + 6 boundary points = **10 comprehensive test cases**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Never write `V(G) = E - N + 2`** (missing `P`!) — Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this common error. Always include `2P` or `2(1)`.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse branch coverage with path coverage** — Branch coverage = 100% of True/False outcomes; Path coverage = every independent path. Branch coverage is **weaker** than path coverage.
> 3. **BVA requires 6 test points**, not 2 — many students write only `{a, b}` and miss the off-by-one points. Examiners check the full set $\{a-1, a, a+1, b-1, b, b+1\}$.
> 4. **Equivalence Partitioning ≠ Boundary Value Analysis** — they are complementary, not identical. EP tests *inside* partitions; BVA tests *at the edges*.
> 5. **Grey-Box is NOT "average of black and white box"** — it is a *strategic hybrid* where limited internal info is used to design smarter functional tests.
> 6. **Always draw the CFG in long-answer questions** — Examiners award **2 marks** explicitly for the diagram. Skipping it costs heavily.
> 7. **Use proper V(G) notation** — never write `V of G` in plain English. LaTeX `$V(G)$` is the KTU standard.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

- ⭐ **Black-Box** = "What" the system does (behavior, requirements, inputs/outputs).
- ⭐ **White-Box** = "How" the system does it (internal logic, paths, conditions).
- ⭐ **Grey-Box** = Hybrid with **partial** internal knowledge.
- ⭐ **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G)$ = **minimum number of independent paths = minimum test cases for branch coverage**.
- ⭐ **Three formulas:** $V(G) = E - N + 2P = P + 1 = R$. All must yield the **same value** (verification check).
- ⭐ **Coverage Hierarchy (weakest → strongest):** Statement → Branch → Condition → Multiple Condition → Path → LCSAJ.
- ⭐ **BVA test points** for valid range $[a, b]$: $\{a-1, a, a+1, b-1, b, b+1\}$ — **6 points total**.
- ⭐ **Equivalence Partitioning** divides input domain into disjoint classes; **one test per class**.
- ⭐ **Decision Tables** handle combinations of inputs and corresponding actions.
- ⭐ **State Transition Testing** is for systems with finite states (ATM, login systems).
- ⭐ **Cause-Effect Graphing** is a hybrid that links causes (inputs) to effects (outputs) using Boolean logic.
- ⭐ **Grey-Box is ideal for** integration testing, penetration testing, API testing, distributed systems.
- ⭐ **KTU 2024 mapping:** White-Box = Unit/Integration phase; Black-Box = System/Acceptance phase; Grey-Box = Integration/Pentest.
- ⭐ **Tools:** Black-Box → Selenium, QTP, JMeter; White-Box → JUnit, NUnit, Emma, Cobertura; Grey-Box → Postman, Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP.
- ⭐ **For a 14-mark question**, always include: (1) CFG diagram, (2) all three $V(G)$ formulas, (3) basis path table, (4) test case design.
- ⭐ **V(G) > 10** = high-risk module; refactor recommended.
- ⭐ **MCQ trap:** Black-Box testing CANNOT detect dead code — only White-Box can.
- ⭐ **MCQ trap:** Statement coverage is the **weakest** white-box metric, not the strongest.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automation in Testing - Introduction to automation tools (e.g., Selenium, Cypress, JUnit)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Automation in Testing - Introduction to automation tools (e.g., Selenium, Cypress, JUnit)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Test Automation** is the systematic use of specialized software tools, frameworks, and scripts to execute test cases automatically, compare actual outcomes with predicted outcomes, and generate reproducible test reports without continuous human intervention. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context (PECST631 – Software Testing, Module 1), automation is positioned as the **strategic evolution** of the **Software Testing Life Cycle (STLC)** that addresses the limitations of manual testing in agile, continuous-integration environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *Test Automation is the application of software engineering principles to the testing process itself, where test activities (execution, verification, regression, and reporting) are encoded into executable artifacts that can be re-run deterministically across builds.*

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Automation Tools Covered in KTU Module 1

| Tool | Category | Primary Purpose | KTU Module 1 Scope |
|------|----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Selenium** | Web UI Automation | Cross-browser functional testing | Introduction + Architecture |
| **Cypress** | Modern Web E2E Framework | In-browser end-to-end testing | Introduction + Comparison |
| **JUnit** | Unit Testing Framework | Java-level xUnit testing | Introduction + Annotations |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Robot Chef" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine you run a restaurant where every dish must pass a **taste test** before being served.
>
> - **Manual Testing** = A human chef repeatedly tastes 200 dishes every evening — slow, inconsistent, and exhausting.
> - **Test Automation** = You install a **robotic tongue** sensor that *reliably* checks temperature, salt level, and plating for every dish, every night, at machine speed.
>
> - **Selenium** is the *versatile robotic arm* that can test dishes on *any* plate (browser: Chrome, Firefox, Edge).
> - **Cypress** is the *modern AI tasting station* that lives *inside* the kitchen (inside the browser) and gives instant feedback.
> - **JUnit** is the *ingredient-level quality checker* — it verifies each raw ingredient (unit/component) *before* cooking even begins.

### 1.4 Why KTU Introduces Automation in Module 1

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (CO1)**, students must be able to *"identify, formulate, and solve complex engineering problems in software testing by selecting appropriate automation tools."* Therefore, Module 1 establishes the **taxonomy of tools** before moving to test design techniques in later modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metrics in Automation (Bolded Constants/Standards):**
> - **Automation Coverage Ratio (ACR)** – percentage of test cases automated.
> - **Regression Defect Density** – defects found per KLOC after automation.
> - **Industry Benchmark ROI Threshold:** automation becomes profitable when re-execution count exceeds **5 cycles** (industry rule of thumb cited in KTU reference material).
> - **Selenium W3C WebDriver Standard:** W3C Recommendation since **2018** — this is the formal protocol browsers expose for automation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Anatomy of a Test Automation Framework

Every automation tool discussed in KTU Module 1 follows a **four-layer operational stack**:

1. **Test Specification Layer** — Where test cases are authored (Java/Python for Selenium, JS for Cypress, Java for JUnit).
2. **Test Execution Layer** — The runner/compiler that interprets scripts and executes them against the **System Under Test (SUT)**.
3. **Locator/Driver Layer** — The mechanism that identifies UI elements (Selenium uses **WebDriver + Locators**; Cypress uses **CSS/XPath + jQuery selectors**; JUnit uses **Java object references**).
4. **Reporting Layer** — Generates **pass/fail logs**, **screenshots**, and **CI dashboards** (Extent Reports, Allure, Mochawesome).

### 2.2 Deep Dive: Selenium

**Selenium** is a *suite* of tools, not a single tool. KTU Module 1 expects students to recognize all four components:

- **Selenium IDE** – Browser plugin for *record-and-playback* prototyping (Firefox/Chrome).
- **Selenium WebDriver** – The W3C-standard API for **programmatic browser control**. This is the heart of the suite.
- **Selenium Grid** – Enables **parallel + distributed execution** across machines.
- **Selenium Manager** (introduced in Selenium 4) – Auto-downloads browser drivers.

**Why it works:** Selenium uses the **JSON Wire Protocol** (Selenium 3) and the **W3C WebDriver Protocol** (Selenium 4) to translate commands from your test script into native browser calls via browser-specific drivers (e.g., `chromedriver.exe`, `geckodriver`).

### 2.3 Deep Dive: Cypress

**Cypress** is a **next-generation front-end testing tool** built specifically for modern web applications. Its architecture is fundamentally different from Selenium:

- It executes **inside the browser** (not as an out-of-process driver).
- It uses **Node.js** as the runtime.
- It provides **automatic waiting**, **time-travel debugging**, and **real-time reloads**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (High KTU Yield):**
> - *Selenium* drives the browser **from outside** (client-server).
> - *Cypress* runs **inside the browser**, giving it native access to `window`, `document`, and DOM events.

### 2.4 Deep Dive: JUnit

**JUnit** is the **de facto Java unit-testing framework**, an instance of the broader **xUnit family**. KTU Module 1 emphasizes:

- **Version JUnit 5 (Jupiter)** is the current standard (since 2017).
- It is **annotation-driven**, not script-driven.
- It forms the basis of **Test-Driven Development (TDD)** in Java.

### 2.5 Comparative Analysis – KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is board-exam-ready memorizable content. It carries high marks in Part A questions.**

| Parameter | **Selenium** | **Cypress** | **JUnit** |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Testing Level** | Integration / System | End-to-End (E2E) | Unit |
| **Primary Language** | Java, Python, C#, Ruby, JS | JavaScript / TypeScript | Java |
| **Execution Architecture** | Out-of-process (client–server) | In-browser (in-process) | In-JVM (in-process) |
| **Browser Support** | Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari, Opera | Chrome, Edge, Firefox, Electron | N/A (runs on JVM) |
| **Parallel Execution** | Via Selenium Grid | Built-in (via CI config) | Built-in (parallel runner) |
| **Locator Strategy** | XPath, CSS, ID, Name | CSS, XPath, jQuery, `data-cy` | Java object references |
| **Reporting** | External (Extent, Allure) | Built-in (Mochawesome, Cypress Dashboard) | Built-in (XML, HTML via Maven Surefire) |
| **License** | Apache 2.0 (Open Source) | MIT (Open Source) | EPL 2.0 (Open Source) |
| **Initial Release** | 2004 (Selenium 1), 2016 (Selenium 3), 2021 (Selenium 4) | 2017 | 2002 (JUnit 4), 2017 (JUnit 5) |
| **CI/CD Integration** | Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI | Jenkins, CircleCI, GitHub Actions | Jenkins, Maven, Gradle |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility and Industry Relevance

- **Selenium** dominates legacy enterprise testing — used by Google, Netflix, LinkedIn for cross-browser regression.
- **Cypress** is the preferred framework for **modern SPA (Single Page Applications)** built with React, Angular, Vue — used by Daimler, NASA JPL, Shopify.
- **JUnit** underpins almost every Java enterprise application — integrated with **Spring Boot**, **Maven**, **Gradle**, **IntelliJ IDEA**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The Automation ROI Formula (KTU High-Yield)

Test automation is an **engineering investment**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to derive when automation pays off.

**Step 1 — Define Manual Cost per Execution**

Let the manual execution cost per cycle be denoted as $C_m$, including labour, infrastructure, and opportunity cost.

$$C_m = H \cdot R \cdot T_m$$

where:

- $H$ = number of human testers
- $R$ = hourly rate in INR/USD
- $T_m$ = average hours per manual cycle

**Step 2 — Define Automation Cost per Execution**

Let $C_a$ be the per-cycle automation cost after the initial build.

$$C_a = (H_a \cdot R \cdot T_a) + L_c$$

where:

- $H_a$ = human effort hours for script maintenance
- $T_a$ = maintenance time per cycle
- $L_c$ = licensing / infrastructure cost (often **0** for open-source tools)

**Step 3 — Initial Setup Cost**

Let $I$ be the one-time investment to design, develop, and stabilize the automation suite.

$$I = H_s \cdot R \cdot T_s + C_{tool}$$

where $T_s$ = scripting hours, $C_{tool}$ = tool licensing cost.

**Step 4 — The Breakeven Equation**

Automation becomes profitable at the cycle count $n$ where cumulative savings exceed cumulative cost.

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} C_m \;=\; I \;+\; \sum_{k=1}^{n} C_a$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Breakeven Cycle**

Since manual and automation costs are constant per cycle (assume linearity):

$$n \cdot C_m = I + n \cdot C_a$$

Rearranging:

$$n \cdot (C_m - C_a) = I$$

$$\boxed{\,n \;=\; \dfrac{I}{C_m - C_a}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)**

Suppose:

- $I$ = **₹2,00,000** (one-time scripting cost)
- $C_m$ = **₹40,000** per cycle (manual team)
- $C_a$ = **₹8,000** per cycle (automation maintenance + infra)

Then:

$$n = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{40{,}000 - 8{,}000} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{32{,}000} = 6.25$$

Therefore, automation breaks even at the **7th cycle** — every cycle after that yields **net savings of ₹32,000**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This mathematical result is why the industry rule of thumb (cited earlier) of *"automate when test cases run more than 5 cycles"* holds — it matches the empirical breakeven model.

### 3.2 The Automation Test Pyramid (KTU Analytical Matrix)

Mike Cohn's **Test Automation Pyramid** is a foundational analytical model the KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to draw and explain.

$$
\text{Automation Pyramid} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Unit Tests (Base)} & \Rightarrow \text{JUnit, TestNG, NUnit} \\
\text{Service/API Tests (Middle)} & \Rightarrow \text{RestAssured, Postman, SoapUI} \\
\text{UI/E2E Tests (Top)} & \Rightarrow \text{Selenium, Cypress, Playwright}
\end{cases}
$$

The pyramid structure encodes the following engineering principles:

- **Base = widest layer** — unit tests are the most numerous, fastest, and cheapest.
- **Middle = medium layer** — service tests validate business logic without UI overhead.
- **Top = narrowest layer** — UI tests are slow, brittle, and expensive, so they should be fewer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfall:** Students often invert this pyramid (writing too many UI tests). KTU examiners specifically deduct marks if the student does not justify the pyramid shape with execution-time and cost data.

### 3.3 Locator Strategy Derivation (Selenium vs Cypress)

The choice of locator directly impacts **script stability** — a key grading criterion in KTU practical examinations.

**Stability Score Formula (qualitative engineering metric):**

$$S_{loc} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Change Probability of Locator}}$$

| Locator Type | Change Probability | Stability $S_{loc}$ | Recommendation |
|--------------|--------------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| `id` | Low | **High** | Preferred |
| `name` | Low-Medium | **High** | Preferred |
| `data-cy` (Cypress) | Very Low | **Very High** | Best Practice |
| CSS Selector | Medium | Medium | Acceptable |
| XPath (absolute) | High | **Low** | Avoid |
| XPath (relative) | Medium | Medium | Use carefully |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Selenium WebDriver Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TS[Test Script - Java, Python, or C#]
    WB[Selenium Client Library - Language Bindings]
    WP[W3C WebDriver Protocol - JSON over HTTP]
    DRV1[ChromeDriver]
    DRV2[GeckoDriver - Firefox]
    DRV3[EdgeDriver]
    BR1[Chrome Browser]
    BR2[Firefox Browser]
    BR3[Edge Browser]
    SUT[Web Application Under Test]

    TS --> WB
    WB --> WP
    WP --> DRV1
    WP --> DRV2
    WP --> DRV3
    DRV1 --> BR1
    DRV2 --> BR2
    DRV3 --> BR3
    BR1 --> SUT
    BR2 --> SUT
    BR3 --> SUT
```

### 4.2 Cypress Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TSC[Test File - JavaScript or TypeScript]
    NW[Node.js Process - Cypress Runner]
    BROWSER[Browser - In-Process Execution]
    APP[Application Under Test - AUT]
    PROXY[Cypress Proxy Server - Network Control]
    REPORTER[Built-in Reporter - Mochawesome, Spec, JSON]

    TSC --> NW
    NW --> BROWSER
    BROWSER --> APP
    NW --> PROXY
    PROXY --> BROWSER
    BROWSER --> REPORTER
```

### 4.3 JUnit 5 Test Lifecycle (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ANNO1[Class Declaration - TestClass]
    ANNO2[BeforeAll - Setup once per class]
    ANNO3[BeforeEach - Setup before every test]
    TEST1[Test 1 - TestMethod One]
    ANNO3a[BeforeEach - Setup before every test]
    TEST2[Test 2 - TestMethod Two]
    ANNO4[AfterEach - Teardown after every test]
    ANNO5[AfterAll - Teardown once per class]
    REPORT[Test Report - XML, HTML]

    ANNO1 --> ANNO2
    ANNO2 --> ANNO3
    ANNO3 --> TEST1
    TEST1 --> ANNO3a
    ANNO3a --> TEST2
    TEST2 --> ANNO4
    ANNO4 --> ANNO5
    ANNO5 --> REPORT
```

### 4.4 Comparison Workflow – How the Three Tools Work Together

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ[Requirement Document]
    TC[Manual Test Case Suite]
    J1[JUnit - Unit Tests on Java Classes]
    S1[Selenium - UI Regression Tests]
    C1[Cypress - E2E Modern UI Tests]
    CI[CI Server - Jenkins or GitHub Actions]
    DASH[Dashboard - Allure or Cypress Dashboard]
    GO[Go or No-Go Release Decision]

    REQ --> TC
    TC --> J1
    TC --> S1
    TC --> C1
    J1 --> CI
    S1 --> CI
    C1 --> CI
    CI --> DASH
    DASH --> GO
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define test automation. List any two advantages of test automation over manual testing.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Test automation is the use of specialized software tools and scripts to control the execution of tests, compare actual outcomes with expected outcomes, and generate test reports automatically, without human intervention.
>
> **Advantages (any two):**
> 1. **Faster Regression Execution** — automated suites run orders of magnitude faster than humans, enabling daily or hourly CI cycles.
> 2. **Reusability & Repeatability** — once authored, a script can be executed thousands of times with identical precision.
> 3. **Improved Defect Detection** — automation can detect subtle regressions humans miss, particularly in numeric, date, and boundary value calculations.
> 4. **Reduced Long-Term Cost** — despite high initial investment, automation lowers the *cost-per-defect-found* over multiple cycles.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*Compare Selenium and Cypress based on architecture and execution model.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **Selenium** | **Cypress** |
|--------|--------------|-------------|
| **Architecture** | Client–Server, out-of-process | In-browser, in-process |
| **Execution Model** | Communicates via W3C WebDriver protocol | Runs inside the browser's own JS runtime |
| **Browser Access** | Indirect (through drivers) | Direct DOM access via the browser |

Selenium drives the browser externally using the W3C WebDriver protocol, while Cypress executes *inside* the browser, giving it native access to DOM events, `window`, and `document` objects, enabling features like time-travel debugging and automatic waits.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of Selenium WebDriver with a neat block diagram. Discuss how the W3C WebDriver protocol enables cross-browser testing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe JUnit 5 testing framework. Explain any four key annotations with their execution order in the JUnit test lifecycle. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Selenium WebDriver Architecture:**

Selenium WebDriver follows a **client–server architecture** with the following components:

1. **Test Script Layer** — Test cases are written in Java, Python, C#, Ruby, or JavaScript using language-specific bindings.
2. **Selenium Client Library** — Provides a high-level API (e.g., `WebDriver driver = new ChromeDriver();`).
3. **W3C WebDriver Protocol** — A standardized HTTP/JSON-based communication protocol adopted by all major browsers since 2018.
4. **Browser Drivers** — Native executables (`chromedriver`, `geckodriver`, `msedgedriver`) that translate W3C commands into browser-specific automation calls.
5. **Browsers** — The actual web browsers where tests run.
6. **SUT (System Under Test)** — The web application being validated.

**Cross-browser mechanism:**
Because every modern browser vendor exposes the same W3C WebDriver protocol endpoint, the **same test script** can drive Chrome, Firefox, Edge, and Safari without code changes. Selenium Grid extends this by allowing **parallel, distributed execution** across machines.

> **Block Diagram:** (Refer to *Section 4.1* above — Selenium WebDriver Architecture.)

**(b) JUnit 5 Annotations and Lifecycle:**

JUnit 5 (codename **Jupiter**) is annotation-driven. Four key annotations are:

| Annotation | Scope | Execution Timing |
|------------|-------|------------------|
| `@BeforeAll` | Static method | Once **before all** test methods in the class |
| `@BeforeEach` | Instance method | Once **before each** `@Test` method |
| `@Test` | Instance method | The actual test method |
| `@AfterEach` | Instance method | Once **after each** `@Test` method |
| `@AfterAll` | Static method | Once **after all** test methods in the class |

**Lifecycle Order:**

```
@BeforeAll → @BeforeEach → @Test → @AfterEach → @BeforeEach → @Test → @AfterEach → @AfterAll
```

This sequence guarantees a clean test environment for every test method and a single global setup/teardown for resource-intensive operations like database connections.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss Cypress as a modern web automation tool. Compare it with Selenium in terms of architecture, execution speed, debugging support, and language support. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable engineering example, derive the formula for the **breakeven cycle** of test automation. When does automation become more economical than manual testing? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cypress vs Selenium – Detailed Comparison:**

| Parameter | Cypress | Selenium |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| **Architecture** | In-browser, runs inside the same JS runtime as the AUT | Client–server, uses W3C WebDriver protocol |
| **Execution Speed** | Faster (no driver marshalling overhead) | Slower (HTTP round-trips to drivers) |
| **Debugging** | Time-travel debugging, snapshots at every step, DevTools integration | Relies on screenshots and external loggers |
| **Language Support** | JavaScript / TypeScript only | Java, Python, C#, Ruby, JavaScript, Kotlin |
| **Auto-Wait** | Built-in (waits for elements and network calls automatically) | Manual waits required (`WebDriverWait`) |
| **Multi-Tab Support** | Limited | Full support |
| **Parallel Execution** | Built-in via CI configuration | Requires Selenium Grid |

Cypress is best suited for **modern SPA-based web applications** (React, Angular, Vue) where its in-browser execution model provides superior speed and debugging, while Selenium remains the better choice for **legacy enterprise applications** requiring broad language support and cross-browser depth.

**(b) Breakeven Cycle Derivation:**

*(The complete derivation is provided in Section 3.1 above; the student must reproduce all five steps.)*

**Recap of the final equation:**

$$n = \dfrac{I}{C_m - C_a}$$

where $I$ = one-time scripting investment, $C_m$ = manual cost per cycle, $C_a$ = automation cost per cycle.

**Engineering Example:** Suppose a startup invests ₹2,00,000 in Selenium automation. Manual testing costs ₹40,000/cycle, automation maintenance costs ₹8,000/cycle. Then $n = 2{,}00{,}000 / 32{,}000 = 6.25$. Automation breaks even at the **7th cycle**.

**Conclusion:** Automation becomes economical whenever the projected number of regression cycles exceeds the breakeven point $n$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse Cypress with Selenium** — many students write that Cypress is "a tool inside Selenium." It is **NOT**. They are independent projects with different architectures.
> 2. **JUnit is for unit testing, not UI testing** — students often wrongly describe JUnit as a Selenium alternative. They serve different layers of the test pyramid.
> 3. **Cite the version** — KTU 2024 expects **Selenium 4**, **Cypress 13+**, **JUnit 5** (Jupiter). Older versions will cost marks.
> 4. **In breakeven derivation, students often forget the $L_c$ (licensing) term** — for open-source tools it is **zero**, but the equation must still be acknowledged.
> 5. **Diagrams must be neat and labelled** — unlabelled block diagrams lose 2–3 marks in Part B.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Test Automation** = executing tests via software tools without human intervention.
- **Selenium** = Web UI automation; **Cypress** = Modern E2E in-browser automation; **JUnit** = Java unit-testing framework.
- Selenium uses **W3C WebDriver Protocol** (since 2018) for cross-browser automation.
- Cypress runs **inside** the browser → automatic waiting, time-travel debugging.
- JUnit 5 (Jupiter) lifecycle order: `@BeforeAll` → `@BeforeEach` → `@Test` → `@AfterEach` → `@AfterAll`.
- The **Automation ROI breakeven formula** is $n = I / (C_m - C_a)$.
- Industry rule of thumb: **automate when test cases will be executed more than 5 times.**
- The **Test Automation Pyramid** places unit tests at the base (widest, fastest, cheapest) and UI tests at the top (narrowest, slowest, most brittle).
- Locator stability ranking: `data-cy` (Cypress) > `id` > `name` > CSS > absolute XPath.
- All three tools are **open-source** and integrate with **CI/CD pipelines** (Jenkins, GitHub Actions).
- Always prefer **relative XPath** over **absolute XPath** for script maintainability.
- The Selenium suite has 4 components: **IDE, WebDriver, Grid, Manager**.
- Cypress is **JavaScript-only**; Selenium supports **6 languages**; JUnit is **Java-only**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study - Automation of Unit Testing and Mutation Testing using JUnit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Study - Automation of Unit Testing and Mutation Testing using JUnit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Unit Testing — The Foundational Pillar

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Unit Testing* is a level of software testing where **individual units/components** of a software (typically functions, methods, or classes) are tested in **isolation** from the rest of the program. The primary purpose is to validate that each unit of the software performs as designed.

A **unit** is the smallest testable part of any software. In Object-Oriented Programming (Java context for this case study), a unit is usually a **public method** belonging to a **class**.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy
> Imagine a car manufacturing assembly line. Before assembling the full car, every critical component — the brake pad, the spark plug, the gear — is tested **individually** on a test bench. If the brake pad fails under heat, you don't wait to discover this when the customer is driving downhill at 100 km/h. **Unit testing is exactly this — testing the smallest cogs before they become an engine.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Unit Testing critical in the KTU 2024 Scheme?**
> - Catches **~70% of defects** at the earliest (and cheapest) stage of the **Software Testing Life Cycle (STLC)**.
> - Forms the **base of the testing pyramid** (Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance).
> - Enables **refactoring with confidence** (a key agile/Agile-DevOps enabler).

---

## 1.2 Automation of Unit Testing — From Manual to Machine

> [!NOTE]
> **Automation of Unit Testing** is the practice of using a **software framework** to programmatically execute test cases, compare actual outcomes against expected outcomes, and report pass/fail results **without manual intervention**.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy
> Doing unit tests manually is like checking the spelling of a 500-page manuscript by hand with a red pen. **Automation** is like running a spell-checker — instant, repeatable, and tireless.

### Key Drivers of Automation
| Driver | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Speed** | Hundreds of tests run in seconds |
| **Repeatability** | Same script run on every code change (CI/CD) |
| **Regression Safety** | Catches breakage in previously working code |
| **Coverage** | Easy to measure **Code Coverage** (Line/Branch) |

---

## 1.3 JUnit — The De-Facto Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **JUnit** is an **open-source, Java-based, xUnit-style unit testing framework** used to write and run repeatable automated tests. The current major version for the KTU 2024 syllabus case study is **JUnit 5 (Jupiter)**.

- **xUnit** → A family of frameworks (JUnit for Java, NUnit for .NET, pytest for Python, etc.) following Kent Beck's original SUnit design.
- **Eclipse Foundation project**, hosted on GitHub.
- Built on top of **Java 8+** features like **Lambda expressions** and **Annotations**.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy
> Think of JUnit as a **scientific laboratory workbench** for a Java developer. The Java code under test is the *experiment sample*, JUnit provides the *test tubes, microscopes, and report cards*, and the developer (tester) designs the *experiments* (test methods).

---

## 1.4 Mutation Testing — The "Tricky Judge"

> [!NOTE]
> **Mutation Testing** is a **fault-based testing technique** that evaluates the **quality of an existing test suite** by intentionally introducing small syntactic changes (called **mutations**) into the program code, and checking whether the test suite can **detect (kill) these mutants**.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy
> Imagine a security guard's alertness test. To verify the guard is awake, the inspector drops harmless "fake intruders" (mutants) into the building. If the guard detects them (test fails → mutant **killed** ✅), the guard is alert. If the guard doesn't react (test passes → mutant **survived** ❌), the guard is sleeping on duty, and the test suite is **weak**.

### Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Mutant** | A deliberately faulted version of the program |
| **Mutation Operator** | The rule used to create mutants (e.g., replace `+` with `-`) |
| **Killed Mutant** | A mutant that causes at least one test to fail |
| **Live Mutant (Survived)** | A mutant for which all tests still pass |
| **Equivalent Mutant** | A mutant that is semantically identical to the original (undetectable by any test) |
| **Mutation Score** | $MS = \frac{Killed}{Killed + Survived + Equivalent} \times 100\%$ |

> [!WARNING]
> A high **Mutation Score** is the strongest empirical evidence that a test suite is **discriminating** and not just a "happy path" coverage exercise.

---

## 1.5 PIT (Pitest) — The Mutation Testing Tool for Java/JUnit

> [!NOTE]
> **PIT (Pitest)** is the most widely used **mutation testing tool for Java** that integrates seamlessly with **JUnit**. It performs **bytecode-level mutation** (fast, no recompilation) and reports mutation coverage in HTML/XML formats.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Anatomy of a JUnit 5 Test Class

A JUnit test class is a plain Java class with a few **standard conventions**:

1. The class is declared `public`.
2. Test methods are annotated with `@Test`.
3. Test methods return `void` and take **no arguments**.
4. Class & methods can be `package-private` (JUnit 5 relaxes visibility rules).

---

## 2.2 The JUnit 5 Lifecycle Annotations (Most Important for KTU)

| Annotation | Purpose | Execution Time |
|---|---|---|
| `@Test` | Marks a method as a test case | Per test method |
| `@BeforeAll` | Runs **once** before all tests in the class | Static method, executes first |
| `@AfterAll` | Runs **once** after all tests in the class | Static method, executes last |
| `@BeforeEach` | Runs **before each** `@Test` method | Per test method |
| `@AfterEach` | Runs **after each** `@Test` method | Per test method |
| `@DisplayName` | Custom human-readable name for the test | N/A (declarative) |
| `@Disabled` | Skips a test | N/A (declarative) |
| `@Nested` | Groups related tests into inner classes | N/A (organizational) |
| `@Tag` | Filters tests by tag (e.g., `"smoke"`, `"regression"`) | N/A (organizational) |

### JUnit 5 Assertion API (Static Methods in `org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions`)

| Method | Use Case |
|---|---|
| `assertEquals(expected, actual)` | Equality check |
| `assertNotEquals(unexpected, actual)` | Inequality check |
| `assertTrue(condition)` | Boolean true check |
| `assertFalse(condition)` | Boolean false check |
| `assertNull(value)` | Null check |
| `assertNotNull(value)` | Non-null check |
| `assertThrows(ExceptionClass, executable)` | Exception thrown check |
| `assertAll(...)` | Group assertions; reports all failures together |

---

## 2.3 Test-Driven Development (TDD) — The Red-Green-Refactor Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TDD** is a development methodology where tests are written **before** the production code. It has three repeating phases:

1. 🔴 **RED** → Write a test for a feature that doesn't exist yet. It fails.
2. 🟢 **GREEN** → Write the *minimum* production code to make the test pass.
3. 🔵 **REFACTOR** → Improve the code (and tests) while keeping them green.

> TDD is the **philosophical foundation** that justifies why we automate unit tests with JUnit in the first place.

---

## 2.4 Mutation Operators (KTU High-Yield List)

These are the **syntactic transformations** applied to source code to create mutants:

| Operator | Symbol | Example (Original → Mutant) |
|---|---|---|
| **AOR** (Arithmetic Operator Replacement) | Change arithmetic op | `a + b` → `a - b` |
| **ROR** (Relational Operator Replacement) | Change relational op | `a < b` → `a \leq b` |
| **COR** (Conditional Operator Replacement) | Change logical op | `a \&\& b` → `a \mid\mid b` |
| **LOR** (Logical Operator Replacement) | Change bitwise/logical | `a \& b` → `a \mid b` |
| **UOI** (Unary Operator Insertion) | Insert `!` or `-` | `x` → `-x` |
| **ABS** (Absolute Value Insertion) | Wrap expr with `Math.abs` | `x` → `Math.abs(x)` |
| **AOD** (Arithmetic Operator Deletion) | Remove an operator | `a + b` → `a b` |
| **CRL** (Constant Replacement) | Replace literal | `0` → `1` |
| **RVM** (Return Value Modification) | Modify return | `return x;` → `return 0;` |
| **STD** (Statement Deletion) | Delete a line | `x = x + 1;` → *(deleted)* |

> [!NOTE]
> **PIT by default applies a curated set of mutation operators** that are effective and fast: `INCREMENTS`, `NEGATE_CONDITIONALS`, `INVERT_NEGATIVES`, `MATH`, `RETURN_VALS`, `VOID_METHOD_CALLS`, `EMPTY_RETURNS`, `FALSE_RETURNS`, `TRUE_RETURNS`, `PRIMITIVE_RETURNS`.

---

## 2.5 The Mutation Score Formula — The Single Most Important Equation

The **Mutation Score (MS)** is the central metric of mutation testing:

$$
MS = \frac{M_{killed}}{M_{killed} + M_{survived} + M_{equivalent}} \times 100\%
$$

| Variable | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $M_{killed}$ | Number of mutants detected (killed) by the test suite |
| $M_{survived}$ | Number of mutants that the test suite failed to detect |
| $M_{equivalent}$ | Mutants that are semantically equivalent to the original (unavoidable) |

> A score of **$\geq 80\%$** is the industry-accepted threshold for a *strong* test suite.

---

## 2.6 The Test Automation Pyramid (For Context)

The position of **Unit Testing** within the broader testing strategy:

$$
\underbrace{\text{Unit Tests}}_{\text{Largest, Fastest, Cheapest}} \;\gg\; \text{Integration} \;\gg\; \text{System} \;\gg\; \underbrace{\text{UI/Acceptance}}_{\text{Smallest, Slowest, Most Expensive}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Companies like Google run **millions** of unit tests daily in CI/CD. Mutation testing (via PIT) is a *meta-test* that audits whether these millions of tests are actually doing their job or are just ceremonial "happy path" placeholders.

# 📙 SECTION 3: Case Study — Step-by-Step Walkthrough (Code & Derivations)

## 3.1 Case Study Setup

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Study:** A banking module computes the **interest on a savings account**. We will (a) automate its unit tests with JUnit 5, and (b) audit the test suite's quality with PIT (mutation testing).

### Production Class — `SavingsAccount.java`

```java
package com.ktu.bank;

public class SavingsAccount {
    private double balance;
    private double rate;          // Annual interest rate (e.g., 0.05 = 5%)

    public SavingsAccount(double initialBalance, double annualRate) {
        if (initialBalance < 0) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Balance cannot be negative");
        }
        if (annualRate < 0) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Rate cannot be negative");
        }
        this.balance = initialBalance;
        this.rate    = annualRate;
    }

    public double getBalance() {
        return balance;
    }

    // Calculates interest for 'years' years (simple interest)
    public double calculateInterest(int years) {
        if (years <= 0) {
            return 0.0;
        }
        return balance * rate * years;
    }

    // Withdraw money; returns true if successful
    public boolean withdraw(double amount) {
        if (amount <= 0) {
            return false;
        }
        if (amount > balance) {
            return false;
        }
        balance = balance - amount;
        return true;
    }
}
```

---

## 3.2 Step 1: Project Setup (Maven `pom.xml` dependencies)

```xml
<dependencies>
    <!-- JUnit 5 -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.junit.jupiter</groupId>
        <artifactId>junit-jupiter</artifactId>
        <version>5.10.2</version>
        <scope>test</scope>
    </dependency>
    <!-- PIT Mutation Testing Plugin (configured under <build>) -->
</dependencies>
```

---

## 3.3 Step 2: Writing the JUnit 5 Test Class

```java
package com.ktu.bank;

import org.junit.jupiter.api.BeforeEach;
import org.junit.jupiter.api.DisplayName;
import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.*;

class SavingsAccountTest {

    private SavingsAccount account;

    @BeforeEach
    void setUp() {
        // Arrange: a fresh account before every test
        account = new SavingsAccount(1000.0, 0.05);
    }

    @Test
    @DisplayName("Interest for 1 year on 1000 @ 5% should be 50")
    void testInterestOneYear() {
        double interest = account.calculateInterest(1);
        assertEquals(50.0, interest, 0.0001);
    }

    @Test
    @DisplayName("Interest for 0 years should be 0")
    void testInterestZeroYears() {
        assertEquals(0.0, account.calculateInterest(0), 0.0001);
    }

    @Test
    @DisplayName("Negative years should return 0 (no negative interest)")
    void testInterestNegativeYears() {
        assertEquals(0.0, account.calculateInterest(-5), 0.0001);
    }

    @Test
    @DisplayName("Withdraw valid amount reduces balance and returns true")
    void testWithdrawValid() {
        boolean result = account.withdraw(200.0);
        assertTrue(result);
        assertEquals(800.0, account.getBalance(), 0.0001);
    }

    @Test
    @DisplayName("Withdraw amount greater than balance should fail")
    void testWithdrawInsufficient() {
        assertFalse(account.withdraw(5000.0));
        assertEquals(1000.0, account.getBalance(), 0.0001);
    }

    @Test
    @DisplayName("Withdraw negative amount should fail")
    void testWithdrawNegative() {
        assertFalse(account.withdraw(-100.0));
    }

    @Test
    @DisplayName("Constructor should reject negative balance")
    void testConstructorNegativeBalance() {
        assertThrows(IllegalArgumentException.class,
                     () -> new SavingsAccount(-1.0, 0.05));
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Explanation of the Test Class

1. **`@BeforeEach setUp()`** → A new `SavingsAccount` with balance=1000, rate=0.05 is created before **each** test. This guarantees **test isolation** — one test cannot pollute another's state.
2. **`assertEquals(expected, actual, delta)`** → The third argument is a *tolerance* for floating-point comparisons (avoids `0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3` surprises).
3. **`assertThrows(...)`** → Tests that the constructor **does** throw when given bad input (validates the defensive guard clause).
4. **`@DisplayName`** → Provides a human-readable description in test reports (visible in IDEs and CI dashboards).

---

## 3.4 Step 3: Running the Tests (Maven Command)

```bash
mvn test
```

**Output (simulated):**
```

[INFO] BUILD SUCCESS
```

> ✅ All 7 tests pass. We have **100% line coverage** (every line is exercised). **But is the test suite *good*?** This is where mutation testing enters.

## 3.5 Step 4: Configure PIT for Mutation Testing

Add the PIT plugin to `pom.xml`:

```xml
<build>
  <plugins>
    <plugin>
      <groupId>org.pitest</groupId>
      <artifactId>pitest-maven</artifactId>
      <version>1.16.1</version>
      <configuration>
        <targetClasses>
          <param>com.ktu.bank.*</param>
        </targetClasses>
        <targetTests>
          <param>com.ktu.bank.*Test</param>
        </targetTests>
      </configuration>
    </plugin>
  </plugins>
</build>
```

Run mutation analysis:

```bash
mvn org.pitest:pitest-maven:mutationCoverage
```

---

## 3.6 Step 5: Interpreting the PIT Mutation Report

PIT generates a report at `target/pit-reports/index.html`. A sample summary:

| Class | Line Coverage | Mutation Score |
|---|---|---|
| `SavingsAccount` | 100% | **71%** |

### Detailed Mutant Analysis (Excerpt)

| Mutant # | Operator | Original | Mutated | Killed? | Test that killed it |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NEGATE_CONDITIONALS | `if (years <= 0)` | `if (!(years <= 0))` | ✅ Yes | `testInterestZeroYears` |
| 2 | NEGATE_CONDITIONALS | `if (amount <= 0)` | `if (!(amount <= 0))` | ✅ Yes | `testWithdrawNegative` |
| 3 | NEGATE_CONDITIONALS | `if (amount > balance)` | `if (!(amount > balance))` | ✅ Yes | `testWithdrawInsufficient` |
| 4 | MATH | `balance * rate * years` | `balance + rate + years` | ✅ Yes | `testInterestOneYear` |
| 5 | RETURN_VALS | `return 0.0;` | `return 1.0;` | ✅ Yes | `testInterestZeroYears` |
| 6 | INVERT_NEGATIVES | `if (initialBalance < 0)` | `if (initialBalance >= 0)` | ❌ **SURVIVED** | *(no test)* |
| 7 | INVERT_NEGATIVES | `if (annualRate < 0)` | `if (annualRate >= 0)` | ❌ **SURVIVED** | *(no test)* |
| 8 | VOID_METHOD_CALLS | `balance = balance - amount;` | *(deleted)* | ❌ **SURVIVED** | *(no test verifies balance after)* |

---

## 3.7 Step 6: Mutation Score Calculation (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> Let's say PIT generated **14 mutants** in total: 10 killed, 2 survived, 2 equivalent. We compute the score as follows.

$$
M_{killed} = 10, \quad M_{survived} = 2, \quad M_{equivalent} = 2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
MS &= \frac{M_{killed}}{M_{killed} + M_{survived} + M_{equivalent}} \times 100\% \\
   &= \frac{10}{10 + 2 + 2} \times 100\% \\
   &= \frac{10}{14} \times 100\% \\
   &= 0.71428\ldots \times 100\% \\
   &= 71.43\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The score of **71.43%** is **below the 80% industry threshold**. The team must add more tests to kill the surviving mutants.

---

## 3.8 Step 7: Improving the Test Suite to Kill Surviving Mutants

Add the following tests to eliminate the surviving mutants:

```java
@Test
@DisplayName("Constructor should reject negative rate")
void testConstructorNegativeRate() {
    assertThrows(IllegalArgumentException.class,
                 () -> new SavingsAccount(1000.0, -0.05));
}

@Test
@DisplayName("Withdraw exactly equal to balance should succeed and set balance to 0")
void testWithdrawExactBalance() {
    boolean result = account.withdraw(1000.0);
    assertTrue(result);
    assertEquals(0.0, account.getBalance(), 0.0001);
}
```

Re-running PIT now:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MS_{new} &= \frac{12}{12 + 0 + 2} \times 100\% \\
         &= \frac{12}{14} \times 100\% \\
         &= 85.71\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ Score now **85.71%** → Above the 80% threshold. Test suite is now demonstrably **strong**.

---

## 3.9 Analytical Matrix — JUnit vs. Mutation Testing

| Aspect | JUnit Unit Testing | Mutation Testing (PIT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Verify functional correctness of units | Evaluate the *quality* of the test suite |
| **Input** | Source code + test cases | Source code + test cases + mutation operators |
| **Output** | Pass/Fail per test | Mutation Score, list of killed/survived mutants |
| **Phase** | Development / CI | Post-development / Pre-release audit |
| **When to Use** | Always (TDD / continuous) | Periodically (e.g., nightly builds) |
| **Cost** | Low (milliseconds per test) | High (each mutant = a full test run) |
| **KTU Bloom's Level** | Apply (L3) | Analyze / Evaluate (L4–L5) |

```mermaid
    E --> F["@BeforeEach: Setup before Test 2"]
    F --> G["@Test Method 2 runs"]
    G --> H["@AfterEach: Cleanup after Test 2"]
    H --> I["@AfterAll: One-time teardown (static)"]
```

> **Read this as:** The blue nodes (`@BeforeAll`, `@AfterAll`) execute **once** per class. The pink nodes (`@BeforeEach`, `@AfterEach`) form a **sandwich around every green `@Test` method**.

## 4.2 Mutation Testing Workflow (PIT)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Production Source Code] --> M[PIT Engine]
    T1[JUnit Test Suite] --> M
    M --> M1[Apply Mutation Operators to Bytecode]
    M1 --> M2[Generate Mutant 1]
    M1 --> M3[Generate Mutant 2]
    M1 --> Mn[Generate Mutant N]
    M2 --> R1{Run Test Suite}
    M3 --> R2{Run Test Suite}
    Mn --> Rn{Run Test Suite}
    R1 -->|Test Fails| K1[Killed Mutant]
    R1 -->|Test Passes| S1[Survived Mutant]
    R2 -->|Test Fails| K2[Killed Mutant]
    R2 -->|Test Passes| S2[Survived Mutant]
    Rn -->|Test Fails| Kn[Killed Mutant]
    Rn -->|Test Passes| Sn[Survived Mutant]
    K1 --> S[Compute Mutation Score]
    K2 --> S
    Kn --> S
    S1 --> S
    S2 --> S
    Sn --> S
    S --> R[Mutation Report]
    S --> D{MS greater than 80 percent?}
    D -->|Yes| OK[Test Suite is Strong]
    D -->|No| ACT[Add More Tests to Kill Survivors]
    ACT --> T1

    style P1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000

    style S1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000

    style Sn fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OK fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ACT fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Key insight:** The "Mutate → Run → Decide" cycle is a **feedback loop** that iteratively hardens the test suite.

---

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> RED
    RED: Write Failing Test
```

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (3 Marks, Bloom: Remember/Understand)**
**Differentiate between *Manual Unit Testing* and *Automated Unit Testing* using JUnit. List any two advantages of the latter.**

#### ✅ Model Answer:
- **Manual Unit Testing:** Test cases are executed by a human tester; results are recorded by hand. **Time-consuming, error-prone, non-repetitive**.
- **Automated Unit Testing (JUnit):** Test cases are written as code; the JUnit framework executes them. **Fast, repeatable, self-validating**.
- **Advantages:** (1) Enables **Continuous Integration (CI)** — tests run on every commit. (2) Provides **automated pass/fail reports** with no human bias or fatigue.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (3 Marks, Bloom: Understand)**
**Define the term *mutant* in mutation testing. What is an *equivalent mutant*? Why is it considered a limitation of the technique?**

#### ✅ Model Answer:
- A **mutant** is a deliberately faulted copy of the original program, produced by applying a small syntactic change (mutation operator).
- An **equivalent mutant** is one that is **semantically identical** to the original program — it produces the *exact same output* for *every possible input* — but syntactically different. Hence, **no test** can ever kill it.
- **Limitation:** Equivalent mutants inflate the denominator of the mutation score formula, artificially **lowering the score** even when the test suite is strong. Detecting them often requires manual human analysis.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks with two sub-parts (7 + 7).

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted] (14 Marks, Bloom: Apply/Analyze)**

**(a)** Explain the **JUnit 5 test lifecycle** with the help of a neat diagram. Describe the purpose of any **four lifecycle annotations** with a code snippet. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following Java method:
```java
public int divide(int a, int b) {
    if (b == 0) throw new ArithmeticException("Divide by zero");
    return a / b;
}
```
Write a complete **JUnit 5 test class** to test this method for: (i) a **valid division** (e.g., 10/2 = 5), (ii) **division resulting in zero** (5/10 = 0), and (iii) the **exception** thrown when dividing by zero. Use appropriate assertions. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a):

The JUnit 5 lifecycle consists of a one-time **class-level setup/teardown** and a per-test **method-level setup/teardown**.

**Four key annotations:**

| Annotation | Purpose | Code Snippet |
|---|---|---|
| `@BeforeAll` | Static method that runs **once** before all tests. Used for expensive resources (DB connections). | `@BeforeAll static void init() { conn = DriverManager.getConnection(url); }` |
| `@BeforeEach` | Runs **before each** test. Used to reset state. | `@BeforeEach void setUp() { list = new ArrayList<>(); }` |
| `@AfterEach` | Runs **after each** test. Used for cleanup. | `@AfterEach void tearDown() { list.clear(); }` |
| `@AfterAll` | Static method that runs **once** after all tests. Used to close expensive resources. | `@AfterAll static void close() { conn.close(); }` |

**Sequence of execution for a class with 2 tests:**
```
@BeforeAll → @BeforeEach → @Test1 → @AfterEach → @BeforeEach → @Test2 → @AfterEach → @AfterAll
```

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b):

```java
import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.*;

class DivisionTest {

    @Test
    void testValidDivision() {
        assertEquals(5, divide(10, 2), "10/2 should be 5");
    }

    @Test
    void testDivisionResultingInZero() {
        assertEquals(0, divide(5, 10), "5/10 should be 0");
    }

    @Test
    void testDivideByZeroThrowsException() {
        assertThrows(ArithmeticException.class, () -> divide(10, 0));
    }

    // The method under test (would normally be in a separate class)
    private int divide(int a, int b) {
        if (b == 0) throw new ArithmeticException("Divide by zero");
        return a / b;
    }
}
```

**Explanation of assertions used:**
- `assertEquals(5, divide(10, 2))` — verifies the computed result equals the expected value `5`.
- `assertEquals(0, divide(5, 10))` — verifies the boundary case where dividend < divisor returns `0` (integer division).
- `assertThrows(ArithmeticException.class, () -> divide(10, 0))` — uses a **lambda executable** to verify the exception is thrown; if no exception is thrown, the test fails.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted] (14 Marks, Bloom: Analyze/Evaluate)**

**(a)** Define **Mutation Testing**. With a suitable example, explain the terms: *Mutation Operator, Killed Mutant, Survived Mutant*, and *Mutation Score*. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the following JUnit test suite for the method `int max(int a, int b) { return (a > b) ? a : b; }`:

```java
@Test
void testMax() {
    assertEquals(5, max(5, 3));
}
```

Apply the **NEGATE_CONDITIONALS** mutation operator. List the mutants generated, state whether each is **killed, survived, or equivalent**, and compute the **Mutation Score**. Suggest **one additional test** to improve the score. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a):

**Definition:** Mutation Testing is a fault-based testing technique that evaluates the quality of a test suite by introducing small syntactic changes (mutations) into the program and observing whether the test suite can detect them.

**Example:** Original: `if (x > 0) y = 1; else y = -1;`

**Mutation Operator** — a rule for creating mutants. Applying `NEGATE_CONDITIONALS` to `x > 0` yields `!(x > 0)` or equivalently `x <= 0`.

**Killed Mutant** — A mutant for which the test suite produces at least one failure. If a test inputs `x = 5`, the mutant's output becomes `y = -1` instead of `1`, causing the test to fail → **killed**.

**Survived Mutant** — A mutant for which all tests pass (test suite failed to detect the fault).

**Mutation Score** — The percentage of mutants killed by the test suite:
$$
MS = \frac{M_{killed}}{M_{killed} + M_{survived} + M_{equivalent}} \times 100\%
$$

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b):

The original code is:
```java
int max(int a, int b) { return (a > b) ? a : b; }
```

Applying **NEGATE_CONDITIONALS** on the condition `a > b` gives the mutant:
```java
int maxMut(int a, int b) { return (a <= b) ? a : b; }
```

**Mutant analysis against `testMax()` (which calls `max(5, 3)`):**

| Mutant | Mutated Condition | Test Input | Original Output | Mutant Output | Test Result | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | `a > b` → `a <= b` | `max(5, 3)` | 5 | 3 | FAIL (expected 5, got 3) | ✅ **Killed** |

**One more mutant may emerge from deleting the ternary, but with a single test we evaluate as follows:**

Assuming PIT reports: $M_{killed} = 1$, $M_{survived} = 0$, $M_{equivalent} = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MS &= \frac{1}{1 + 0 + 0} \times 100\% \\
   &= 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

However, this is misleading — **the test only covers one branch** (the "true" branch of the ternary). To detect mutants affecting the "false" branch (e.g., changing `a` to `b` in the false arm), we need a test for the case where `a <= b`.

**Suggested additional test:**
```java
@Test
void testMaxWhenBIsLarger() {
    assertEquals(5, max(3, 5), "max(3,5) should be 5");
}
```

With this second test, the test suite can detect mutants that flip the ternary branches, and the score becomes more representative of true coverage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully to Avoid Losing Marks):**
> 1. **Confusing Mutation Score with Code Coverage.** They are **not the same**. 100% line coverage can give 30% mutation score. Always mention both separately.
> 2. **Forgetting the equivalent-mutant term** in the mutation score denominator. The correct denominator is $Killed + Survived + Equivalent$, not $Killed + Survived$.
> 3. **Writing `assertEquals` for floating-point without a `delta`**. This loses 1 mark in code questions. Always use the three-argument form: `assertEquals(expected, actual, 0.0001)`.
> 4. **Not using `assertThrows` with a lambda executable** — using a try-catch is *outdated* and loses style marks. Use `assertThrows(ExceptionClass.class, () -> { ... })`.
> 5. **Mixing up `@BeforeEach` with `@BeforeAll`**: `@BeforeAll` must be `static`. Forgetting `static` causes a **compilation error** at runtime, losing all 7 marks in a code question.
> 6. **Calling mutation testing "the same as fuzz testing"** — they are entirely different. Mutation = small targeted syntactic faults; Fuzzing = random/invalid inputs.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Unit Testing** tests the *smallest testable part* (typically a method) in isolation.
- ✅ **JUnit 5 (Jupiter)** is the Java standard; tests are methods annotated with `@Test`.
- ✅ **Lifecycle order:** `@BeforeAll` → `@BeforeEach` → `@Test` → `@AfterEach` → ... → `@AfterAll`.
- ✅ `@BeforeAll` and `@AfterAll` methods **must be static**.
- ✅ Use `assertThrows(Class, Executable)` for exception testing, not try-catch.
- ✅ Use **delta** in `assertEquals` for floating-point: `assertEquals(exp, act, 0.0001)`.
- ✅ **TDD Cycle:** 🔴 Red (failing test) → 🟢 Green (passing code) → 🔵 Refactor.
- ✅ **Mutation Testing** evaluates the *quality* of a test suite by introducing faults (mutants) and checking detection.
- ✅ **Mutant states:** Killed (good — test detected it) | Survived (bad — test missed it) | Equivalent (unavoidable).
- ✅ **Mutation Score** $= \frac{K}{K + S + E} \times 100\%$. Target: **$\geq 80\%$**.
- ✅ **Mutation Operators:** AOR, ROR, COR, LOR, UOI, ABS, CRL, RVM, STD.
- ✅ **PIT (Pitest)** is the de-facto mutation testing tool for Java/JUnit; works at the **bytecode** level for speed.
- ✅ Mutation testing is a **meta-test** — it does not test the *program*; it tests the *tests*.
- ✅ Mutation testing is **expensive** (each mutant = a full test run); run it periodically (e.g., nightly in CI), not on every commit.
- ✅ **Test Automation Pyramid:** Unit (base, most) → Integration → System → Acceptance (apex, fewest).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unit Testing - Static and Dynamic Unit Testing, control flow testing, data flow testing, domain testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unit Testing - Static and Dynamic Unit Testing, control flow testing, data flow testing, domain testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Unit Testing?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECST631, Module 2)**
> *Unit Testing* is the lowest level of software testing performed on **individual software units** (functions, methods, classes, or modules) **in isolation** from the rest of the program, with the primary objective of verifying that each unit performs as designed in its specification.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned outcome-based curriculum, unit testing is positioned as the **first tangible execution-based verification activity** in the V-Model / Waterfall test hierarchy, performed immediately after coding by the developer (white-box testing) before integration testing.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Car Engine Bench Test

Imagine an automotive engineer building a **V8 engine**. Before bolting the engine into a car chassis, the engineer mounts it on a **test bench**, supplies synthetic fuel, oil pressure, and electrical signals, then measures RPM, temperature, and torque at every cylinder.

- The **engine on the bench** = the *unit* (a function/method).
- The **synthetic fuel & signals** = *test inputs / stubs*.
- The **measurements (RPM, torque)** = *assertions / expected outputs*.
- **Finding a faulty cylinder before installing** = the *whole purpose* of unit testing.

This is exactly what unit testing does in software — it isolates a small, testable piece of code and verifies it **before it is integrated** into the bigger system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Unit Testing is Critical in KTU Projects (per Course Outcome CO2)**
> - **Early Defect Detection**: Industry data shows defects fixed at the unit level cost **10–100× less** than those caught after release.
> - **Localized Fault Diagnosis**: Easier to pinpoint a bug when scope is one function.
> - **Refactoring Safety Net**: Allows confident code modification.
> - **Living Documentation**: Acts as executable specification.

---

## 1.3 The Two Grand Branches of Unit Testing

Unit testing, as prescribed by **IEEE 829 / ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119** and taught in KTU's PECST631, is broadly classified into two complementary philosophies:

| Branch | Execution? | Code Runs? | Primary Methods | When Used |
|--------|-----------|------------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Static Unit Testing** | ❌ No | ❌ No | Reviews, inspections, walkthroughs, static analysis (lint, compilers) | **Before** compilation / execution |
| **Dynamic Unit Testing** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | White-box (control-flow, data-flow, domain) and black-box (equivalence, boundary) | **After** code is compiled |

### 1.3.1 Static Unit Testing — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Static Unit Testing)**
> Static Unit Testing is the process of evaluating a software unit's **code, design, or documentation** without executing the program. The objective is to discover defects by **manual reasoning** (reviews/walkthroughs) or **automated tools** (linters, type checkers, formal verifiers).

### 1.3.2 Dynamic Unit Testing — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Dynamic Unit Testing)**
> Dynamic Unit Testing is the process of verifying a software unit by **executing it with sample inputs** and observing outputs. It requires a compiled/executable form of the unit plus a *test harness* or *driver* to feed inputs and capture outputs.

### 1.4 Conceptual Map of Unit Testing (Syllabus Scope)

```
                        UNIT TESTING
                             │
              ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
              │                              │
       STATIC TESTING                DYNAMIC TESTING
       (No Execution)                (With Execution)
              │                              │
   ┌──────────┼──────────┐         ┌─────────┼─────────┐
   │          │          │         │         │         │
 Reviews Walkthroughs Inspec. White-Box    Black-Box   Domain
   │          │          │         │         │         │
 Peer       Informal  Formal   Control Data   Equiv.   Boundary
 Review     Author-   Fagan    Flow    Flow   Partition Tests
            led                Test.  Test.
```

---

## 1.5 Static vs Dynamic — A Side-by-Side Comparison

| Parameter | Static Testing | Dynamic Testing |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| Execution required? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Cost per defect found | **Lower** | **Higher** |
| Defects found early | **Yes** (pre-compilation) | **No** (post-compilation) |
| Skill required | Reading, reasoning, domain knowledge | Analytical + coding |
| Tools | Lint, PMD, Checkstyle, SonarQube | JUnit, PyTest, NUnit |
| KTU Course Outcome | CO2 (Apply) | CO2, CO3 (Analyze) |
| Typical % of bugs caught | **30–70%** (industry stat) | **30–40%** of remaining |
| Verifies *behavior* | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If asked "Differentiate between Static and Dynamic Testing," always include **at least five comparison points** and conclude with the statement: *"Both techniques are complementary; static catches what dynamic cannot (and vice-versa)."* Examiners reward this synthesizing sentence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy — Static Unit Testing

### 2.1.1 Formal Reviews (Fagan Inspection — Michael Fagan, IBM 1976)

The **Fagan Inspection** is a highly structured, **7-stage** review process used widely in industry. KTU frequently asks this as a 7-mark sub-question.

**The 7 Stages:**

1. **Planning** — Moderator prepares materials, selects participants (4–6 inspectors), distributes documents.
2. **Overview** — Author gives a brief walk-through so inspectors gain context.
3. **Individual Preparation** — Each inspector studies the artifact independently and logs defects.
4. **Inspection Meeting** — Inspectors (NOT the author) read code line-by-line; defects are logged, not fixed.
5. **Rework** — Author fixes identified defects.
6. **Follow-up** — Moderator verifies fixes.
7. **Cause-Analysis** *(optional)* — Root-cause the defects for process improvement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fagan Inspection Cardinal Rule:** *Defects are logged, NOT solved during the meeting.* This is a very common KTU question. Examiners deduct marks if you say "the team fixes bugs in the meeting."

### 2.1.2 Walkthroughs (Informal, Author-led)

- Led by the **author**, not a moderator.
- Goal is to **educate** and **discover** defects through discussion.
- Audience asks questions, suggests alternatives.
- **No formal metrics** (unlike inspection).

### 2.1.3 Technical Reviews (Semi-formal)

- Conducted by a **team of peers + technical experts**.
- Includes **inspection-like metrics** but with less rigid structure.
- Focuses on **technical correctness**, design choices, standards conformance.

### 2.1.4 Static Analysis (Automated)

Automated tools parse the source code without executing it. Categories:

| Tool Class | What it Detects | Examples |
|-----------|-----------------|----------|
| **Syntax/Lexical** | Typos, missing semicolons, bracket mismatch | `gcc -Wall`, ESLint |
| **Type Checkers** | Type mismatches, undefined variables | TypeScript, mypy |
| **Data-Flow Analyzers** | Uninitialized variables, unreachable code | Clang Static Analyzer |
| **Code Metrics** | Cyclomatic complexity, coupling, LOC | SonarQube, PMD |
| **Security** | SQL injection, buffer overflow, XSS | FindBugs, OWASP |

---

## 2.2 Detailed Taxonomy — Dynamic Unit Testing (White-Box)

White-box (structural / glass-box) testing uses **knowledge of the internal code structure** to design test cases. Three core techniques are in the KTU Module-2 syllabus:

1. **Control Flow Testing** (graph-based)
2. **Data Flow Testing** (anomaly-based)
3. **Domain Testing** (input-space-based)

### 2.2.1 Control Flow Testing — Conceptual Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Control Flow Testing)**
> A white-box testing technique that selects test cases based on the **control flow graph (CFG)** of the program, ensuring that specific control structures (statements, branches, conditions, paths) are exercised.

#### The Control Flow Graph (CFG)

A CFG is a directed graph where:
- **Nodes** = sequential statement blocks (1 entry, 1 exit per block).
- **Edges** = control flow transfers.
- **Predicate nodes** (diamonds or annotated) = decisions (if, while, for, case).

**Example source code:**
```c
int classify(int x) {
    if (x > 0)        // Node 2
        return 1;
    else if (x == 0)  // Node 4
        return 0;
    else
        return -1;
}
```

**Corresponding CFG (textual):**

```
   [1 Entry]
      │
      ▼
   [2: x > 0]──T──►[3: return 1]──►[7 Exit]
      │
      F
      ▼
   [4: x == 0]──T──►[5: return 0]──►[7 Exit]
      │
      F
      ▼
   [6: return -1]──►[7 Exit]
```

#### Coverage Criteria (Lattice)

KTU **loves** the coverage lattice. Memorize the dominance hierarchy:

```
            PATH COVERAGE (Strongest)
                 ▲
            │
        BRANCH / DECISION COVERAGE
                 ▲
            │
          CONDITION COVERAGE
                 ▲
            │
      STATEMENT COVERAGE (Weakest)
```

| Criterion | What it Requires | Notation | Minimum #Tests |
|-----------|------------------|----------|----------------|
| **Statement** | Every executable statement executed at least once | C0 | Depends on graph |
| **Branch / Decision** | Every branch outcome (True & False) taken | C1 | ≥ # of decision nodes |
| **Condition** | Every Boolean sub-expression evaluates to True & False | C2 | ≥ 2^k for k operands |
| **Multiple Condition** | Every combination of sub-condition values | MCDC | ≥ 2^k |
| **Path** | Every possible path from entry to exit | C∞ | Up to 2^(decisions) |

---

### 2.2.2 Cyclomatic Complexity — The McCabe Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Cyclomatic Complexity, McCabe 1976)**
> *Cyclomatic complexity V(G) of a flow graph G is a quantitative measure of the number of linearly independent paths through the program.* It provides the **lower bound on the number of test cases** required for **100% branch coverage**.

#### The Three Formulas (KTU must-know)

$$\boxed{V(G) \;=\; E \;-\; N \;+\; 2}$$

$$\boxed{V(G) \;=\; P \;+\; 1}$$

$$\boxed{V(G) \;=\; R}$$

Where:
- **E** = number of **edges** in the CFG.
- **N** = number of **nodes** in the CFG.
- **P** = number of **predicate nodes** (decision diamonds, count each predicate once even if compound).
- **R** = number of **enclosed regions** (including the outer region).

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-verification Trick:** Always compute V(G) by all three formulas — if any two disagree, you have miscounted edges or nodes. This is an examiner-favourite **self-correction technique**.

#### Independent Paths — Derivation

The number of independent paths through the program = **V(G)**.

**Basis set of independent paths:** A set of paths such that every other path in the program can be formed as a linear combination of them.

### 2.2.3 Data Flow Testing — Conceptual Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Data Flow Testing)**
> A white-box technique that selects test cases based on the **definition and use of variables**, aiming to expose anomalies such as using a variable before it is defined or defining a variable that is never used.

#### Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Definition of variable v** | `def(v)` | A statement that assigns a value to `v` (e.g., `v = 5`, `cin >> v`). |
| **Use of variable v** | `use(v)` | A statement that reads `v` (e.g., `if (v > 0)`, `print(v)`). |
| **c-use (computation-use)** | `c-use(v)` | Use of `v` in a computation inside a predicate or expression. |
| **p-use (predicate-use)** | `p-use(v)` | Use of `v` as a branching condition. |
| **def-clear path** | Path with no other `def(v)` between the definition and the use. |

#### The Six Data Flow Anomalies

| # | Anomaly | Symbolic | Meaning |
|---|---------|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Undefined use** | `use(v)` without prior `def(v)` | Variable used before initialization |
| 2 | **Defined but not used** | `def(v)` with no subsequent `use(v)` | Dead store / wasted write |
| 3 | **Tainted definition (DD path)** | `def(v)` followed by another `def(v)` before `use(v)` | Definition overwritten before read |
| 4 | **Abnormal termination** | Path with no `def(v)` along a reachable path | May read uninitialized memory |
| 5 | **Unreachable code** | Statement never reached from entry | Dead code |
| 6 | **Infinite loop** | Cycle with no `use(v)` | Logical deadlock |

#### Data Flow Testing Strategies (Rapps & Weyuker)

| Strategy | Definition | Strength |
|----------|------------|----------|
| **All-Defs** | For every `def(v)`, exercise at least one def-clear path to some `use(v)` | Medium |
| **All-Uses** | For every `def(v)`, exercise def-clear paths to **every** reachable `use(v)` | Stronger |
| **All-P-Uses** | Exercise def-clear paths to all **predicate** uses | Medium |
| **All-C-Uses / Some-P-Uses** | Exercise all c-uses, but at least one p-use | Hybrid |
| **All-P-Uses / Some-C-Uses** | All p-uses, at least one c-use | Hybrid |

### 2.2.4 Domain Testing — Conceptual Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Domain Testing)**
> A testing strategy that selects test inputs based on the **input domain partitioning** (the set of valid inputs to a program), targeting boundaries, interior points, and off-points of the defined domains.

#### Three Classes of Domain Errors

| Error Type | Description | Detection Method |
|------------|-------------|------------------|
