### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **JUnit** is a Java-based, open-source unit testing framework from the **xUnit** family, originally created by **Kent Beck and Erich Gamma (1997)**.
- The **four xUnit components** are: Test Fixture, Test Case, Test Suite, Test Runner.
- The **JUnit lifecycle** is: `@BeforeAll` → (per test: instance → `@BeforeEach` → `@Test` → `@AfterEach`) → `@AfterAll`.
- `@BeforeEach` / `@AfterEach` run **per test**; `@BeforeAll` / `@AfterAll` run **once** and require `static`.
- The `assertEquals(exp, act)` order is **expected first, actual second** — reversing them will show misleading error messages.
- For exception testing, use `assertThrows(ExceptionClass.class, () -> {...})` (JUnit 5) — **not** the deprecated `@Test(expected = ...)` annotation.
- **Statement Coverage** $\leq$ **Branch Coverage** $\leq$ **Path Coverage** in terms of strictness.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G) = \text{decision points} + 1$ (simple form) — used to compute the **minimum number of test cases** for full branch coverage.
- JUnit tests must be **isolated, repeatable, and deterministic** — the three pillars of automated testing.
- The **Test Pyramid** places JUnit at the **base** — unit tests must be the **most numerous** and **fastest** in the test suite.
- **Parameterized tests** in JUnit 5 use `@ParameterizedTest` with sources like `@ValueSource`, `@CsvSource`, and `@MethodSource`.
- JUnit integrates with **Maven** (`mvn test`), **Gradle** (`gradle test`), and **CI/CD** pipelines (Jenkins, GitHub Actions).
- JUnit 5 = **Jupiter** (programming model) + **Vintage** (JUnit 4 compatibility) + **Platform** (launcher API).
- Coverage tools commonly paired with JUnit: **JaCoCo** (Java Code Coverage), **Cobertura**, **Emma**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AI in Testing - GenAI for test case generation and optimization, impact on automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** AI in Testing - GenAI for test case generation and optimization, impact on automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generative AI (GenAI) in Software Testing** refers to the application of Large Language Models (LLMs), transformer-based architectures, and generative neural networks to autonomously create, refine, prioritize, and optimize software test artifacts — including test cases, test scripts, test data, regression suites, and bug reports — by learning patterns from existing repositories, requirements documents, and historical defect data.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Outcome mapping: *CO2 – Apply AI/ML techniques to enhance software testing activities*), GenAI in testing is positioned as the convergence of three engineering domains:

$$
\text{GenAI Testing} = f(\text{Natural Language Processing},\ \text{Knowledge Representation},\ \text{Software Engineering Heuristics})
$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Infinite Intern" Mental Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine hiring an intern who has read *every* QA book ever written, remembers every bug your team has ever filed, and can instantly draft 500 test cases at 3 AM without coffee. That intern is GenAI for testing. It does not *replace* the senior tester — it **amplifies** their coverage, speed, and creativity.

A second useful analogy: think of GenAI as a **"Spell-Checker for Tests"** — just as spell-check suggests the next word, GenAI suggests the next logical test step, branch condition, or edge case based on context.

### 1.3 Core Sub-Capabilities in KTU Syllabus

| Sub-Capability | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Automated Test Case Generation** | Synthesize test inputs and expected outputs from requirements/code |
| **Test Suite Optimization** | Reduce redundancy using genetic algorithms, RL, or clustering |
| **Smart Test Selection** | Pick the *minimum* test subset that catches *maximum* regressions |
| **Self-Healing Automation** | Auto-update locators/scripts when the AUT (Application Under Test) changes |
| **Defect Prediction & Triage** | Predict high-risk modules before execution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly requires students to *"understand the impact of AI/GenAI on test automation frameworks, CI/CD pipelines, and the role of the modern QA engineer."* This is a **high-weightage** Module-2 topic for ESE (End Semester Examination).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 How GenAI Generates Test Cases — The Operational Pipeline

GenAI-based test generation operates in **five logical layers**:

1. **Input Ingestion Layer** — Accepts requirements (BRD/SRS), code (AST/CFG), API contracts (OpenAPI/Swagger), or historical test logs.
2. **Prompt Engineering Layer** — Crafts structured prompts using techniques like *Chain-of-Thought (CoT)*, *Few-Shot Prompting*, and *Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)*.
3. **LLM Inference Layer** — Uses models like GPT-4, CodeLlama, or StarCoder to generate test code (Selenium, JUnit, PyTest, Postman).
4. **Validation & Coverage Layer** — Runs generated tests, measures branch/line coverage, and feeds back failures for regeneration.
5. **Optimization Layer** — Applies meta-heuristic algorithms to prune redundant cases.

### 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind Each Layer

- **Why Layer 1?** Because LLMs need *context* — without source code or requirements, generated tests are hallucinations.
- **Why Layer 2?** Because prompt quality directly correlates with test accuracy (a 2023 *IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering* study showed a **42%** coverage improvement with CoT prompting vs. zero-shot).
- **Why Layer 3?** Because transformer models excel at pattern completion — they predict the *next logical assertion* based on learned distributions.
- **Why Layer 4?** Because unverified AI output is dangerous — validation closes the loop.
- **Why Layer 5?** Because naive generation produces duplicate/redundant cases.

### 2.3 Test Optimization — Formal Algorithms

Test suite optimization in the GenAI era blends classical heuristics with ML:

#### 2.3.1 Genetic Algorithm (GA) for Test Prioritization
The fitness function balances **fault detection** against **execution cost**:

$$
F(t_i) = \alpha \cdot \text{APFD}(t_i) - \beta \cdot \text{Cost}(t_i) + \gamma \cdot \text{Risk}(t_i)
$$

Where:
- $\text{APFD}(t_i)$ = Average Percentage of Faults Detected by test $t_i$
- $\text{Cost}(t_i)$ = Execution time/resource consumption
- $\text{Risk}(t_i)$ = Predicted defect likelihood (from ML model)
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = Weighting coefficients where $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$

#### 2.3.2 Reinforcement Learning (RL) Test Selection
The Q-learning update rule for adaptive test selection:

$$
Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \eta \left[ r_{t+1} + \delta \cdot \max_{a} Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]
$$

Where $s_t$ = current code-change state, $a_t$ = test selection action, $r_{t+1}$ = reward (e.g., fault found), $\eta$ = learning rate, $\delta$ = discount factor.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this Module-2 topic. These appear frequently in Part-B (14-mark) derivations.

| **Metric / Concept** | **Formula** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|
| Code Coverage (Statement) | $C_s = \frac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Measures % of statements exercised by tests |
| Branch Coverage | $C_b = \frac{B_{executed}}{B_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Critical for GenAI branch-coverage targets |
| Mutation Score | $M_s = \frac{M_{killed}}{M_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Validates effectiveness of AI-generated tests |
| APFD (Prioritization) | $\text{APFD} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (n - i) \cdot f_i}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}$ | Used to rank AI-suggested test cases |
| Test Reduction Ratio | $R_r = 1 - \frac{\vert T_{optimized} \vert}{\vert T_{original} \vert}$ | Quantifies GenAI optimization gain |
| LLM Token Coverage | $T_c = \frac{\text{Context tokens used}}{\text{Model max context}}$ | Determines effective prompt scope |
| Precision (Defect Predict) | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality of AI defect-prediction model |
| Recall (Defect Predict) | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Completeness of AI defect-prediction model |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balanced metric for ML-based triage |
| Cyclomatic Complexity | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Guides GenAI on how many tests to generate per function |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** All absolute-value notation in formulas uses `\vert` or `\mid` rather than raw pipes, ensuring clean rendering in KTU digital answer sheets.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **industry pipelines**, these formulas power tools like:
- **GitHub Copilot for Tests** — generates unit tests using code-context inference (uses $V(G)$ to determine test depth).
- **CodiumAI / Diffblue Cover** — achieves **85%+** branch coverage autonomously on Java/Python codebases.
- **Testim.io / Mabl** — applies ML locator healing using locator-confidence scores (a continuous analog of the $Q(s,a)$ function above).
- **Google's "LLM for Test Gen"** internal research — published APFD improvements of **30–60%** over random ordering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: APFD Improvement from GenAI-Based Prioritization

The **APFD metric** is the gold standard for evaluating test prioritization. We will derive how GenAI-driven prioritization improves APFD over random ordering.

**Step 1 — Define APFD formally:**

$$
\text{APFD} = 1 - \frac{T_{T_1} + T_{T_2} + \cdots + T_{T_n}}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}
$$

Where:
- $n$ = total number of test cases
- $m$ = total number of faults in the test suite
- $T_{T_i}$ = position of the *first* test that detects fault $i$

**Step 2 — Express $T_{T_i}$ as a function of test order:**

Let $P_i$ be the position (rank) of the first test detecting fault $i$. Then $T_{T_i} = P_i$.

**Step 3 — Simplify the summation for random ordering:**

Under random ordering, the expected position of fault $i$'s first detection is:

$$
E[P_i^{random}] = \frac{n + 1}{m_i + 1}
$$

where $m_i$ is the number of tests capable of detecting fault $i$.

**Step 4 — Substitute into APFD:**

$$
E[\text{APFD}_{random}] = 1 - \frac{1}{n \cdot m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{n+1}{m_i+1} + \frac{1}{2n}
$$

**Step 5 — Now apply GenAI prioritization.**

GenAI uses defect prediction (the $R$ and $P$ metrics from Section 2.4) to *rank* tests such that high-risk tests execute first. The new expected position becomes:

$$
E[P_i^{GenAI}] = \frac{n + 1}{m_i + 1} \cdot \lambda_i
$$

where $\lambda_i \in (0, 1]$ is the **AI-prioritization gain factor** (smaller $\lambda_i$ means better prioritization for fault $i$).

**Step 6 — Compute the APFD gain:**

$$
\Delta \text{APFD} = \text{APFD}_{GenAI} - \text{APFD}_{random}
$$

$$
\Delta \text{APFD} = \frac{1}{n \cdot m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{(n+1)(1 - \lambda_i)}{m_i + 1}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation for KTU answer:**

> A $\Delta \text{APFD} > 0$ confirms GenAI prioritization outperforms random ordering. Empirical studies (e.g., Google's 2023 internal report) show $\Delta \text{APFD}$ between **0.15 and 0.35**, i.e., a **15–35%** improvement.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Test Suite Reduction via Genetic Algorithm

**Step 1 — Represent a test suite subset as a chromosome:**

$$
C = [b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n], \quad b_i \in \{0, 1\}
$$

where $b_i = 1$ means test $i$ is included, $b_i = 0$ means excluded.

**Step 2 — Define the fitness function:**

$$
F(C) = w_1 \cdot \frac{\text{Coverage}(C)}{\text{Coverage}_{max}} - w_2 \cdot \frac{\vert C \vert}{n} + w_3 \cdot \frac{\text{Faults}(C)}{\text{Faults}_{max}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply crossover** (single-point) at gene position $k$:

$$
\text{Parent}_1 = [b_1^A, \ldots, b_k^A \vert b_{k+1}^A, \ldots, b_n^A]
$$

$$
\text{Parent}_2 = [b_1^B, \ldots, b_k^B \vert b_{k+1}^B, \ldots, b_n^B]
$$

$$
\text{Child} = [b_1^A, \ldots, b_k^A, b_{k+1}^B, \ldots, b_n^B]
$$

**Step 4 — Apply mutation** with probability $p_m$:

$$
b_i^{mutated} = 1 - b_i, \quad \text{with probability } p_m
$$

**Step 5 — Iterate** across $G$ generations until convergence:

$$
\vert F(C^{g+1}) - F(C^g) \vert < \epsilon
$$

**Step 6 — Output the optimized subset $C^*$:**

$$
C^* = \arg\max_{C} F(C) \quad \text{subject to } \text{Coverage}(C) \geq \tau
$$

where $\tau$ is the minimum acceptable coverage threshold.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — GenAI Techniques vs. KTU Sub-Topics

| **KTU Sub-Topic** | **GenAI Technique** | **Key Algorithm / Model** | **Output Artifact** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Test Case Generation | LLM-based synthesis | GPT-4, CodeLlama, StarCoder | Source-code test files |
| Test Optimization | Meta-heuristic search | Genetic Algorithm, Particle Swarm | Minimized test subset |
| Test Selection | Reinforcement learning | Q-Learning, DQN | Prioritized test queue |
| Self-Healing Scripts | Computer vision + NLP | CNN-based element detection | Updated locators |
| Defect Prediction | Supervised classification | Random Forest, XGBoost, BERT | Risk heat-map |
| Requirements Testing | RAG over SRS documents | LangChain + Vector DB | Acceptance criteria tests |
| API Test Generation | Contract inference | Fine-tuned transformer | Postman/Newman scripts |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Generative AI in the context of software testing. List any four sub-tasks where GenAI is applied in modern QA pipelines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Generative AI in software testing is the use of large language models (LLMs) and generative neural networks to autonomously create, optimize, and prioritize test artifacts such as test cases, test scripts, test data, and defect reports by learning from historical repositories, requirements documents, and code.

**Four Sub-Tasks (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Automated Test Case Generation** — Synthesizing unit/integration tests directly from source code or requirements using LLMs.
2. **Test Suite Optimization** — Applying genetic algorithms or RL to remove redundant test cases while preserving coverage.
3. **Self-Healing Automation** — Auto-updating broken UI/API locators in test scripts when the application changes.
4. **Defect Prediction & Triage** — Using ML classifiers to flag high-risk modules and auto-classify incoming bug reports.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write only the definition and forget to list sub-tasks, losing 1.5 marks. Always quantify your answer with *numbered* points.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the APFD metric? Why is it important for evaluating GenAI-based test prioritization strategies?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Average Percentage of Faults Detected (APFD) is a metric that quantifies how quickly a prioritized test suite detects faults. It is computed as:

$$
\text{APFD} = 1 - \frac{T_{T_1} + T_{T_2} + \cdots + T_{T_n}}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}
$$

where $n$ = number of tests, $m$ = number of faults, and $T_{T_i}$ = position of the first test that detects fault $i$.

**Importance for GenAI (1.5 marks):**
1. APFD provides a **numerical, comparable measure** of how well GenAI prioritization ranks tests by fault-detection speed.
2. It allows **A/B comparison** between AI-prioritized and random orderings — empirical studies show $\Delta\text{APFD}$ improvements of **15–35%** with GenAI.
3. It supports **CI/CD decision-making** — high APFD means fewer pipeline runs needed to catch regressions early.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a GenAI-driven test automation system with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each layer in the pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the impact of GenAI on traditional test automation frameworks (Selenium, QTP/UFT, Postman). Highlight at least three advantages and two challenges. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture Explanation (7 marks):**

A GenAI-driven test automation system consists of **five interconnected layers**:

**Layer 1 — Input Ingestion (1 mark):**
The system accepts multiple input modalities:
- Source code from repositories (Git, SVN)
- Natural-language requirements (SRS, user stories)
- API contracts (OpenAPI/Swagger YAML files)
- Historical test logs and defect databases

**Layer 2 — Prompt Engineering Layer (1.5 marks):**
This layer transforms raw inputs into structured prompts using:
- *Chain-of-Thought (CoT)* prompting — guides the LLM to reason step-by-step.
- *Few-Shot prompting* — provides 2–5 example test cases as templates.
- *Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)* — retrieves relevant code/requirement chunks via vector similarity search and injects them into the prompt.

**Layer 3 — LLM Inference (1.5 marks):**
Pre-trained or fine-tuned models (GPT-4, CodeLlama, StarCoder) generate:
- Test code in target languages (Java/Python/JavaScript)
- Test data using libraries like Faker or AI-generated edge cases
- Expected assertions based on inferred business rules

**Layer 4 — Validation & Execution (1.5 marks):**
Generated tests are compiled, dry-run in a sandbox, and measured for:
- Statement/branch coverage
- Mutation score (validates fault-detection capability)
- Compilation errors (trigger regeneration via feedback loop)

**Layer 5 — Optimization (1.5 marks):**
A meta-heuristic layer (Genetic Algorithm, RL, or Particle Swarm Optimization) minimizes the test suite while preserving coverage above threshold $\tau$.

**Block Diagram (covered in Section 4.1 of these notes — reference it during writing).**

**(b) Impact on Traditional Automation Frameworks (7 marks):**

**Three Advantages (4.5 marks — 1.5 each):**

1. **Massive Reduction in Script Development Time:** GenAI generates Selenium/Postman scripts in seconds, reducing manual scripting effort by **60–80%**. A 2024 Capgemini report showed test-authoring time dropping from 8 hours to 45 minutes per feature.

2. **Self-Healing Capabilities:** Tools like **Testim.io** and **Mabl** use ML to detect UI changes (e.g., a button ID renaming) and automatically update locators — eliminating **30–50%** of test maintenance overhead.

3. **Intelligent Test Selection for CI/CD:** GenAI analyzes code diffs and selects only impacted tests, reducing CI pipeline execution time by **40–70%** (Google's published metric).

**Two Challenges (2.5 marks — 1.25 each):**

1. **Hallucination and False Confidence:** LLMs may generate syntactically correct but logically meaningless tests that pass vacuously (e.g., `assert True`). Human oversight and mutation testing are essential guardrails.

2. **Data Privacy & IP Concerns:** Sending proprietary source code to external LLM APIs (e.g., OpenAI) raises confidentiality risks. Organizations must adopt on-premise models (CodeLlama, Mistral) or strict data-residency agreements.

**Conclusion (not required but adds polish):** GenAI augments but does not replace automation engineers — the role shifts from *scripting* to *strategy and review*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Alternative]**

**(a)** With a suitable example, derive the APFD formula and show how GenAI-based prioritization improves APFD over random ordering. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain Genetic Algorithm-based test suite optimization. Include the chromosome encoding, fitness function, and convergence criteria in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) APFD Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Formal Definition (1 mark):**
APFD measures the rate of fault detection in a prioritized test suite:

$$
\text{APFD} = 1 - \frac{T_{T_1} + T_{T_2} + \cdots + T_{T_n}}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}
$$

**Step 2 — Setup Example (1 mark):**
Let $n = 5$ tests, $m = 3$ faults, with positions of first detection:
$T_{T_1} = 2$, $T_{T_2} = 4$, $T_{T_3} = 5$ (random ordering).

**Step 3 — Compute Random-Ordering APFD (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{APFD}_{random} = 1 - \frac{2 + 4 + 5}{5 \cdot 3} + \frac{1}{2 \cdot 5}
$$

$$
\text{APFD}_{random} = 1 - \frac{11}{15} + 0.1 = 1 - 0.7333 + 0.1 = 0.3667
$$

**Step 4 — Apply GenAI Prioritization (1.5 marks):**
GenAI's risk predictor reorders tests such that $T_{T_1} = 1$, $T_{T_2} = 2$, $T_{T_3} = 3$:

$$
\text{APFD}_{GenAI} = 1 - \frac{1 + 2 + 3}{15} + 0.1 = 1 - 0.4 + 0.1 = 0.7
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Improvement (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta\text{APFD} = 0.7 - 0.3667 = 0.3333
$$

This represents a **33.33%** improvement — confirming GenAI's prioritization effectiveness.

**Step 6 — General Formula for $\Delta$APFD (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta\text{APFD} = \frac{1}{n \cdot m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{(n+1)(1 - \lambda_i)}{m_i + 1}
$$

where $\lambda_i$ is the AI gain factor for fault $i$.

---

**(b) Genetic Algorithm for Test Suite Optimization (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Chromosome Encoding (1.5 marks):**
Each test subset is encoded as a binary string of length $n$ (number of tests):
$C = [b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n]$, where $b_i = 1$ means test $i$ is included, $b_i = 0$ means excluded.

*Example:* $C = [1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1]$ means tests 1, 3, 4, and 6 are selected from a pool of 6.

**Step 2 — Fitness Function (2 marks):**
The fitness balances three objectives:

$$
F(C) = w_1 \cdot \frac{\text{Coverage}(C)}{\text{Coverage}_{max}} - w_2 \cdot \frac{\vert C \vert}{n} + w_3 \cdot \frac{\text{Faults}(C)}{\text{Faults}_{max}}
$$

Typically $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$ (sum = 1).

**Step 3 — Genetic Operators (1.5 marks):**
- **Selection:** Tournament selection picks the fittest chromosomes.
- **Crossover:** Single-point crossover at gene $k$ swaps the second halves of two parents.
- **Mutation:** Each gene flips with probability $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$.

**Step 4 — Convergence Criteria (1 mark):**
The algorithm stops when either:
- $\vert F(C^{g+1}) - F(C^g) \vert < \epsilon$ (fitness stable), or
- Maximum generations $G_{max}$ is reached.

**Step 5 — Output (1 mark):**
The optimal subset $C^*$ satisfying $\text{Coverage}(C^*) \geq \tau$ (e.g., 85% branch coverage) with minimum cardinality.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $+ \frac{1}{2n}$ term** in APFD — this costs **0.5 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing APFD with APFDc** (cost-aware variant) — use APFD unless the question specifies cost.
> 3. **Not explaining why GenAI is "generative"** — write at least one sentence about LLMs/transformers in Q1.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** in 14-mark questions — diagrams carry **1–2 marks** in KTU valuation keys.
> 5. **Writing formulas without units or interpretations** — always add a one-line engineering interpretation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU ESE:**

- [ ] **Definition of GenAI in Testing** — LLMs + generative models creating test artifacts autonomously.
- [ ] **Five-Layer Architecture** — Input → Prompt → LLM → Validation → Optimization.
- [ ] **APFD Formula** with the $+ \frac{1}{2n}$ term and its interpretation.
- [ ] **$\Delta$APFD derivation** showing GenAI's prioritization gain.
- [ ] **Genetic Algorithm** — Encoding (binary chromosome), Fitness (coverage − size + faults), Operators (crossover, mutation), Convergence ($\epsilon$-stable or $G_{max}$).
- [ ] **RL Q-Learning update rule** for adaptive test selection.
- [ ] **RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)** — Vector DB + LLM pipeline for context-aware test generation.
- [ ] **Mutation Score formula** $M_s = M_{killed}/M_{total}$ for validating AI-generated tests.
- [ ] **Three Advantages** — Script speed, self-healing, smart CI/CD selection.
- [ ] **Two Challenges** — LLM hallucination and data privacy.
- [ ] **Industry Tools** — Testim, Mabl, CodiumAI, Diffblue Cover, GitHub Copilot for Tests.
- [ ] **The "Amplification, Not Replacement" principle** — GenAI augments human testers; the modern QA role shifts to strategy and review.
- [ ] **Key empirical metric to quote in exams:** **15–35%** APFD improvement; **60–80%** reduction in script-development time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 valuation rewards *conceptual clarity + diagram + numerical example*. Never write a 14-mark answer without at least one diagram and one worked numerical. Reference the Mermaid block diagrams in Section 4 of these notes when drawing your own in the exam hall.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industry Tools - Application of AI-driven testing tools in automation and predictive testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Industry Tools - Application of AI-driven testing tools in automation and predictive testing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **AI-Driven Testing Tools (Industry Definition):** > AI-driven testing tools are commercial or open-source software platforms that integrate **Machine Learning (ML)**, **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, **Computer Vision (CV)**, and **Generative AI (GenAI)** techniques to autonomously generate, execute, prioritise, maintain, and heal test artefacts across the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). These tools reduce manual effort, increase coverage, and adapt to UI/logic changes without explicit human re-coding. > [!NOTE] > **Predictive Testing:** > Predictive testing is a data-driven sub-discipline of AI-driven testing that uses historical defect data, code metrics, change logs, and ML models to **forecast the probability of defects, identify high-risk modules, and recommend the optimal subset of tests to execute** before a release. It transforms testing from a *reactive verification* activity into a *proactive risk-mitigation* activity. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Self-Driving Car of QA" Think of **AI-driven testing tools as the *self-driving car* of the Quality Assurance (QA) world**: | Traditional Automation | AI-Driven Automation (Self-Driving QA) | |---|---| | You (tester) drive the car (script) manually along a fixed route. | The car (AI engine) senses the road (DOM, network, API) and steers itself. | | If a road is closed (locator changes), the car stops. | The car **re-routes in real-time** (self-healing locators). | | You decide where to go every morning. | The car's GPS **predicts traffic** and chooses the best path (predictive test selection). | | You inspect the car after every trip. | **Onboard diagnostics** continuously monitor component health (anomaly detection). | > [!NOTE] > Just as **Tesla's Autopilot** does not remove the driver completely, AI-driven testing tools do **not** remove the test engineer — they elevate the engineer's role from "script-writer" to "strategy-architect and exception-handler". ### 1.3 Core Industry Metrics (Bold Constants) - **MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error)** for defect prediction accuracy: industry benchmark is **< 15%** for production-grade predictive models. - **Self-Healing Success Rate**: industry standard is **≥ 90%** for mature tools (Testim, Mabl, Healenium). - **Test Suite Reduction**: AI-based test selection typically reduces execution time by **40–70%** while retaining ≥ 95% of fault-detection capability. - **Flakiness Reduction Target**: top-tier tools claim **> 80%** reduction in flaky test execution. - **Coverage Improvement**: AI-generated tests typically cover **30–50% more unique code paths** than hand-written scripts. ### 1.4 Classification of AI-Driven Testing Tools ```text AI-Driven Testing Tools ├── Test Authoring Tools (NL → Script) │ ├── Testim.io │ ├── Mabl │ └── Functionize ├── Visual / UI Testing Tools │ ├── Applitools Eyes │ ├── Percy (BrowserStack) │ └── Aplitools Ultrafast Grid ├── Self-Healing / Smart Locators │ ├── Healenium │ ├── Testim │ └── Mabl ├── Predictive / Risk-Based Tools │ ├── Sealights │ ├── Launchable │ └── CodeAI / Harness ├── API & Contract Testing │ ├── Postman + AI Assertions │ ├── SmartBear ReadyAPI │ └── PactFlow └── Generative AI Assistants ├── GitHub Copilot (test gen) ├── CodiumAI / Qodo └── ChatGPT-based test designers ``` # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Pillars of AI-Driven Testing ### Pillar 1 — **Test Authoring via Generative AI / NLP** - Converts plain-English requirements (e.g., "Login with valid credentials should land on dashboard") into executable test scripts. - Uses **Large Language Models (LLMs)** such as GPT-4, Claude, or fine-tuned Code-LLMs. - **How it works**: Requirements → Tokenisation → Embedding (BERT/CodeBERT) → Sequence-to-Sequence model → Test code (Selenium/Cypress/Playwright). - **Why it matters**: Cuts script-creation time by ~60%; empowers non-coders (manual testers, BAs) to contribute. ### Pillar 2 — **Self-Healing Automation** - When locators (`#login-btn`, XPath, CSS selectors) change due to refactoring, the AI engine: 1. Detects the failure (test step timeout / NoSuchElementException). 2. Captures the DOM snapshot of the new page. 3. Uses a **similarity algorithm** (Levenshtein distance, cosine similarity over DOM vectors, or a Siamese Neural Network) to find the closest match to the originally intended element. 4. Updates the locator transparently and re-runs the step. - **Why it matters**: Reduces maintenance overhead which typically consumes **30–50%** of total automation effort. ### Pillar 3 — **Visual AI Testing** - Uses **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)** to compare screenshots pixel-by-pixel *semantically* rather than literally. - Ignores anti-aliasing, font-rendering, and minor 1-pixel shifts — but flags genuine layout, colour, or content regressions. - **Engine**: Visual AI typically uses Siamese CNNs or Vision Transformers (ViT) to produce an embedding per screenshot; threshold-based comparison. - **Why it matters**: Eliminates brittle pixel-diff tests; supports cross-browser, cross-device, cross-resolution testing. ### Pillar 4 — **Predictive Testing & Risk-Based Prioritisation** - Uses **supervised ML** (Random Forest, XGBoost, Gradient Boosting) trained on historical defect data. - Inputs: code churn, complexity (cyclomatic), author history, module size, prior defect density, commit time, test coverage. - Output: a **Defect Probability Score** $P(d_i)$ for each module $i \in [0, 1]$. - **Why it matters**: Enables *risk-based regression* — only run the top-K% of tests that cover the riskiest modules, saving CI minutes and cost. ## 2.2 Why & How — Operational Logic | Step | Action | Why It Matters | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Data Collection** — gather commits, defects, test results, code metrics | ML models are only as good as their training data | | 2 | **Feature Engineering** — compute Halstead complexity, churn, coverage, author tenure | Converts raw repo data into predictive features | | 3 | **Model Training** — train classifier (XGBoost) on labelled defect data | Learns the "signature" of buggy code | | 4 | **Inference** — score every new commit / module | Gives testers an actionable risk ranking | | 5 | **Test Selection** — pick top-N% tests mapped to high-risk modules | Optimises CI runtime without sacrificing fault detection | | 6 | **Feedback Loop** — push production defects back to the model | Continuously improves model accuracy (online learning) | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All metrics below are **exam-ready** and frequently appear in KTU 14-mark analytical questions. | # | Metric / Concept | Formula | Variables | Unit / Range | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | TP = True Positives, FP = False Positives | $[0, 1]$ | | 2 | **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | FN = False Negatives | $[0, 1]$ | | 3 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P, R | $[0, 1]$ | | 4 | **Accuracy** | $A = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | TN = True Negatives | $[0, 1]$ | | 5 | **MCC (Matthews CC)** | $MCC = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Balanced metric for imbalanced data | $[-1, +1]$ | | 6 | **ROC-AUC** | $\displaystyle \int_0^1 TPR(FPR^{-1}(t))\, dt$ | TPR vs FPR trade-off | $[0.5, 1.0]$ | | 7 | **Defect Probability** | $P(d \vert \mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)$ | $\sigma$ = sigmoid, $\mathbf{x}$ = feature vector | $[0, 1]$ | | 8 | **Risk Score per Module** | $Risk_i = P(d_i) \cdot Impact_i \cdot \dfrac{1}{TestCoverage_i}$ | Combines probability, business impact, coverage gap | dimensionless | | 9 | **Test Suite Reduction** | $TSR = 1 - \dfrac{\vert S_{AI} \vert}{\vert S_{full} \vert}$ | $S_{AI}$ = AI-selected suite | $[0, 1]$ | | 10 | **Flakiness Index** | $FI = \dfrac{\text{Number of Inconsistent Pass/Fail Runs}}{\text{Total Runs of Test}}$ | Tracks unreliable tests | $[0, 1]$ | | 11 | **Levenshtein Distance** (self-heal) | $LD(s_1, s_2) = \min\{\text{insert, delete, substitute costs}\}$ | For matching similar DOM locators | integer $\geq 0$ | | 12 | **Cosine Similarity** (self-heal) | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}}{\Vert \mathbf{A}\Vert \, \Vert \mathbf{B}\Vert}$ | Vector similarity of DOM embeddings | $[-1, +1]$ | | 13 | **MAPE** | $MAPE = \dfrac{100\%}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \dfrac{A_i - F_i}{A_i} \right\vert$ | $A_i$ = actual defects, $F_i$ = forecast | ideally $< 15\%$ | | 14 | **Cyclomatic Complexity** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components | $\geq 1$ | | 15 | **Code Churn** | $Churn = LOC_{added} + LOC_{deleted} + LOC_{modified}$ | Activity in a time window | lines | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking (e.g., HSBC, JPMorgan)** — AI visual testing catches subtle UI regressions in trading dashboards that pixel-diff misses. - **E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart)** — Predictive test selection reduces a 4-hour regression suite to 45 minutes during peak sale releases. - **Healthcare (Philips, Siemens Healthineers)** — Self-healing locators protect patient-portal UIs across thousands of configuration variants. - **Automotive (Tesla, Bosch)** — Generative AI creates thousands of test scenarios for ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems) faster than engineers can. - **Telecom (Ericsson)** — Risk-based regression prioritises tests for 5G core-network components under continuous deployment. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Confusion Matrix to F1-Score (Exhaustive) > [!NOTE] > This derivation is **exam-favourite** for the 14-mark analytical sub-question. Every step is shown — do not skip any row in the exam. ### Step 1 — Define the Four Outcomes of a Binary Classifier A predictive testing model classifies each module as **Defective (Positive)** or **Non-Defective (Negative)**: $$ TP = \text{Modules correctly predicted defective} $$ $$ FP = \text{Modules wrongly predicted defective} $$ $$ TN = \text{Modules correctly predicted non-defective} $$ $$ FN = \text{Modules wrongly predicted non-defective} $$ ### Step 2 — Build the Confusion Matrix $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Predicted} \rightarrow \\ \text{Actual} \downarrow \end{aligned} \qquad \begin{array}{c|c|c} & \text{Positive} & \text{Negative} \\ \hline \text{Positive} & TP & FN \\ \hline \text{Negative} & FP & TN \\ \end{array} $$ ### Step 3 — Derive Precision $$ P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} $$ > **Logic**: Of all modules the model *flagged as defective*, what fraction were *truly* defective? High precision ⇒ few false alarms (saves test effort on wrong suspects). ### Step 4 — Derive Recall (True Positive Rate) $$ R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} $$ > **Logic**: Of all *truly defective* modules, what fraction did the model *catch*? High recall ⇒ few missed defects (safer releases). ### Step 5 — Derive F1-Score (Harmonic Mean) The harmonic mean penalises extreme imbalance between $P$ and $R$. $$ \begin{aligned} F_1 &= \frac{2}{\dfrac{1}{P} + \dfrac{1}{R}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} \\[6pt] &= \frac{2 \cdot \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} + \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{2 \cdot TP^2 \,/\, \big[(TP+FP)(TP+FN)\big]}{TP(TN+FN) + TP(FP+TN)} \,/\, \big[(TP+FP)(TP+FN)\big] \\[6pt] &= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2TP + FP + FN} \end{aligned} $$ > **Engineering Insight**: For *imbalanced* defect data (very few defective modules), **F1** is preferred over Accuracy. KTU examiners love this nuance. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Tool Capability Comparison | Tool | Vendor | AI Technique Used | Primary Use-Case | Self-Heal | Visual AI | Predictive | Pricing Model | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Testim.io** | Testim | ML locators, NLP | Web UI automation | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | SaaS, freemium | | **Mabl** | Mabl | ML, anomaly detection | End-to-end web | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | SaaS subscription | | **Applitools Eyes** | Applitools | CNN, ViT | Visual regression | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✗ | SaaS, tiered | | **Functionize** | Functionize | NLP, ML | NLP-driven test creation | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Enterprise | | **Healenium** | Healenium (OSS) | ML, Levenshtein | Self-healing for Selenium | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Open-source | | **Launchable** | Launchable | ML, test selection | Predictive test selection | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓ | SaaS | | **Sealights** | Sealights | ML, code coverage | Risk-based testing | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | Enterprise | | **Percy** | BrowserStack | Visual diff + AI | Visual regression | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | SaaS | | **CodiumAI / Qodo** | Qodo | LLM (GPT-class) | Unit test generation | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Freemium | | **GitHub Copilot** | GitHub | LLM (Codex/GPT) | Test-code autocomplete | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Subscription | | **PactFlow** | PactFlow | ML for contract | API contract testing | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | SaaS | | **Sauce Labs + AI** | Sauce Labs | ML analytics | Cross-browser insights | partial | ✗ | ✓ | SaaS | > ✓ = supported, ✓✓ = flagship feature, ✗ = not available, partial = limited ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Risk Score Calculation > [!NOTE] > Practice this as a **sure-shot 7-mark sub-question**. **Problem:** Given three modules $M_1$, $M_2$, $M_3$ with the following data, compute the **Risk Score** for each and rank them for regression priority. | Module | Defect Probability $P(d)$ | Business Impact (1–10) | Test Coverage | |---|---|---|---| | $M_1$ | 0.7 | 8 | 0.60 | | $M_2$ | 0.5 | 9 | 0.85 | | $M_3$ | 0.9 | 4 | 0.40 | **Formula:** $$ Risk_i = P(d_i) \cdot Impact_i \cdot \frac{1}{TestCoverage_i} $$ **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} Risk_{M_1} &= 0.7 \times 8 \times \frac{1}{0.60} \\ &= 0.7 \times 8 \times 1.667 \\ &= 9.33 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} Risk_{M_2} &= 0.5 \times 9 \times \frac{1}{0.85} \\ &= 0.5 \times 9 \times 1.176 \\ &= 5.29 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} Risk_{M_3} &= 0.9 \times 4 \times \frac{1}{0.40} \\ &= 0.9 \times 4 \times 2.50 \\ &= 9.00 \end{aligned} $$ **Ranking (highest risk first):** $$ M_1 \; (9.33) \;>\; M_3 \; (9.00) \;>\; M_2 \; (5.29) $$ > **Engineering Insight**: $M_3$ has the highest *defect probability* but **lower business impact and better coverage**, so $M_1$ edges it out. Always **justify** your ranking in the exam with all three factors. ## 3.4 Sample Pseudocode — Self-Healing Locator (Healenium-style) > [!NOTE] > Since PECST631 is a CS/IS course, a small pseudocode is permitted to illustrate the algorithmic flow. **Do NOT memorise syntax**; understand the logic. ``` ALGORITHM: SelfHeal(originalLocator, timeout) INPUT: original XPath/CSS locator, timeout in seconds OUTPUT: healedLocator OR FAIL 1. TRY 2. element = driver.findElement(originalLocator) // initial attempt 3. RETURN originalLocator 4. CATCH NoSuchElementException OR TimeoutException 5. domSnapshot = driver.getPageSource() // capture current DOM 6. candidates = [] 7. FOR each element e IN parseHTML(domSnapshot) 8. score = weightedSum( 9. tagMatch(e.tag, original.tag) * 0.3 + 10. attrSimilarity(e.attrs, orig) * 0.3 + 11. textSimilarity(e.text, orig) * 0.2 + 12. proximity(e, orig.position) * 0.2 13. ) 14. candidates.append((e, score)) 15. END FOR 16. candidates.sortBy(score, DESCENDING) 17. bestMatch = candidates[0] 18. IF bestMatch.score >= THRESHOLD (e.g., 0.75) 19. newXPath = generateXPath(bestMatch.element) 20. healenium.updateLocator(originalLocator, newXPath) // persist 21. log("Healed: " + originalLocator + " → " + newXPath) 22. RETURN newXPath 23. ELSE 24. RETURN FAIL 25. END IF ``` # 4. List any four industry-leading tools with their primary AI technique.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **AI-driven testing tools** are software platforms that apply **Artificial Intelligence techniques** such as Machine Learning, Natural Language Processing, Computer Vision, and Generative AI to **automate, optimise, and self-maintain** software testing activities across the SDLC. | # | Tool | AI Technique | Primary Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Testim.io** | ML-based smart locators | UI test automation | | 2 | **Applitools Eyes** | CNN / Visual AI | Visual regression | | 3 | **Mabl** | ML + Anomaly detection | E2E web testing | | 4 | **Launchable** | ML-based test selection | Predictive testing | *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the tool table with techniques.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]** **What is predictive testing? Write the formula for Risk Score of a module.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Predictive testing** is a data-driven approach that uses **historical defect data, code metrics, and Machine Learning models** to **forecast defect probabilities** and **prioritise tests** for high-risk modules, transforming testing from reactive to proactive. **Risk Score formula:** $$ Risk_i = P(d_i) \cdot Impact_i \cdot \frac{1}{TestCoverage_i} $$ where $P(d_i)$ is the predicted defect probability, $Impact_i$ is the business criticality (1–10), and $TestCoverage_i$ is the existing test coverage fraction of module $i$. *(2 marks for definition + explanation, 1 mark for the correct formula with variable meaning.)* --- ## 📕 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: Predictive Testing **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the architecture of a **predictive testing system** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **feature engineering** and **feedback loop** in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Given the following data for four modules, compute the **Defect Probability using Logistic Regression coefficients**, **Risk Score**, and rank them for regression priority. Assume weights $w_1 = 0.4$ (churn), $w_2 = 0.3$ (complexity), $w_3 = 0.3$ (coverage gap), and bias $b = -1.5$. Use $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$. **(7 marks)** | Module | Churn | Complexity | Coverage Gap | |---|---|---|---| | A | 5 | 3 | 0.2 | | B | 2 | 4 | 0.5 | | C | 4 | 5 | 0.7 | | D | 1 | 2 | 0.1 | --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **1. Architecture of Predictive Testing System (4 marks)** The predictive testing system has **six major layers**: 1. **Data Collection Layer** — Gathers raw data from: - Version Control (Git commits, churn) - Issue Trackers (Jira defects) - Test Reports (pass/fail history) - Code Quality Tools (SonarQube metrics) 2. **Feature Engineering Layer** — Transforms raw data into ML-ready features: - Code Churn $Churn = LOC_{added} + LOC_{deleted} + LOC_{modified}$ - Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ - Defect Density of last 30 days - Author Tenure and Ownership Stability 3. **Model Training Layer** — Trains a classifier (XGBoost, Random Forest, Logistic Regression) on labelled historical data with features as inputs and the binary label "defective/non-defective" as output. 4. **Inference Layer** — Scores new commits/modules in real time, producing a **Defect Probability** $P(d_i) \in [0, 1]$. 5. **Test Selection Layer** — Maps the high-risk modules to their associated test cases and selects the **top-K%** for execution. 6. **Feedback Loop Layer** — Pushes production defects and test outcomes back to the dataset, enabling **online/periodic retraining** to prevent model drift. **2. Role of Feature Engineering (1.5 marks)** Feature engineering is the **most critical step** because ML model accuracy depends entirely on the **quality and relevance of input features**. Good features: - Distinguish buggy from clean code (e.g., high churn + high complexity ⇒ high risk). - Reduce noise and dimensionality. - Encode domain knowledge (e.g., payment modules have higher business impact). **3. Role of Feedback Loop (1.5 marks)** The feedback loop: - Prevents **model drift** as codebases evolve. - Captures **new defect patterns** that the original model never saw. - Ensures the predictive system **ages well** — accuracy improves over time, matching the **CI/CD continuous-improvement philosophy**. --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Logistic Regression Formula (1 mark)** $$ P(d_i) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad \text{where } z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + w_3 x_3 + b $$ **Step 2 — Compute $z$ for each module (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} z_A &= (0.4 \times 5) + (0.3 \times 3) + (0.3 \times 0.2) + (-1.5) \\ &= 2.0 + 0.9 + 0.06 - 1.5 = 1.46 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} z_B &= (0.4 \times 2) + (0.3 \times 4) + (0.3 \times 0.5) - 1.5 \\ &= 0.8 + 1.2 + 0.15 - 1.5 = 0.65 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} z_C &= (0.4 \times 4) + (0.3 \times 5) + (0.3 \times 0.7) - 1.5 \\ &= 1.6 + 1.5 + 0.21 - 1.5 = 1.81 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} z_D &= (0.4 \times 1) + (0.3 \times 2) + (0.3 \times 0.1) - 1.5 \\ &= 0.4 + 0.6 + 0.03 - 1.5 = -0.47 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply Sigmoid (2 marks)** $$ P_A = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1.46}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.232} = 0.812 $$ $$ P_B = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.65}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.522} = 0.657 $$ $$ P_C = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1.81}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.163} = 0.860 $$ $$ P_D = \frac{1}{1 + e^{0.47}} = \frac{1}{1 + 1.600} = 0.385 $$ **Step 4 — Risk Score (assume Impact = 5 for all, $1 - CoverageGap = Coverage$) (1.5 marks)** $$ Risk_i = P(d_i) \cdot Impact_i \cdot \frac{1}{Coverage_i} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} Risk_A &= 0.812 \times 5 \times \frac{1}{(1-0.2)} = 0.812 \times 5 \times 1.25 = 5.075 \\ Risk_B &= 0.657 \times 5 \times \frac{1}{0.5} = 0.657 \times 5 \times 2.00 = 6.570 \\ Risk_C &= 0.860 \times 5 \times \frac{1}{0.3} = 0.860 \times 5 \times 3.33 = 14.319 \\ Risk_D &= 0.385 \times 5 \times \frac{1}{0.9} = 0.385 \times 5 \times 1.11 = 2.137 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Final Ranking (0.5 mark)** $$ \boxed{C \; (14.32) \;>\; B \; (6.57) \;>\; A \; (5.08) \;>\; D \; (2.14)} $$ > **Engineering Insight**: Module **C** has the highest defect probability *and* the lowest coverage ⇒ highest risk. Module **D** is the safest and should be deprioritised. --- ### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: Self-Healing & Tool Comparison **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the concept of **self-healing test automation**. Describe any **two ML-based techniques** used for locator repair with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **Testim, Applitools, Mabl, and Healenium** in terms of AI technique, primary use-case, self-healing support, visual-AI support, and pricing model. Justify which tool you would recommend for a **large BFSI (Banking, Financial Services, Insurance) project with strict UI compliance**. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **1. Concept of Self-Healing Automation (2 marks)** **Self-healing automation** is the ability of an AI-driven testing tool to **automatically detect, diagnose, and repair broken test locators** (XPath, CSS, ID) at runtime — without human intervention — when the application under test (AUT) undergoes UI changes such as refactoring, re-styling, or DOM restructuring. > It eliminates the **#1 cause of automation failure** — brittle locators — and is implemented via a combination of **DOM analysis, similarity scoring, and ML classification**. **2. Technique 1 — Levenshtein Distance-based Locator Matching (2.5 marks)** - The engine computes the **Levenshtein (edit) distance** between the **attributes** of the original element (e.g., `id="login-btn"`) and candidate elements in the new DOM. - Lower edit distance ⇒ higher similarity. **Example:** ``` Original locator: //button[@id='login-btn'] Old attribute: id="login-btn" New DOM candidate: id="loginBtn" → Edit distance = 2 (lowercase 'b' → 'B', removed hyphen) New DOM candidate: id="signup" → Edit distance = 7 (high, rejected) ``` The candidate with the **lowest edit distance above a threshold** is chosen as the healed locator. **3. Technique 2 — Siamese Neural Network / Cosine Similarity over DOM Embeddings (2.5 marks)** - A **Siamese Neural Network** is trained on pairs of (old-element, new-element) screenshots or DOM vectors. - It produces a **similarity score** $\in [0, 1]$. - If score $\geq 0.75$, the locator is updated; else, a human is alerted. **Example:** ``` Old element vector: [0.12, 0.88, 0.45, 0.33, ...] (computed via BERT/DOM-encoder) New candidate 1: [0.14, 0.86, 0.44, 0.34, ...] → cos θ = 0.998 ✓ heal New candidate 2: [0.91, 0.05, 0.10, 0.02, ...] → cos θ = 0.234 ✗ reject ``` > **Engineering Insight**: Siamese networks are **more robust** than Levenshtein for **visual** or **multi-attribute** locators, while Levenshtein is **faster** for **text-based IDs/classes** — most production tools use a **hybrid** of both. --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **1. Comparison Table (4 marks)** | Criterion | **Testim** | **Applitools** | **Mabl** | **Healenium** | |---|---|---|---|---| | AI Technique | ML locators, NLP | CNN / ViT visual AI | ML, anomaly detection | ML, Levenshtein | | Primary Use-Case | Web UI test authoring | Visual regression | E2E web automation | Self-healing for Selenium | | Self-Healing | ✓ Yes | ✗ No | ✓ Yes | ✓✓ Strong | | Visual AI | ✗ No | ✓✓ Strong | ✓ Partial | ✗ No | | Pricing | SaaS, freemium | SaaS, tiered | SaaS subscription | **Open-source** | | Best For | Fast authoring | Pixel-perfect UI checks | Auto-heal + visual hybrid | OSS budget projects | **2. Recommendation for Large BFSI Project (3 marks)** > **Recommended Tool: Applitools Eyes + Healenium (combined) OR Mabl (if budget allows)** **Justification:** 1. **Strict UI compliance** is a regulatory requirement in BFSI (e.g., RBI, SEBI, PCI-DSS guidelines) — **Applitools' CNN-based visual AI** provides the strongest protection against subtle UI regressions that pixel-diff tools miss (e.g., font shifts, colour-contrast failures for accessibility). 2. **Self-healing is essential** because BFSI applications undergo **frequent refactoring** (regulatory changes force UI updates) — **Healenium** (open-source) or **Mabl's** self-heal layer prevents CI flakiness. 3. **Combined strategy**: Use **Healenium** for locator-level stability (Selenium/Java stack) **and** **Applitools** as the visual-validation overlay — gives a **defence-in-depth** QA architecture with **zero vendor lock-in** (Healenium is OSS) and **best-in-class visual AI** (Applitools). 4. **Cost-benefit**: For multi-team enterprise rollouts, the **Applitools + Healenium combo** is more economical than Mabl's all-in-one SaaS pricing, while delivering equivalent or superior AI capability. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — AI-Driven & Predictive Testing** - ⭐ **AI-Driven Testing** = ML + NLP + CV + GenAI integrated into QA. - ⭐ **Predictive Testing** = ML-based *forecasting* of defects and *selection* of optimal test subset. - ⭐ **Four Pillars**: (1) Generative Authoring, (2) Self-Healing, (3) Visual AI, (4) Predictive Selection. - ⭐ **Core Tools**: Testim (authoring), Applitools (visual), Mabl (E2E), Healenium (OSS self-heal), Launchable (predictive), Sealights (risk). - ⭐ **F1-Score** $F_1 = \dfrac{2 P R}{P + R}$ — preferred over accuracy for **imbalanced** defect data. - ⭐ **Risk Score** $Risk = P(d) \cdot Impact \cdot \dfrac{1}{Coverage}$ — combines probability, business impact, and test gap. - ⭐ **Self-healing techniques**: Levenshtein distance (text), Cosine similarity (vectors), Siamese NN (visual). - ⭐ **Sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ — maps raw score to defect probability $[0, 1]$. - ⭐ **Feedback loop** = non-negotiable in any predictive pipeline; prevents model drift. - ⭐ **Predictive pipeline order**: Data → Features → Model → Inference → Test Selection → Execution → Feedback. - ⭐ **BFSI recommendation**: Applitools + Healenium for visual + self-heal defence-in-depth. - ⭐ **Visual AI** uses **CNN / ViT** (Siamese) — *not* pixel-by-pixel diff. - ⭐ **Industry KPIs**: ≥ 90% heal success, ≤ 15% MAPE, 40–70% CI time reduction. - ⭐ **KTU must-draw**: Pipeline diagram with feedback loop; tool-comparison table; risk-score numerical. - ⭐ **Avoid confusion**: Predictive testing ≠ test automation; AI tools ≠ traditional record-and-playback tools.

topics.title: Case Study - Mutation testing using JUnit, AI-enhanced test case automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Study - Mutation testing using JUnit, AI-enhanced test case automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Mutation Testing?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Jia & Harman, 2011):**
> *Mutation Testing is a fault-based software testing technique that involves making small syntactic changes (called mutations) to the program under test (PIT) to generate mutant programs. The effectiveness of a test suite is measured by its ability to **distinguish (kill)** these mutants from the original program.*

In simple words — **we deliberately inject deliberate, tiny artificial faults** into the source code, and then ask the question:
> *"Does my existing test suite actually catch these planted bugs?"*

If the tests still pass even after the fault was injected, the test suite is **weak** — it failed to notice the bug.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Spelling Bee Exam" Analogy
Imagine a teacher giving a spelling test to students. The teacher writes a paragraph but **intentionally introduces 10 small spelling mistakes** (swapping letters, missing letters, swapped words). The students must proofread and find the mistakes.

- If a student finds **8 of 10 errors** → their proofreading ability is **80% effective** → their "mutation score" is `8/10 = 0.80`.
- If a student finds **all 10** → their mutation score is **100%** — they are excellent testers.

In mutation testing, **mutants = planted errors**, **tests = the students**, and **mutation score = percentage of errors caught**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):**
> A mutant is considered **KILLED** if at least one test case from the test suite fails when run against the mutant. A mutant **SURVIVES** if the test suite still passes against the mutant (i.e., the test suite cannot distinguish it from the original program).

---

## 1.2 What is AI-Enhanced Test Case Automation?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *AI-enhanced test case automation refers to the application of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) techniques — such as search-based optimization, symbolic execution, neural program synthesis, and Large Language Models (LLMs) — to automatically generate, prioritize, repair, and execute test cases, thereby improving coverage, reducing manual effort, and accelerating the feedback loop in CI/CD pipelines.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Driving Car Inspector"
A traditional test engineer manually drives the car on every road type to test brakes. An AI-enhanced system **learns from past crash data**, simulates millions of road scenarios overnight, **automatically picks the 50 most crash-likely scenarios**, and tells the human engineer: *"Focus on testing THESE only."*

The AI doesn't replace the engineer — it **massively boosts productivity and accuracy**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus Glossary)

| Term | Definition | Units / Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Mutant** | A modified version of the program containing one small fault | Integer count |
| **Mutant Operator (MOP)** | The transformation rule used to create mutants | Type (AOR, ROR, UOI…) |
| **Mutation Score (MS)** | Ratio of killed mutants to total non-equivalent mutants | `0 ≤ MS ≤ 1` |
| **Equivalent Mutant** | A mutant that behaves identically to the original — cannot be killed | Uncountable in MS |
| **PIT** | Program Under Test (the original) | — |
| **Test Suite Adequacy** | Measured by mutation score | Percentage |

> [!TIP]
> The unit **Mutation Adequate Test Suite** means MS = **1.0 (100%)** — every non-equivalent mutant is killed.

---

## 1.4 Where These Techniques Are Used in Industry

- **Finance (HDFC, JP Morgan):** Detecting off-by-one errors in trading logic
- **Healthcare (Philips, Siemens Healthineers):** Mutation testing in life-critical device firmware
- **Automotive (Tesla, Bosch):** AI-based regression test prioritization
- **Search Engines (Google, Microsoft Bing):** AI-based fuzz testing of parsers
- **Java Spring Boot Enterprise Apps:** PIT (Pitest) is the de-facto industry tool

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mutation Testing Process (Structured Steps)

The complete mutation testing workflow follows a strict pipeline. Each step has a clear **why** and **how**:

1. **Step 1 — Pick the Program Under Test (PIT)**
   - *Why:* We need a known-good baseline.
   - *How:* Select the source file(s) — typically a single method or class for academic case studies.

2. **Step 2 — Apply Mutation Operators**
   - *Why:* Create faulty variants systematically.
   - *How:* Apply pre-defined transformation rules (e.g., change `+` to `-`).

3. **Step 3 — Generate Mutants**
   - *Why:* Build the mutant pool.
   - *How:* One mutation operator applied to one location → one mutant.

4. **Step 4 — Run the Test Suite Against Each Mutant**
   - *Why:* To check if the tests can detect the fault.
   - *How:* JUnit-based tools like **PIT (Pitest)** automate this in parallel.

5. **Step 5 — Classify Outcomes**
   - **Killed Mutant:** At least one test failed.
   - **Survived Mutant:** All tests passed (test suite is weak).
   - **Equivalent Mutant:** Semantically same as original (impossible to kill — discard).
   - **Timed-Out / Compile-Error:** Treated as killed in most tools.

6. **Step 6 — Compute Mutation Score**
   - *Why:* Quantify test suite quality.
   - *How:* Apply the formula below.

---

## 2.2 Mutation Operators — The Mutation Operator Zoo

| Operator | Full Name | Example Transformation |
|---|---|---|
| **AOR** | Arithmetic Operator Replacement | `+` → `-`, `*` → `/`, `%` → `+` |
| **ROR** | Relational Operator Replacement | `>` → `>=`, `==` → `!=`, `<` → `<=` |
| **LOR** | Logical Operator Replacement | `&&` → `\|\|`, `\|\|` → `&&` |
| **UOI** | Unary Operator Insertion | `x` → `x++`, `x` → `--x`, `x` → `+x` |
| **SVR** | Scalar Variable Replacement | `x` → `y`, `int a` → `int b` |
| **CR** | Constant Replacement | `0` → `1`, `2` → `-2`, `5` → `6` |
| **SDL** | Statement Deletion | `x = x + 1;` → *(deleted)* |
| **ASR** | Assignment Operator Replacement | `+=` → `-=`, `*=` → `/=` |
| **ABS** | Absolute Value Insertion | `x` → `abs(x)` |
| **OBL** | Off-By-One (Loop/Array) | `i < 10` → `i <= 10` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, you are expected to remember **at least AOR, ROR, LOR, UOI, CR, and SDL** — these are the "big six" appearing frequently in question papers.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mutation Score (MS)** | $MS = \dfrac{\vert K \vert}{\vert T \vert - \vert E \vert}$ | $K$ = killed mutants, $T$ = total mutants, $E$ = equivalent mutants | $0 \leq MS \leq 1$ |
| 2 | **Mutation Adequacy** | $MS = 1.0$ | Means test suite kills every non-equivalent mutant | Equivalent mutants excluded |
| 3 | **Mutant Generation Count (Upper Bound)** | $\vert M \vert = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m_{i}} O_{ij}$ | $n$ = locations, $m_i$ = applicable op positions, $O_{ij}$ = ops applicable at position $ij$ | Cannot exceed tool's max-mutants cap |
| 4 | **Detection Probability** | $P_{detect} = \dfrac{\vert K \vert}{\vert T \vert}$ | Includes equivalent mutants in denominator (strict version) | $0 \leq P_{detect} \leq 1$ |
| 5 | **Cost (Time)** | $C = \vert T \vert \times \vert S \vert \times T_{exec}$ | $\vert S \vert$ = test suite size, $T_{exec}$ = avg test execution time (s) | Parallel execution reduces wall-clock cost |
| 6 | **Survival Rate (weakness indicator)** | $SR = \dfrac{\vert S \vert}{\vert T \vert - \vert E \vert}$ | $S$ = survived mutants | High SR → weak test suite |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Pitfall: Many students write $MS = \dfrac{K}{T}$ forgetting to **subtract equivalent mutants**. Always use $\vert T \vert - \vert E \vert$ as the denominator!

---

## 2.4 Equivalent Mutant Problem — Why It Matters

An **equivalent mutant** is a syntactically different program that behaves **identically** to the original for ALL possible inputs.

**Example:**
```java
// Original
return x * 2;

// Equivalent mutant (mathematically identical for integers)
return x + x;
```

For all integer inputs, `x*2 == x+x` → **equivalent mutant — cannot be killed**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Equivalent mutants are the **#1 practical limitation** of mutation testing. They require human inspection, are non-trivial to detect automatically, and inflate the cost.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace (NASA)** | Validating flight control test suites |
| **Banking (SWIFT)** | Detecting subtle calculation faults in payment processors |
| **Healthcare (FDA)** | Compliance for medical device software (IEC 62304) |
| **E-Commerce (Amazon)** | AI prioritizes regression tests for pricing/promo engines |
| **DevOps CI/CD** | PIT integrated into Maven/Jenkins pipelines |

---

## 2.6 AI-Enhanced Test Case Automation — The Six Pillars

1. **Search-Based Software Testing (SBST)** — Genetic algorithms, hill climbing
2. **Symbolic Execution** — Pathfinder, KLEE, SPF (Java Path Finder)
3. **Fuzz Testing (Coverage-Guided)** — AFL, libFuzzer
4. **ML-Based Test Prioritization** — Regression test ordering using classifiers
5. **LLM-Based Test Generation** — GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT for unit-test synthesis
6. **Neural Program Synthesis** — DeepCoder, Codex generating test inputs from specs

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 emphasis: Be ready to explain **Search-Based Testing (SBST)** and **Symbolic Execution** with examples — they appear most often in Module 2 questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Mutation Score (Numerical Derivation)

**Given:** A program `computeDiscount(int price, int customerType)` has been mutated. The mutation testing tool generated the following results:

| Category | Count |
|---|---|
| Total Mutants Generated ($\vert T \vert$) | 50 |
| Killed Mutants ($\vert K \vert$) | 38 |
| Survived Mutants ($\vert S \vert$) | 8 |
| Equivalent Mutants ($\vert E \vert$) | 4 |
| Compile-Error / Timeout | 0 |

**Required:** Compute the Mutation Score and Survival Rate.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.**

$$MS = \dfrac{\vert K \vert}{\vert T \vert - \vert E \vert}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MS &= \dfrac{38}{50 - 4} \\[6pt]
   &= \dfrac{38}{46} \\[6pt]
   &= 0.8260 \\[6pt]
   &\approx 82.61\% \quad \text{(rounded to 2 decimal places)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Survival Rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SR &= \dfrac{\vert S \vert}{\vert T \vert - \vert E \vert} \\[6pt]
   &= \dfrac{8}{46} \\[6pt]
   &= 0.1739 \\[6pt]
   &\approx 17.39\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
- The test suite is **82.61% mutation adequate** — acceptable but not perfect.
- **8 surviving mutants** indicate 8 subtle faults the tests fail to catch.
- **Equivalent mutants (4)** are dropped from the denominator.

**Step 5 — Engineering Conclusion.**
- Add 8 new test cases targeting the surviving mutants.
- Re-run mutation testing until MS = 1.0.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** Always express MS as a **decimal (0.0 to 1.0)** OR as a **percentage** — both are accepted, but consistency matters.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Generating Mutants from Java Code

**Original Java Method (PIT):**

```java
public int grade(int score) {
    if (score >= 50) {
        return 1;     // Pass
    } else {
        return 0;     // Fail
    }
}
```

**Apply Mutation Operators — Show 5 Mutants:**

### Mutant 1 — ROR (Relational Operator Replacement)
```java
if (score > 50) {       // >= replaced with >
```

### Mutant 2 — CR (Constant Replacement)
```java
if (score >= 51) {      // 50 replaced with 51
```

### Mutant 3 — SDL (Statement Deletion — entire if-body)
```java
public int grade(int score) {
    return 0;
}
```

### Mutant 4 — UOI (Unary Operator Insertion on return)
```java
return -1;              // unary negation
```

### Mutant 5 — LOR (Logical Operator Replacement, if condition extended)
```java
if (score >= 50 && false) {  // 'true' inserted as second clause, then mutated
```

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Mutation Testing with JUnit 5 + PIT (Pitest)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Case Study Focus:**
> The integration of JUnit with **PIT (Pitest)** is the canonical academic case study.

**Step 1 — Add Maven dependencies (`pom.xml`):**

```xml
<properties>
    <pitest.version>1.15.8</pitest.version>
</properties>

<dependencies>
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.junit.jupiter</groupId>
        <artifactId>junit-jupiter</artifactId>
        <version>5.10.0</version>
        <scope>test</scope>
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

<build>
    <plugins>
        <plugin>
            <groupId>org.pitest</groupId>
            <artifactId>pitest-maven</artifactId>
            <version>${pitest.version}</version>
            <executions>
                <execution>
                    <goals>
                        <goal>mutationCoverage</goal>
                    </goals>
                </execution>
            </executions>
        </plugin>
    </plugins>
</build>
```

**Step 2 — Write the Production Code (`Grade.java`):**

```java
public class Grade {
    public String classify(int score) {
        if (score >= 90) return "A";
        else if (score >= 75) return "B";
        else if (score >= 50) return "C";
        else return "F";
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Write the JUnit Test Suite (`GradeTest.java`):**

```java
import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.*;

class GradeTest {

    @Test
    void testGradeA() {
        assertEquals("A", new Grade().classify(95));
    }

    @Test
    void testGradeB() {
        assertEquals("B", new Grade().classify(80));
    }

    @Test
    void testGradeC() {
        assertEquals("C", new Grade().classify(60));
    }

    @Test
    void testGradeF() {
        assertEquals("F", new Grade().classify(30));
    }
}
```

**Step 4 — Run the mutation test from CLI:**

```bash
mvn test
mvn org.pitest:pitest-maven:mutationCoverage
```

**Step 5 — Read the HTML report (produced in `target/pit-reports/index.html`):**

| Mutant Operator | Mutations | Killed | Survived | MS Line | MS Branch |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ROR | 8 | 8 | 0 | 100% | 100% |
| CR | 12 | 10 | 2 | 83% | 100% |
| **TOTAL** | **20** | **18** | **2** | **90%** | **100%** |

**Step 6 — Compute Mutation Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MS_{total} &= \dfrac{\vert K \vert}{\vert T \vert - \vert E \vert} \\[6pt]
           &= \dfrac{18}{20 - 0} \\[6pt]
           &= 0.90 = 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Strengthen the test suite:**

```java
// New test to kill the 2 surviving mutants (score = 89 and score = 49 boundary)
@Test
void testGradeBBoundary() {
    assertEquals("B", new Grade().classify(89));   // Kills CR: 75 → 76
}
@Test
void testGradeCBoundary() {
    assertEquals("C", new Grade().classify(49));   // Kills ROR: < 50 → <= 50
}
```

After adding these, **MS becomes 100%**, and the test suite is **mutation adequate**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — AI-Enhanced Test Case Automation (Conceptual Walkthrough)

**Pipeline Architecture:**

1. **Input Layer:** Source code + existing test suite + change history from Git.
2. **AI Engine:**
   - **Module A — Static Analysis:** LLM parses the AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) of the code.
   - **Module B — LLM Test Generation:** GPT-4 / Codex generates candidate test cases in JSON.
   - **Module C — Symbolic Executor (SPF / KLEE):** Generates boundary inputs.
   - **Module D — SBST Optimizer:** Uses a Genetic Algorithm to evolve the test suite towards **higher branch coverage**.
3. **Validation Layer:** JUnit runs each candidate test; passing ones are added.
4. **Feedback Loop:** Survived mutants from mutation testing feed back as additional training signal for the LLM.

**Mathematical Representation of SBST Fitness Function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(test_{suite}) &= w_1 \cdot C_{branch} + w_2 \cdot C_{line} + w_3 \cdot MS \\[6pt]
\text{where} \quad w_1 + w_2 + w_3 &= 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

The GA evolves test suites maximizing $F$.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 Note: When asked about AI-test automation, mention **at least 3 of these techniques** — SBST, Symbolic Execution, and LLM-based generation. Always give a one-line definition each.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Dimension | Mutation Testing | AI-Enhanced Test Automation |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Measure test suite quality | Auto-generate & prioritize tests |
| **Metric** | Mutation Score (MS) | Coverage + AI confidence score |
| **Automation** | High (with PIT) | Very High (with LLMs/GA) |
| **Manual Effort** | Medium (handling equivalent mutants) | Low |
| **Cost** | Linear in mutants × tests | Compute-heavy (GPU for LLMs) |
| **Strength** | Strong fault-finding signal | Scales to large codebases |
| **Weakness** | Equivalent mutants, slow | Hallucinated tests by LLMs |
| **Tool Examples** | Pitest, MuJava, Major | EvoSuite, Randoop, Codex, KLEE |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mutation Testing Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Select Program Under Test PIT] --> B[Apply Mutation Operators]
    B --> C[Generate Mutant Pool M1, M2, ... Mn]
    C --> D{Run Test Suite Against Each Mutant}
    D --> E[Test Failed]
    D --> F[Test Passed]
    E --> G[Killed Mutant]
    F --> H{Same Behavior as Original?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Equivalent Mutant Discard]
    H -->|No| J[Survived Mutant Record]
    G --> K[Aggregate Results]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compute Mutation Score MS]
    L --> M{MS = 100%?}
    M -->|No| N[Add Tests Targeting Survived Mutants]
    N --> B
    M -->|Yes| O[Mutation Adequate Test Suite Achieved]
```

## 4.2 AI-Enhanced Test Automation Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        SRC[Source Code]
        HIST[Git Change History]
        EXIST[Existing Test Suite]
    end

    subgraph AIEngine[AI Engine Core]
        LLM[LLM Test Generator GPT Codex]
        SYM[Symbolic Executor SPF KLEE]
        SBST[Search Based SBST GA HillClimb]
        MLP[ML Test Prioritizer XGBoost]
    end

    subgraph ValidationLayer[Validation Layer]
        JUNIT[JUnit Test Runner]
        PIT[PIT Mutation Feedback]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        GEN[Generated Test Cases]
        RANK[Ranked Test Suite]
        REPORT[Coverage and Mutation Report]
    end

    SRC --> LLM
    SRC --> SYM
    SRC --> SBST
    HIST --> MLP
    EXIST --> MLP
    LLM --> JUNIT
    SYM --> JUNIT
    SBST --> JUNIT
    JUNIT --> PIT
    PIT -->|Survived Mutants| LLM
    JUNIT --> GEN
    MLP --> RANK
    PIT --> REPORT
```

## 4.3 Mutation Operator Classification (Mermaid Concept Map)

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Mutation Operators))
    Arithmetic
      AOR
        Plus to Minus
        Times to Divide
    Relational
      ROR
        Greater to Less
        Equal to NotEqual
    Logical
      LOR
        AND to OR
        OR to AND
    Constant
      CR
        Zero to One
        Five to Six
    Statement Level
      SDL
        Delete Statement
      UOI
        Insert Increment
    Variable
      SVR
        Replace Variable Name
    Loop
      OBL
        Off By One in Loop Bound
```

## 4.4 AI Test Generation Pipeline Sequence (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DEV as Developer
    participant CI as CI Pipeline
    participant AI as AI Test Generator
    participant JU as JUnit Runner
    participant PIT as PIT Mutation Tool
    participant DB as Test Database

    DEV->>CI: Push Code Change
    CI->>AI: Trigger Test Generation
    AI->>AI: Analyze AST and Diff
    AI->>DB: Query Historical Patterns
    AI->>JU: Submit Candidate Tests
    JU->>PIT: Run Mutation Coverage
    PIT-->>CI: Return Mutation Report
    CI->>DEV: Display MS and Coverage
    DEV->>JU: Accept Refined Tests
    JU->>DB: Persist Tests for Future Runs
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define Mutation Testing. List any four mutation operators with one example each.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Mutation testing is a fault-based software testing technique in which small syntactic changes called **mutations** are introduced into the source code to create **mutants**. A test suite is considered effective if it can **distinguish (kill)** these mutants from the original program.

**Four Mutation Operators (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

| Operator | Transformation |
|---|---|
| **AOR (Arithmetic Operator Replacement)** | `+` → `-` |
| **ROR (Relational Operator Replacement)** | `>` → `>=` |
| **CR (Constant Replacement)** | `5` → `6` |
| **SDL (Statement Deletion)** | `x = x + 1;` → *(deleted)* |

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is Mutation Score? Explain the equivalent mutant problem.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Mutation Score (1.5 Marks):**
Mutation score is the ratio of mutants **killed** by the test suite to the total number of **non-equivalent mutants** generated. It is given by:

$$MS = \dfrac{\vert K \vert}{\vert T \vert - \vert E \vert}$$

where $K$ = killed mutants, $T$ = total mutants, $E$ = equivalent mutants. The value lies between **0 and 1**. A mutation score of **1.0** indicates the test suite is fully mutation adequate.

**Equivalent Mutant Problem (1.5 Marks):**
An equivalent mutant is a syntactically altered program that produces **identical output** for all possible inputs. For example, `x * 2` and `x + x` are equivalent for integers. Equivalent mutants **cannot be killed** and inflate the denominator, reducing the measured mutation score. Detecting them requires manual analysis, making mutation testing expensive and sometimes imprecise.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the complete **Mutation Testing Process**. Describe the role of mutation operators and the classification of mutants (Killed, Survived, Equivalent). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following Java method. Apply **at least five mutation operators** to generate mutants. For each mutant, write a JUnit test case that would **kill** it. **(7 Marks)**

```java
public boolean isEligible(int age, boolean hasLicense) {
    if (age >= 18 && hasLicense) {
        return true;
    }
    return false;
}
```

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks):

**Mutation Testing Process (4 Marks):**

The mutation testing process involves six steps:

1. **Selection of PIT:** The Program Under Test is identified — `isEligible(int age, boolean hasLicense)`.
2. **Application of Mutation Operators:** Transformation rules like AOR, ROR, LOR, SDL, CR are applied to the source code.
3. **Generation of Mutants:** Each operator applied to one location creates one mutant.
4. **Execution of Test Suite:** The same test suite is run against each mutant.
5. **Classification of Mutants:**
   - **Killed** — at least one test fails.
   - **Survived** — all tests pass.
   - **Equivalent** — semantic behavior unchanged.
   - **Timeout/Error** — treated as killed.
6. **Mutation Score Computation:** $MS = \dfrac{\vert K \vert}{\vert T \vert - \vert E \vert}$.

**Diagram (2 Marks):** Refer to **Section 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart** of these notes.

**Role of Mutation Operators (1 Mark):**
Mutation operators provide systematic, automated rules to introduce realistic faults. They ensure **coverage** of common programmer mistakes (arithmetic, logical, relational errors) and provide a **standardized** fault model.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks):

**Mutant Generation (4 Marks):**

| # | Mutant Operator | Mutant Code | JUnit Test That Kills It |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | **ROR** on `age >= 18` | `if (age > 18 && hasLicense)` | `assertFalse(isEligible(18, true));` — original returns true; mutant returns false → **KILLED** |
| M2 | **CR** on `18` | `if (age >= 19 && hasLicense)` | `assertTrue(isEligible(18, true));` — mutant returns false → **KILLED** |
| M3 | **LOR** on `&&` | `if (age >= 18 \|\| hasLicense)` | `assertFalse(isEligible(20, false));` — original returns false; mutant returns true → **KILLED** |
| M4 | **UOI** on `age` | `if (age++ >= 18 && hasLicense)` | Hard to kill via side-effect; usually caught by boundary test |
| M5 | **CR** on `true` | `if (age >= 18 && false)` | `assertTrue(isEligible(25, true));` — mutant returns false → **KILLED** |
| M6 | **SDL** of `return true;` | `return false;` | `assertTrue(isEligible(20, true));` — **KILLED** |

**JUnit Test Suite (3 Marks):**

```java
import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.*;

class EligibilityTest {

    @Test
    void testExactBoundaryAge() {
        // Kills M1 (ROR > instead of >=) and M2 (CR 19)
        assertTrue(new Eligibility().isEligible(18, true));
    }

    @Test
    void testLicenseMissing() {
        // Kills M3 (LOR || instead of &&)
        assertFalse(new Eligibility().isEligible(20, false));
    }

    @Test
    void testCorrectCase() {
        // Kills M6 (statement deletion)
        assertTrue(new Eligibility().isEligible(25, true));
    }

    @Test
    void testUnderAgeNoLicense() {
        assertFalse(new Eligibility().isEligible(15, false));
    }
}
```

**Mutation Score Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MS &= \dfrac{6}{6 - 0} = 1.00 = 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---
