topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to PEX - Symbolic execution, parameterized unit testing, symbolic execution trees, and their application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is PEX?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PEX (Program EXploration)** is an automated white-box **test generation tool** developed by **Microsoft Research** that uses **dynamic symbolic execution** (also called **concolic testing**) to systematically explore program paths and generate a minimal set of high-coverage parameterized unit tests.

PEX is integrated with the **.NET ecosystem** (Visual Studio) and is academically backed by research from **Nikolai Tillmann and Jonathan de Halleux** at Microsoft Research. It operates directly on **.NET assemblies** (MSIL/CIL bytecode) and treats every conditional branch as a decision point whose path feasibility must be mathematically explored.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PEX is covered as a flagship example of *automated test generation tools* that bridge the gap between symbolic reasoning and practical software testing.

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of PEX (as per Module 4 syllabus)

| # | Pillar | One-Line Definition |
|---|--------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Symbolic Execution** | A program analysis technique where program inputs are treated as **symbolic variables** rather than concrete values. |
| 2 | **Parameterized Unit Testing (PUT)** | An extension of traditional unit tests that accept **parameters** and an external **test data generator** (like PEX) to produce actual test inputs. |
| 3 | **Symbolic Execution Trees (SET)** | A tree-structured representation of all feasible program paths, where each node denotes a program state and each edge represents a branch decision. |
| 4 | **Application Areas** | Test generation, contract verification, bug finding, security testing, and regression test suite minimization. |

## 1.3 Intuitive Conceptual Analogies

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — Symbolic Execution as "Mathematical GPS Navigation"**
> Imagine you are driving a car in a city with **traffic signals at every junction**. A normal driver follows one path with concrete inputs (turn left at the 3rd signal). A **symbolic executor**, however, asks: *"If I treat every turn as a variable `t₁, t₂, t₃…`, can I find a path that visits ALL possible signal combinations?"* It mathematically solves each junction's constraints using an **SMT solver** (like Z3) to compute concrete input values that would force each unexplored path.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — Parameterized Unit Testing as "A Blank Exam Paper with a Solver"**
> A traditional unit test is like an exam paper where every question already has fixed numbers (e.g., *compute `Add(2,3)`*). A **Parameterized Unit Test (PUT)** is like a paper that says *"compute `Add(x, y)` for ANY valid `x` and `y`"* — but the actual numbers come from an external generator (PEX). PEX picks numbers that maximize **branch coverage**.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 3 — Symbolic Execution Tree as "A Decision Tree of Program Reality"**
> Picture a **flowchart** where every `if` statement is a fork in the road. The **Symbolic Execution Tree** is this flowchart enriched with **path constraints** (mathematical conditions) at each branch. The tree's **leaves** represent concrete terminal states that the program can reach.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in PEX

- **Branch Coverage (%)** — Percentage of conditional branches executed.
- **Path Coverage** — Percentage of feasible paths traversed.
- **PUT Success Ratio** — Ratio of unique test inputs generated to total branches.
- **Constraint Solving Time (ms)** — Time taken by the Z3 SMT solver per query.
- **Maximum Tree Depth (`D_max`)** — The depth of the deepest path in the Symbolic Execution Tree.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Symbolic Execution — Formal Operational Model

Symbolic execution maintains two key state components at every program point:

1. **Symbolic Store (σ):** A mapping from program variables to symbolic expressions.
   $$\sigma: \text{Var} \rightarrow \text{SymbolicExpr}$$
2. **Path Constraint (π):** A quantifier-free first-order formula over symbolic inputs.
   $$\pi: \text{BooleanFormula over } \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$$

### The Operational Semantics (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> For each program statement, the symbolic executor applies a formal transition rule:

**Step 1 — Assignment Statement** `x = e`:
- Update σ so that `σ(x) ← σ(e)`.
- Path constraint π remains unchanged.
- **Why?** Because assignment simply rebinds a variable to a new symbolic expression.

**Step 2 — Conditional Branch** `if (c) then S1 else S2`:
- **Then-branch:** Fork state → add `σ(c)` as a conjunct to π (i.e., `π ← π ∧ σ(c)`).
- **Else-branch:** Fork state → add `¬σ(c)` as a conjunct to π (i.e., `π ← π ∧ ¬σ(c)`).
- **Why?** Because each branch is reachable only when its logical guard holds.

**Step 3 — Constraint Negation at Backtracking:**
- When the executor backtracks to cover an unexplored branch, it **negates** one atomic predicate in π and queries the **SMT solver** for a satisfying model.
- **Why?** Negation produces the path that leads to the *opposite* side of the most recent branch — a key heuristic driving branch coverage.

**Step 4 — Termination / Error Sink:**
- A leaf is reached when the program halts, throws, or returns. The current σ and π are reported as a *concrete test case* if π is SAT.

## 2.2 Parameterized Unit Testing (PUT) — Formal Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Parameterized Unit Test (PUT)** is a triplet:
> $$\text{PUT} = \langle \mathcal{P}, \tau, \rho \rangle$$
> where:
> - $\mathcal{P}$ — A generic method with **typed parameters** (the *test logic*).
> - $\tau$ — A **test data provider** (e.g., PEX) supplying concrete input values.
> - $\rho$ — A **contract** (precondition, postcondition, invariant) describing expected behavior.

### Why PUT is Superior to Classical Unit Tests

| Classical Unit Test | Parameterized Unit Test (PUT) |
|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| Hard-coded inputs (`int x = 5;`) | Symbolic parameters (`int x`) |
| Tests one execution path | Tests *families* of executions |
| Human-written test cases | Machine-generated test cases |
| Cannot scale beyond a few cases | Scales to thousands of cases via Z3 solver |

## 2.3 Symbolic Execution Tree (SET) — Mathematical Formulation

A **Symbolic Execution Tree** is a directed tree $\mathcal{T} = (N, E)$ where:

- Each **node** $n \in N$ corresponds to a program state $s_n = (\text{pc}_n, \sigma_n, \pi_n)$.
- Each **edge** $e \in E$ is labeled with a path predicate (true/false outcome of a branch).
- **Internal nodes** represent branch decisions; **leaf nodes** represent program termination or uncaught exceptions.

### Tree Properties

- **Branching Factor (b):** Equals the arity of the most complex conditional (typically 2 for binary `if`).
- **Tree Depth (D):** Equals the maximum number of branches on any root-to-leaf path.
- **Feasibility Set:** The set of leaves whose accumulated path constraint π is **SAT** (satisfiable).
- **Infeasible Subtrees:** Subtrees whose π is **UNSAT** (no satisfying input exists) are pruned — these are called *dead* branches.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Symbol | Meaning |
|---------|------------------|---------|
| Branch Coverage | $BC = \frac{B_{\text{executed}}}{B_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | % of branches traversed |
| Path Constraint | $\pi = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{k} c_i$ | Conjunction of all guards on a path |
| Symbolic Store Update | $\sigma(x) \leftarrow \sigma(e)$ | Assignment semantics |
| Tree Size (upper bound) | $\vert \mathcal{T} \vert \leq b^{D}$ | Worst-case exponential paths |
| Negation Heuristic | $\pi' = \pi \setminus \{c_j\} \cup \{\neg c_j\}$ | Flip the *j*-th branch |
| PUT Cardinality | $\vert \text{PUT} \vert = \vert \tau(\mathcal{P}) \vert$ | Number of generated tests |
| Path Feasibility | $\text{SAT}(\pi)$ | Solver check |
| Test Generation Yield | $Y = \frac{\vert \text{unique paths covered} \vert}{\vert \text{calls to solver} \vert}$ | Solver efficiency metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Symbolic execution trees are used in:
> - **Cybersecurity** — detecting buffer overflows, SQL injection patterns.
> - **Embedded Systems** — exhaustive branch validation in safety-critical avionics (DO-178C).
> - **Compiler Optimization** — dead-code elimination uses infeasible-path detection.
> - **API Fuzzing** — generating boundary inputs for cloud microservices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Symbolic Execution of a Simple Method

Consider the following C# method (the canonical PEX tutorial example):

```csharp
public static int Check(int x, int y)
{
    if (x < 0) return -1;
    if (y == 0) return 0;
    if (x + y > 100) return 2;
    return 1;
}
```

**Initial Symbolic State:**

$$
\sigma_0 = \{x \mapsto x,\ y \mapsto y\}, \quad \pi_0 = \text{true}
$$

We denote the symbolic inputs as `x` and `y` (lowercase), distinct from the program variables.

---

### Path 1 — `x < 0` is TRUE

**Step 1:** Branch 1 takes the *then* path. Add `x < 0` to π:

$$
\pi_1 = \pi_0 \wedge (x < 0) \Rightarrow \pi_1 = (x < 0)
$$

**Step 2:** Method returns `-1` immediately. Concrete test case generated:

$$
\tau_1 = \{x = -5,\ y = 0\}
$$

The SMT solver (Z3) produces the model above. **Branch 1 covered.**

---

### Path 2 — `x < 0` is FALSE, then `y == 0` is TRUE

**Step 1:** Branch 1 takes *else*. Negate the constraint:

$$
\pi_2 = (x \geq 0)
$$

**Step 2:** Branch 2 takes *then* (`y == 0`):

$$
\pi_2 = (x \geq 0) \wedge (y == 0)
$$

**Step 3:** Method returns `0`. Concrete test case:

$$
\tau_2 = \{x = 1,\ y = 0\}
$$

---

### Path 3 — `x < 0` FALSE, `y == 0` FALSE, `x + y > 100` TRUE

**Step 1:** Branch 1 *else*:

$$
\pi_3 = (x \geq 0)
$$

**Step 2:** Branch 2 *else*:

$$
\pi_3 = (x \geq 0) \wedge (y \neq 0)
$$

**Step 3:** Branch 3 *then*:

$$
\pi_3 = (x \geq 0) \wedge (y \neq 0) \wedge (x + y > 100)
$$

**Step 4:** Method returns `2`. Concrete test case:

$$
\tau_3 = \{x = 80,\ y = 30\}
$$

---

### Path 4 — All branches FALSE → returns 1

**Step 1:** Branch 1 *else*:

$$
\pi_4 = (x \geq 0)
$$

**Step 2:** Branch 2 *else*:

$$
\pi_4 = (x \geq 0) \wedge (y \neq 0)
$$

**Step 3:** Branch 3 *else*:

$$
\pi_4 = (x \geq 0) \wedge (y \neq 0) \wedge (x + y \leq 100)
$$

**Step 4:** Concrete test case:

$$
\tau_4 = \{x = 10,\ y = 20\}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Final Result

| Path | Path Constraint $\pi$ | Solver Model | Return | Status |
|------|----------------------|--------------|--------|--------|
| P1 | $(x < 0)$ | $x = -5,\ y = 0$ | $-1$ | SAT |
| P2 | $(x \geq 0) \wedge (y == 0)$ | $x = 1,\ y = 0$ | $0$ | SAT |
| P3 | $(x \geq 0) \wedge (y \neq 0) \wedge (x + y > 100)$ | $x = 80,\ y = 30$ | $2$ | SAT |
| P4 | $(x \geq 0) \wedge (y \neq 0) \wedge (x + y \leq 100)$ | $x = 10,\ y = 20$ | $1$ | SAT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** With only **3 conditional branches**, PEX produced **4 concrete test cases** achieving **100% branch coverage** without any human-written test data. This is the central power of symbolic execution.

## 3.3 Construction of the Symbolic Execution Tree (SET) — Formal Walkthrough

For the same `Check(int x, int y)` method, the SET is constructed as follows:

### Level 0 (Root)
- Node $N_0$: Program entry.
  $$\sigma_0 = \{x \mapsto x,\ y \mapsto y\}, \quad \pi_0 = \text{true}$$

### Level 1 (After Branch 1: `if (x < 0)`)
- Left child $N_1$: taken when $\pi_1 = (x < 0)$.
- Right child $N_2$: taken when $\pi_2 = (x \geq 0)$.

### Level 2 (After Branch 2: `if (y == 0)`, only from $N_2$)
- Left child $N_3$: $\pi_3 = (x \geq 0) \wedge (y == 0)$.
- Right child $N_4$: $\pi_4 = (x \geq 0) \wedge (y \neq 0)$.

### Level 3 (After Branch 3: `if (x + y > 100)`, only from $N_4$)
- Left child $N_5$: $\pi_5 = (x \geq 0) \wedge (y \neq 0) \wedge (x + y > 100)$.
- Right child $N_6$: $\pi_6 = (x \geq 0) \wedge (y \neq 0) \wedge (x + y \leq 100)$.

### Leaves
- $N_1$: returns $-1$ (path 1).
- $N_3$: returns $0$ (path 2).
- $N_5$: returns $2$ (path 3).
- $N_6$: returns $1$ (path 4).
- **Total leaves = 4**, total nodes = 7, branching factor $b = 2$.

## 3.4 Algebraic Derivation: Branch Coverage Lower Bound

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (PEX Coverage Guarantee):** For an *acyclic* program with $k$ independent conditional branches, PEX guarantees a branch coverage of:
> $$BC_{\text{PEX}} = \frac{2^k - U}{2^k} \times 100\%$$
> where $U$ is the number of infeasible (UNSAT) paths.

**Derivation for our `Check` example:**

$$
BC_{\text{PEX}} = \frac{2^3 - 0}{2^3} \times 100\% = \frac{8}{8} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

This matches our analytical matrix — all 3 branches were traversed on both sides (note that one branch is short-circuited, so the effective coverage is computed over actually-reached branches).

## 3.5 The Negation Heuristic — Deep Dive

PEX uses the **"flip last unexplored branch"** heuristic. Formally, given a current path constraint π and an explored sequence of branch outcomes $B = (b_1, b_2, \dots, b_k)$, the next query is constructed as:

$$
\pi_{\text{next}} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{k-1} b_i \wedge \neg b_k
$$

This generates a sibling path. The process iterates **depth-first** until all SAT leaves are exhausted or a **search bound** (default: 100 iterations per PUT) is reached.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level PEX Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code .NET Assembly] --> B[PEX Instrumentation Engine]
    B --> C[Build Initial Symbolic State sigma0, pi0]
    C --> D[Interpret MSIL Bytecode]
    D --> E[Encounter Conditional Branch]
    E --> F{Constraint Solver Z3 SAT}
    F -- Yes, Then-branch --> G[Record Concrete Test Case 1]
    F -- No --> H[Negate Constraint pi prime]
    H --> I[Query Z3 Solver for Model]
    I --> J[Record Concrete Test Case 2]
    G --> K[Update Symbolic Execution Tree SET]
    J --> K
    K --> L{All Branches Covered?}
    L -- No --> D
    L -- Yes --> M[Emit Parameterized Unit Test PUT Suite]
    M --> N[Save .cs Test File for MSTest or NUnit]
```

## 4.2 Symbolic Execution Tree (SET) Diagram for `Check(x, y)`

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeN0["N0: Entry sigma0 = x to x, y to y, pi0 = true"]
    nodeN1["N1: pi = x less than 0, Return -1"]
    nodeN2["N2: pi = x greater or equal 0"]
    nodeN3["N3: pi = x greater or equal 0 AND y equals 0, Return 0"]
    nodeN4["N4: pi = x greater or equal 0 AND y not equal 0"]
    nodeN5["N5: pi = x greater or equal 0 AND y not equal 0 AND x+y greater than 100, Return 2"]
    nodeN6["N6: pi = x greater or equal 0 AND y not equal 0 AND x+y less or equal 100, Return 1"]
    nodeN0 -- "if x less than 0 : true" --> nodeN1
    nodeN0 -- "if x less than 0 : false" --> nodeN2
    nodeN2 -- "if y equals 0 : true" --> nodeN3
    nodeN2 -- "if y equals 0 : false" --> nodeN4
    nodeN4 -- "if x+y greater than 100 : true" --> nodeN5
    nodeN4 -- "if x+y greater than 100 : false" --> nodeN6
```

## 4.3 PUT Architecture — Interaction Between Test Code and PEX

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Dev["Developer Writes PUT Logic P"] --> PEX["PEX Engine"]
    PEX --> Solver["Z3 SMT Solver"]
    Solver --> Model["Satisfying Model: Concrete Inputs"]
    Model --> PEX
    PEX --> TestSuite["Generated Test Suite: tau of P"]
    TestSuite --> Runner["MSTest / NUnit / xUnit Runner"]
    Runner --> Report["Coverage Report and Bug Detection"]
    Contracts["Code Contracts: Pre, Post, Invariants"] --> PEX
    PEX --> Contracts
```

## 4.4 The Exploration Algorithm (Pseudocode Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: PUT P with parameters"] --> init["Initialize sigma, pi = true, frontier = empty"]
    init --> run["Run P with arbitrary concrete inputs e.g. all zeros"]
    run --> log1["Log execution path and update frontier"]
    log1 --> whileCheck{"Frontier non-empty?"}
    whileCheck -- Yes --> select["Pick last unexplored branch b from frontier"]
    select --> neg["Negate b in pi to obtain pi prime"]
    neg --> solverCheck{"Z3: SAT pi prime?"}
    solverCheck -- Yes --> newRun["Run P with new concrete model"]
    newRun --> log2["Log new path, update frontier"]
    log2 --> whileCheck
    solverCheck -- No --> prune["Mark b as infeasible, prune subtree"]
    prune --> whileCheck
    whileCheck -- No --> emit["Emit all logged paths as test suite"]
    emit --> end["End"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architecture Insight:** Notice the **bidirectional arrow** between PEX and "Code Contracts" in the PUT diagram. PEX honors `.NET Code Contracts` — when a postcondition fails on a generated test, PEX automatically **minimizes the failing input** (delta-debugging) before reporting it, dramatically simplifying bug triage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q: Define symbolic execution. How does it differ from concrete execution?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Symbolic execution is a program analysis technique in which program inputs are treated as **symbolic variables** (e.g., `x`, `y`) rather than concrete values, and program execution is performed by maintaining a **symbolic store (σ)** and **path constraint (π)**. Each conditional branch produces a new path constraint by conjoining the symbolic guard.

> **Difference from Concrete Execution:**
> - **Concrete execution** runs the program with **specific numeric values** (e.g., `x = 5`) and observes one path.
> - **Symbolic execution** runs the program with **placeholders** and reasons over **all feasible paths simultaneously**, using an SMT solver to produce concrete test inputs that satisfy each path's accumulated constraints.

*(Marking scheme: Definition 1m, σ & π explanation 1m, Difference 1m.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

**Q: What is a Parameterized Unit Test (PUT)? List two advantages over conventional unit tests.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Parameterized Unit Test (PUT)** is a test method that accepts **typed input parameters** instead of hard-coded values. The actual test data is supplied by an external **test data provider** (e.g., PEX, NUnit's `TestCase` attribute).

> **Advantages over Conventional Unit Tests:**
> 1. **Scalability:** PUTs enable automatic generation of hundreds of test cases, achieving higher branch coverage than human-written tests.
> 2. **Decoupling of logic and data:** Test logic (the method body) is separated from test inputs, improving maintainability — when logic changes, data need not be rewritten.

*(Marking scheme: Definition 1.5m, Two advantages 0.75m each.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **confuse PEX (a tool) with symbolic execution (a technique)**. PEX *uses* symbolic execution. Do not interchange.
> - When asked to construct a path constraint, students often **omit the negation** of the prior path's branch. Always negate the last unexplored branch's predicate.
> - In PUT questions, students forget to mention the **external test data provider** — this is a *mandatory* component of the formal definition.
> - Drawing a Symbolic Execution Tree: students draw it like a control flow graph — remember, SETs must include **path constraints (π) inside each node**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With a suitable example, explain the concept of symbolic execution. Also construct the symbolic execution tree and identify the path constraints for each leaf node. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Symbolic Execution Explained (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Symbolic execution is a static-dynamic analysis technique where program variables are replaced with **symbolic expressions** of the input parameters. The executor maintains:

- A **Symbolic Store σ** mapping each program variable to a symbolic expression.
- A **Path Constraint π**, a quantifier-free boolean formula over the symbolic inputs.

**Example:** Consider the method:

```csharp
public int Compute(int a, int b)
{
    if (a > 0) {
        if (b > 10) return a + b;
        else return a - b;
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Symbolic Execution Trace:**

- **Initial:** $\sigma_0 = \{a \mapsto a,\ b \mapsto b\}$, $\pi_0 = \text{true}$.
- **Branch 1 (`a > 0` TRUE):** $\pi_1 = (a > 0)$.
  - **Branch 2 (`b > 10` TRUE):** $\pi_{1a} = (a > 0) \wedge (b > 10)$, return `a + b`.
  - **Branch 2 (`b > 10` FALSE):** $\pi_{1b} = (a > 0) \wedge (b \leq 10)$, return `a - b`.
- **Branch 1 (`a > 0` FALSE):** $\pi_2 = (a \leq 0)$, return `0`.

**Solver-generated concrete tests:**
- $\tau_{1a} = \{a = 5, b = 20\}$, expects $25$.
- $\tau_{1b} = \{a = 5, b = 5\}$, expects $0$.
- $\tau_2 = \{a = -3, b = 5\}$, expects $0$.

#### Part (b) — Symbolic Execution Tree + Path Constraints (7 marks)

```
                       [N0: σ0={a↦a, b↦b}, π0=true]
                      /                                 \
              [N1: π=(a>0)]                        [N2: π=(a≤0)]
              /            \                          |
       [N3: π=(a>0)∧(b>10)]  [N4: π=(a>0)∧(b≤10)]    [N5: π=(a≤0)]
       returns a+b            returns a-b            returns 0
```

**Path Constraint Table:**

| Leaf Node | Path Constraint π | Concrete Model |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| N3 | $(a > 0) \wedge (b > 10)$ | $a=5, b=20$ |
| N4 | $(a > 0) \wedge (b \leq 10)$ | $a=5, b=5$ |
| N5 | $(a \leq 0)$ | $a=-3, b=0$ |

**Conclusion:** The symbolic execution tree enumerates all feasible paths, and PEX's Z3 solver instantiates concrete inputs that satisfy each path constraint — achieving 100% branch coverage with just 3 generated tests.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: Discuss Parameterized Unit Testing (PUT) in detail. How does PEX use PUT together with symbolic execution trees for automated test generation? List at least four real-world applications. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — PUT Formalism & PEX Integration (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A **Parameterized Unit Test (PUT)** is a generic test method whose signature contains parameters, and whose body treats those parameters as placeholders. Formally:

$$\text{PUT} = \langle \mathcal{P}(\text{params}),\ \tau,\ \rho \rangle$$

where $\mathcal{P}$ is the test logic, $\tau$ is the test data generator (PEX), and $\rho$ represents the **contracts** (preconditions, postconditions, invariants) declared via .NET Code Contracts.

**Example of a PUT:**

```csharp
[PexMethod]
public void PutCheck(int x, int y)
{
    int result = Check(x, y);
    PexAssert.IsTrue(result >= -1 && result <= 2);
}
```

Here, `[PexMethod]` is the **attribute** that marks a method as a PUT. PEX recognizes this and invokes the symbolic execution engine.

**How PEX integrates PUT with Symbolic Execution Trees:**

1. PEX **reads the PUT signature** to discover the parameter types (`int x`, `int y`).
2. PEX **interprets the IL bytecode** of the called methods to construct a symbolic execution tree.
3. At each branch node of the SET, PEX **queries the Z3 SMT solver** with the negated path constraint.
4. Satisfying models become **concrete test inputs**, which PEX **emits as a `.cs` file** containing `[PexGeneratedTest]` methods.
5. The emitted tests can be re-run with standard frameworks (MSTest, xUnit, NUnit).

#### Part (b) — Real-World Applications (7 marks)

**Four Real-World Applications of PEX / Symbolic Execution:**

| # | Application Domain | Description |
|---|--------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Security Vulnerability Detection** | Symbolic execution can identify SQL injection, buffer overflows, and integer overflow vulnerabilities by exploring paths where attacker-controlled inputs bypass sanitization. |
| 2 | **Contract Verification** | When methods are annotated with .NET Code Contracts (e.g., `Contract.Requires(x > 0)`), PEX can systematically search for inputs that violate postconditions. |
| 3 | **Automated Regression Testing** | Generated test suites serve as a regression baseline; future code changes can be re-validated against the same PEX-generated inputs. |
| 4 | **API Boundary Testing** | PEX is extensively used to test web API endpoints and library APIs by feeding boundary inputs (null, max-int, empty strings). |

**Bonus Applications (for higher marks):**
- **Compiler optimization verification** — confirming that compiler-introduced transformations preserve semantics.
- **Model-based testing** — symbolic execution generates test cases that satisfy state-machine transition guards.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **PEX** is a *tool*, **symbolic execution** is a *technique*, and **PUT** is a *test paradigm* — do not interchange.
- ✅ Every symbolic execution state maintains **two components**: symbolic store **σ** and path constraint **π**.
- ✅ Branch coverage is achieved by **negating one branch predicate at a time** and querying the **SMT solver (Z3)**.
- ✅ The **Symbolic Execution Tree (SET)** is a tree of program states; internal nodes represent branches, leaves represent terminal/exceptional states.
- ✅ **Parameterized Unit Tests (PUTs)** decouple test *logic* from test *data*, enabling machine-generated high-coverage test suites.
- ✅ PEX honors **.NET Code Contracts**; failing postconditions are auto-minimized via **delta-debugging**.
- ✅ Worst-case SET size is **exponential** ($b^D$); loops and recursion cause **path explosion** — handled by bounded search and infeasible-path pruning.
- ✅ Applications span **security testing, contract verification, regression testing, and API boundary testing**.
- ✅ PEX works on **MSIL/CIL bytecode** of .NET assemblies, not source code directly.
- ✅ Default PEX exploration bound is typically **100 iterations per PUT** (configurable).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GenAI in Testing - Advanced use cases for predictive and responsive testing across devices and environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** GenAI in Testing - Advanced use cases for predictive and responsive testing across devices and environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

**Generative AI (GenAI) in Software Testing** is the application of large-scale generative machine learning models — primarily **Large Language Models (LLMs)**, **Transformer architectures**, **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)**, **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)**, and **Diffusion Models** — to autonomously *create*, *predict*, *adapt*, and *orchestrate* test artifacts (test cases, test data, test scripts, defect predictions, environment configurations) across the entire software test lifecycle.

When applied to **predictive and responsive testing across devices and environments**, GenAI is leveraged for two synergistic objectives:

1. **Predictive Testing** — *Anticipatory* test execution driven by learned defect-probability distributions, code-change risk scoring, and historical failure-pattern mining. The objective is to allocate test effort to the regions of the system most likely to fail *before* they actually fail.
2. **Responsive Testing** — *Adaptive* test execution that dynamically reacts to runtime signals (UI shifts, API drift, network latency variance, OS-level differences, device fragmentation). Self-healing locators, environment-aware orchestration, and responsive assertions are the canonical manifestations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Examination-Ready):**
> *"Predictive testing uses AI-derived probability models to forecast defect-prone modules prior to execution, while responsive testing uses AI-driven adaptation mechanisms to maintain test validity across heterogeneous devices, browsers, operating systems, and network environments."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Weather Forecasting for Bugs" Analogy**

Imagine you are a **meteorologist** responsible for predicting where cyclones will strike a country. You do not wait for the cyclone to form — you ingest satellite data, historical storm tracks, sea-surface temperatures, and atmospheric pressure maps to **forecast** the cyclone's path 72 hours in advance. You then pre-position emergency teams in those predicted zones.

GenAI in testing performs the **exact same role**:
- **Satellite data** → Historical defect logs, code commits, test execution traces, crash reports.
- **Forecast model** → Transformer/LLM trained on the above corpus.
- **Cyclone path** → Predicted high-defect-probability modules, files, or user journeys.
- **Pre-positioned emergency teams** → Prioritized, targeted, AI-generated test suites.
- **Re-routing during the storm** → Responsive (self-healing) tests that adapt when the UI/API "shifts" mid-storm.

For **cross-device / cross-environment** extension: a cyclone behaves differently over land vs. ocean vs. mountains. Likewise, an application behaves differently on **Android 14 + Chrome 124** vs. **iOS 17 + Safari 17** vs. **Windows 11 + Edge 124** vs. a **low-bandwidth 3G connection on a budget Android device**. GenAI learns these "terrain behaviors" and generates environment-specific test variants automatically.

### 1.3 Canonical Metrics & Standard Measurements

The following metrics are **bolded** as they are standard deliverables in KTU valuation:

- **Defect Prediction Precision (DPP)** — fraction of files flagged "defect-prone" that actually contain defects.
- **Defect Prediction Recall (DPR)** — fraction of true defective files that the model successfully flagged.
- **Self-Healing Success Rate (SHSR)** — percentage of broken locators automatically repaired without manual intervention.
- **Cross-Environment Parity Score (CEPS)** — equivalence metric measuring behavioral consistency across device/environment permutations.
- **Test Execution Reduction Ratio (TERR)** — percentage reduction in redundant test executions achieved via AI prioritization.
- **Mean Time to Adapt (MTTA)** — average latency between an environment drift event and successful test re-stabilization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECST631 Module 4):** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly positions GenAI as an *advanced, evaluative* topic. Students must be able to differentiate predictive vs. responsive paradigms, name the AI techniques involved, and justify test-strategy decisions using the metrics above.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars: Predictive vs. Responsive Testing — Operational Breakdown

**Pillar A — Predictive Testing (Anticipation Layer)**

Operational logic, decomposed:

- **Step 1 — Feature Extraction.** Historical repository data (commits, churn, complexity metrics such as cyclomatic complexity, code ownership graphs) is vectorized into numerical embeddings.
- **Step 2 — Pattern Mining.** Transformer-based or gradient-boosted models learn conditional probability distributions $P(\text{defect} \mid \text{features})$.
- **Step 3 — Risk Scoring.** Each new commit, file, or module is scored in real time; a threshold $\tau$ partitions the codebase into *high-risk* and *low-risk* zones.
- **Step 4 — Test Prioritization.** Test cases mapped to high-risk zones are promoted to earlier execution slots in the CI/CD pipeline.
- **Step 5 — Continuous Feedback Loop.** Post-execution defect data is fed back into the training corpus, enabling online/incremental learning.

**Pillar B — Responsive Testing (Adaptation Layer)**

Operational logic, decomposed:

- **Step 1 — Drift Detection.** Visual (pixel-diff via CNNs), DOM (tree-edit-distance), API (schema-diff via LLMs), and behavioral diffs are continuously monitored.
- **Step 2 — Root-Cause Inference.** The GenAI model classifies the drift type: cosmetic, structural, semantic, or environmental.
- **Step 3 — Self-Healing / Regeneration.** Broken XPath/CSS locators are re-synthesized; obsolete assertions are rewritten; obsolete test data is regenerated.
- **Step 4 — Cross-Environment Variant Synthesis.** The model generates environment-specific test variants (e.g., lower-DPI screen renders, RTL locales, low-bandwidth modes).
- **Step 5 — Orchestration & Reporting.** Cloud device farms are dynamically queried, and a parity score is reported back to QA dashboards.

### 2.2 Why & How — The Engineering Rationale

| Pillar | **Why** it exists | **How** GenAI enables it |
|--------|--------------------|---------------------------|
| Predictive | Combinatorial explosion of test space; manual risk analysis is unscalable | Learns latent defect signatures from history and generalizes to unseen code |
| Responsive | UI/API drift, device fragmentation (3000+ Android models), OS upgrades break tests | Generates *new* test logic on demand rather than executing *fixed* test logic |
| Cross-Device | Identical business logic must behave identically on N device × M OS × K browser permutations | Synthetic environment-aware test data + visual/layout regression via generative vision models |
| Cross-Network | Latency, packet loss, offline mode, low bandwidth require protocol-aware tests | GenAI simulates network profiles and generates resilient assertions (e.g., tolerant of 2G delays) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-portable**. Memorize the formula structures and metric definitions. *Note: vertical bars for absolute value / cardinality are rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.*

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula | Boundary / Range | Engineering Use |
|---|------------------|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Defect Prediction Precision | $\text{DPP} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$, ideal $= 1$ | Measures false-alarm cost of AI flags |
| 2 | Defect Prediction Recall | $\text{DPR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$, ideal $= 1$ | Measures missed-defect cost |
| 3 | F1-Score (harmonic mean) | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{DPP} \cdot \text{DPR}}{\text{DPP} + \text{DPR}}$ | $[0, 1]$, ideal $= 1$ | Balanced DPP/DPR trade-off |
| 4 | Self-Healing Success Rate | $\text{SHSR} = \dfrac{\vert H_{\text{success}} \vert}{\vert H_{\text{total}} \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$, target $\geq 0.90$ | Measures autonomy of responsive layer |
| 5 | Cross-Environment Parity Score | $\text{CEPS} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\vert E \vert} \sum_{e \in E} \vert B_{e} - B_{\text{ref}} \vert$ | $[0, 1]$, ideal $= 1$ | Behavioral consistency across env. set $E$ |
| 6 | Test Execution Reduction Ratio | $\text{TERR} = 1 - \dfrac{\vert T_{\text{AI}} \vert}{\vert T_{\text{baseline}} \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$, higher is better | Quantifies AI-driven efficiency gain |
| 7 | Mean Time to Adapt | $\text{MTTA} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{\text{stable},i} - t_{\text{drift},i})$ | seconds / minutes | Responsiveness SLA metric |
| 8 | Risk Score (single module) | $R(m) = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_m + b\right)$ | sigmoid $\to [0,1]$ | Probability that module $m$ is defective |
| 9 | Cosine Similarity (test reuse) | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\vert \mathbf{u} \vert \, \vert \mathbf{v} \vert}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Deduplicating AI-generated test cases |
| 10 | Edit Distance (DOM drift) | $\text{Lev}(s, t) = \text{min ops to transform } s \to t$ | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ | Quantifies UI structural drift magnitude |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking/FinTech**: Predictive models flag high-risk transaction modules before nightly regression runs, saving 40–60% of CI minutes.
- **E-Commerce**: Responsive testing auto-heals locators when seasonal UI redesigns occur (e.g., Diwali, Black Friday), preventing test-suite collapse.
- **Healthcare Apps (FDA-regulated)**: Cross-device parity ensures identical diagnostic output on iPad Pro (M2), Samsung Galaxy Tab, and Surface Pro.
- **Automotive (Infotainment)**: GenAI generates test variants for Android Auto, Apple CarPlay, and proprietary OS environments.
- **Telecom (5G/Edge)**: Network-aware test generation validates behavior under varying latency profiles (1 ms MEC vs. 80 ms rural LTE).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Risk-Threshold $\tau^*$

The objective in predictive testing is to choose a threshold $\tau \in [0, 1]$ such that the **expected testing cost** is minimized. Let us derive it step by step.

**Step 1 — Define the cost function.** Let $C_{FN}$ be the cost of a missed defect (false negative) and $C_{FP}$ be the cost of testing a false-positive file (unnecessary test execution). The expected cost per module is:

$$
C(\tau) = C_{FN} \cdot \text{FNR}(\tau) \cdot P(D) + C_{FP} \cdot \text{FPR}(\tau) \cdot P(\bar{D})
$$

where $P(D)$ is the base defect rate, $\text{FNR}(\tau) = P(\hat{Y} < \tau \mid D = 1)$, and $\text{FPR}(\tau) = P(\hat{Y} \geq \tau \mid D = 0)$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate and set to zero.** The optimum occurs when the marginal cost of reducing $\text{FNR}$ equals the marginal cost of increasing $\text{FPR}$. Equivalently:

$$
\frac{dC}{d\tau} = -C_{FN} \cdot P(D) \cdot f_{D}(\tau) + C_{FP} \cdot P(\bar{D}) \cdot f_{\bar{D}}(\tau) = 0
$$

where $f_D$ and $f_{\bar{D}}$ are the score densities for defective and non-defective modules respectively.

**Step 3 — Solve for $\tau^*$.** Rearranging:

$$
C_{FN} \cdot P(D) \cdot f_{D}(\tau^*) = C_{FP} \cdot P(\bar{D}) \cdot f_{\bar{D}}(\tau^*)
$$

Hence the optimal threshold satisfies:

$$
\tau^* = \arg\min_{\tau} \left[ C_{FN} \cdot P(D) \cdot (1 - \text{DPR}(\tau)) + C_{FP} \cdot (1 - P(D)) \cdot (1 - \text{DPP}'(\tau)) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Practical interpretation.** In KTU exam answers, you may state: *"$\tau^*$ is the threshold at which the cost-weighted balance of false negatives and false positives is minimized; it is found by differentiating the expected cost and equating it to zero."*

### 3.2 Derivation of Cross-Environment Parity Score (CEPS)

**Step 1 — Define the reference behavior.** Let $B_{\text{ref}}$ be the canonical, gold-master behavior vector for a user journey (response time, UI element presence, status codes, etc.).

**Step 2 — Define per-environment behavior.** For each environment $e \in E$, observe $B_e$.

**Step 3 — Normalize the deviation.** Per-environment deviation is:

$$
\Delta_e = \frac{1}{d} \sum_{j=1}^{d} \frac{\vert B_{e,j} - B_{\text{ref},j} \vert}{\max(B_{\text{ref},j}, \epsilon)}
$$

where $d$ is the dimensionality of the behavior vector and $\epsilon$ is a small constant to avoid division by zero.

**Step 4 — Aggregate.** The CEPS is:

$$
\text{CEPS} = 1 - \frac{1}{\vert E \vert} \sum_{e \in E} \Delta_e
$$

This derivation demonstrates *why* CEPS is bounded in $[0, 1]$ and *how* it penalizes environment drift.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Predictive vs. Responsive vs. Cross-Environment Testing

| Dimension | **Predictive Testing** | **Responsive Testing** | **Cross-Device/Env Testing** |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| Core Question | *Where will defects occur?* | *How do we adapt when the SUT changes?* | *Does the SUT behave identically everywhere?* |
| Primary AI Technique | LLMs, Gradient Boosting, Graph Neural Nets on code embeddings | Self-healing via LLMs + visual CNNs + DOM-tree GANs | Generative vision models, emulator-based RL, synthetic data GANs |
| Input Data | Commits, churn, historical defects, code metrics | Runtime UI traces, DOM snapshots, API schemas | Device farms, OS/browser matrix, network profiles |
| Output | Risk-ranked test queue, prioritized suites | Auto-repaired locators, regenerated assertions | Environment-specific test variants, parity reports |
| Key Metric (KTU) | DPP, DPR, F1 | SHSR, MTTA | CEPS, TERR |
| Failure Mode | False negatives (missed critical bugs) | Self-healing hallucination (wrong repair) | Masked device-specific defects |
| CI/CD Stage | Pre-merge / pre-deploy | During & post-deploy | Parallel across device matrix |
| KTU Exam Cue Word | *"Anticipatory"*, *"Risk-based"* | *"Adaptive"*, *"Self-healing"* | *"Environment-aware"*, *"Parity"* |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (CEPS)

Suppose a login journey is tested across $E = \{$Chrome-Win11, Safari-iOS17, Chrome-Android14$\}$. The reference behavior vector $B_{\text{ref}} = (1.0, 1.0, 1.0)$ (all nominal: success, no error, response $< 2$s).

Observed:
- $B_{\text{Chrome-Win11}} = (1.0, 1.0, 1.0)$
- $B_{\text{Safari-iOS17}} = (1.0, 0.9, 1.0)$ — minor UI lag
- $B_{\text{Chrome-Android14}} = (1.0, 1.0, 0.85)$ — slow network on low-end device

Compute $\Delta_e$ for each (with $\epsilon = 0.01$):

- $\Delta_{\text{Chrome-Win11}} = \frac{1}{3}(0 + 0 + 0) = 0.000$
- $\Delta_{\text{Safari-iOS17}} = \frac{1}{3}(0 + 0.10 + 0) \approx 0.033$
- $\Delta_{\text{Chrome-Android14}} = \frac{1}{3}(0 + 0 + 0.176) \approx 0.059$

Then:

$$
\text{CEPS} = 1 - \frac{1}{3}(0.000 + 0.033 + 0.059) = 1 - 0.031 = 0.969
$$

**Interpretation:** A CEPS of 0.969 indicates 96.9% cross-environment parity — a strong result. Anything below 0.90 typically triggers a release-block in industry practice.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture: GenAI-Driven Predictive + Responsive Testing Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the end-to-end architecture for GenAI-augmented predictive and responsive testing across devices and environments. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters per protocol; no markdown formatting appears inside any double-quoted label.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Historical Codebase] --> B1[Feature Extractor]
    A2[Commit History] --> B1
    A3[Defect Logs] --> B1
    A4[Test Execution Traces] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[LLM Embedding Model]
    C1 --> D1[Defect Risk Predictor]
    D1 --> E1[Risk Score R of M]
    E1 --> F1{Threshold Tau Check}
    F1 -->|High Risk| G1[Prioritized Test Suite Queue]
    F1 -->|Low Risk| G2[Regression Pool]
    G1 --> H1[CI CD Pipeline]
    G2 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Cloud Device Farm]
    I1 --> J1[Android Devices]
    I1 --> J2[iOS Devices]
    I1 --> J3[Windows Browsers]
    I1 --> J4[Network Simulators]
    H1 --> K1[Runtime Drift Monitor]
    K1 --> K2[Visual CNN Diff]
    K1 --> K3[DOM Tree Comparator]
    K1 --> K4[API Schema Validator]
    K2 --> L1[GenAI Self Healing Engine]
    K3 --> L1
    K4 --> L1
    L1 --> M1[Repaired Locators]
    L1 --> M2[Regenerated Assertions]
    L1 --> M3[Synthetic Test Data]
    L1 --> N1[Cross Environment Parity Evaluator]
    J1 --> N1
    J2 --> N1
    J3 --> N1
    J4 --> N1
    N1 --> O1[QA Dashboard Report]
    O1 --> P1[Feedback Loop to LLM Embedding Model]
    P1 --> C1
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow: Predictive Layer vs. Responsive Layer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PredictiveLayer[Predictive Anticipation Layer]
        PA1[Input Code Churn and History] --> PA2[Vectorization]
        PA2 --> PA3[Transformer Risk Model]
        PA3 --> PA4[Risk Score R of M]
        PA4 --> PA5[Test Prioritization]
    end
    subgraph ResponsiveLayer[Responsive Adaptation Layer]
        RA1[Drift Signal Ingestion] --> RA2[Root Cause Classifier]
        RA2 --> RA3[Self Healing Locators]
        RA2 --> RA4[Assertion Regeneration]
        RA2 --> RA5[Environment Variant Synthesis]
    end
    subgraph CrossEnvLayer[Cross Device Environment Layer]
        CA1[Device Matrix Orchestrator] --> CA2[Network Profile Simulator]
        CA2 --> CA3[Parity Score Computation]
    end
    PA5 --> RA1
    RA3 --> CA1
    RA4 --> CA1
    RA5 --> CA1
```

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram: Self-Healing Locator Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant TS as Test Script
    participant SH as Self Healing Engine
    participant LLM as GenAI Model
    participant DOM as DOM Snapshot Store
    TS->>SH: Locator Failure Exception
    SH->>DOM: Retrieve Current DOM Tree
    DOM-->>SH: Updated DOM Snapshot
    SH->>LLM: Prompt with Old Locator and Current DOM
    LLM-->>SH: Proposed New Locator with Confidence Score
    SH->>TS: Inject New Locator and Retry Action
    TS-->>SH: Action Result
    SH->>SH: Log Healing Event and Update SHSR Metric
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix Diagram: When to Apply Predictive vs. Responsive vs. Both

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is there a large historical defect corpus available?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is the application UI highly dynamic with frequent redesigns?}
    Q1 -->|No| R1[Use Lightweight Statistical Risk Models]
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2[Apply Both Predictive and Responsive GenAI]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Are there many device OS browser permutations?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Apply Cross Environment GenAI Synthesis]
    Q3 -->|No| R4[Apply Predictive Testing Only]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
*Define predictive testing in the context of GenAI-driven software testing. State any two metrics used to evaluate its effectiveness.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Predictive testing is an anticipatory testing paradigm in which Generative AI models (typically LLMs or gradient-boosted classifiers) are trained on historical defect data, code churn, and complexity metrics to assign a *defect probability score* to each module prior to test execution. Tests targeting high-probability modules are prioritized in the CI/CD pipeline.
Two evaluation metrics: **Defect Prediction Precision (DPP)** and **Defect Prediction Recall (DPR)**; alternatively, F1-score or AUC-ROC. *(1 Mark – Definition, 1 Mark – Mechanism, 1 Mark – Metrics.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between predictive and responsive testing. Give one real-world scenario where responsive testing is indispensable.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Aspect | Predictive | Responsive |
|--------|------------|------------|
| Trigger | Pre-execution risk score | Runtime drift signal |
| Goal | Avoid future bugs | Survive current changes |

**Real-world scenario:** An e-commerce platform undergoing a *seasonal UI redesign* (e.g., Diwali sale). Responsive testing with self-healing locators is indispensable because hand-coded XPath locators break en masse and manual repair is infeasible within the launch window. *(1 Mark – Tabular differentiation, 1 Mark – Definition of responsive, 1 Mark – Scenario justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B Module 4 carries 14 marks. Students answer **one** of two alternatives. Each alternative has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Predictive + Cross-Environment Focus**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Explain in detail the architecture of a GenAI-driven **predictive testing** system. Include the data inputs, the AI model used, the risk-scoring mechanism, and how test prioritization is performed. Use the formula $R(m) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_m + b)$ to illustrate scoring.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* A SaaS company tests a payment module across four environments: Chrome-Win10, Safari-macOS, Chrome-Android, and Safari-iOS. Reference success rate vector $B_{\text{ref}} = (1, 1, 1)$. Observed vectors:
- Chrome-Win10: $(1.0, 1.0, 1.0)$
- Safari-macOS: $(1.0, 0.95, 1.0)$
- Chrome-Android: $(0.98, 1.0, 0.90)$
- Safari-iOS: $(1.0, 1.0, 0.85)$

Compute the **Cross-Environment Parity Score (CEPS)** and interpret whether the release should be blocked (assume industry threshold $\geq 0.90$).

**Model Solution (a):**
1. **Data Inputs (2 Marks):** Historical commits, code churn, cyclomatic complexity, ownership graph, prior defect density, test execution logs.
2. **AI Model (2 Marks):** Transformer-based code embedding (e.g., CodeBERT) followed by a classification head. The model outputs $R(m) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_m + b) \in [0,1]$, where $\mathbf{x}_m$ is the embedding of module $m$, $\mathbf{w}$ the learned weight vector, and $b$ the bias.
3. **Threshold + Prioritization (2 Marks):** A threshold $\tau$ (often derived via cost minimization) partitions modules into high/low risk. Tests targeting modules with $R(m) \geq \tau$ are promoted; the rest fall back to nightly regression.
4. **Feedback (1 Mark):** Post-execution defects feed back into training, enabling online improvement.

**Model Solution (b):**

Step 1 — Compute per-environment deviation $\Delta_e$ using $\Delta_e = \frac{1}{d}\sum_j \frac{\vert B_{e,j} - B_{\text{ref},j} \vert}{\max(B_{\text{ref},j}, \epsilon)}$ with $\epsilon = 0.01$ and $d=3$:

- $\Delta_{\text{Chrome-Win10}} = \frac{1}{3}(0 + 0 + 0) = 0.000$
- $\Delta_{\text{Safari-macOS}} = \frac{1}{3}(0 + 0.05 + 0) \approx 0.0167$
- $\Delta_{\text{Chrome-Android}} = \frac{1}{3}(0.02 + 0 + 0.10) \approx 0.0400$
- $\Delta_{\text{Safari-iOS}} = \frac{1}{3}(0 + 0 + 0.15) = 0.0500$

Step 2 — Aggregate into CEPS:

$$
\text{CEPS} = 1 - \frac{1}{4}(0.000 + 0.0167 + 0.0400 + 0.0500) = 1 - 0.0267 \approx 0.973
$$

Step 3 — Interpretation (1 Mark): CEPS = **0.973**, which exceeds the 0.90 threshold. **Release approved.** Note that Safari-iOS is the weakest link and warrants targeted remediation before the next release.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Responsive + Adaptive Self-Healing Focus**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Describe the operational workflow of a GenAI-based **responsive self-healing** mechanism for UI test locators. Include the role of the visual CNN, the DOM-tree comparator, and the LLM-driven locator regeneration.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* A banking app uses self-healing across 200 test cases. In a sprint, 150 locator failures occurred. The GenAI engine successfully auto-repaired 132 of them. Compute the **Self-Healing Success Rate (SHSR)** and **Mean Time to Adapt (MTTA)** given the following drift timestamps (in minutes from sprint start): drift at $t = [10, 25, 40, 55]$, stabilization at $t = [11.2, 26.8, 41.5, 57.0]$.

**Model Solution (a):**
1. **Drift Detection (2 Marks):** Visual CNN performs pixel-level diffing of UI screenshots; DOM-tree comparator computes edit distance between the previous and current DOM trees; API schema validator detects contract changes.
2. **Root-Cause Inference (1 Mark):** A multimodal LLM classifier categorizes drift as cosmetic / structural / semantic / environmental.
3. **Locator Regeneration (2 Marks):** The LLM is prompted with (a) the broken locator, (b) the current DOM subtree, (c) historical repair context. It proposes new XPath/CSS locators with a confidence score.
4. **Validation + Retry (1 Mark):** Proposed locators are dry-run against the live DOM; if matched and unique, the test script is hot-patched and re-executed. The repair event is logged for SHSR computation.
5. **Fallback (1 Mark):** If confidence < threshold, the failure is escalated to a human QA engineer.

**Model Solution (b):**

Step 1 — SHSR Computation (3 Marks):

$$
\text{SHSR} = \frac{\vert H_{\text{success}} \vert}{\vert H_{\text{total}} \vert} = \frac{132}{150} = 0.880
$$

**Interpretation:** SHSR = **88.0%**, below the industry target of 90%. The team should investigate the 18 unresolved failures.

Step 2 — MTTA Computation (4 Marks):

$$
\text{MTTA} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[(11.2-10) + (26.8-25) + (41.5-40) + (57.0-55)\bigr]
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{4}(1.2 + 1.8 + 1.5 + 2.0) = \frac{1}{4}(6.5) = 1.625 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Interpretation:** MTTA = **1.625 minutes**, indicating the self-healing engine adapts to drift in well under 2 minutes on average — meeting the responsiveness SLA.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic**
> 1. **Confusing predictive with responsive.** Predictive = *before* execution; responsive = *during/after* execution. Examiners deduct heavily when these are swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting to justify metric choice.** When asked "which metric?", do not just name it — state *why* it fits the scenario (e.g., CEPS for cross-environment, SHSR for self-healing).
> 3. **Skipping the feedback loop.** AI-driven testing is *not* a one-shot pipeline. Always mention continuous retraining/feedback — examiners expect it.
> 4. **Numerical errors in CEPS/MTTA.** Off-by-one errors in averaging across environments are the most common mark-loss vector in 14-mark numericals. Show every summation step explicitly.
> 5. **No formula citations.** Even for descriptive 7-mark questions, citing the relevant formula (e.g., $R(m) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_m + b)$) earns the "technical depth" credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **GenAI in Testing** = using generative models (LLM, GAN, VAE, Diffusion, Transformer) to *create*, *predict*, *adapt*, or *orchestrate* test artifacts.
- [ ] **Predictive Testing** = *anticipates* defects *before* execution via risk scoring $R(m) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_m + b)$.
- [ ] **Responsive Testing** = *adapts* tests *during/after* execution via drift detection + self-healing.
- [ ] **Cross-Device Testing** = verifies behavioral parity across Android, iOS, Windows, macOS, browsers.
- [ ] **Cross-Environment Testing** = parity across OS versions, network profiles, locales, screen DPIs.
- [ ] **Key Metrics to Memorize**: DPP, DPR, F1, SHSR, CEPS, TERR, MTTA.
- [ ] **Optimal Threshold** $\tau^*$ derived from cost minimization: $C_{FN} \cdot P(D) \cdot f_D(\tau^*) = C_{FP} \cdot P(\bar D) \cdot f_{\bar D}(\tau^*)$.
- [ ] **CEPS Formula**: $\text{CEPS} = 1 - \frac{1}{\vert E \vert}\sum_e \Delta_e$; ideal = 1; release threshold typically $\geq 0.90$.
- [ ] **Self-Healing Pipeline**: Drift Signal → DOM/Visual Snapshot → LLM Prompt → New Locator → Validate → Hot-Patch → Log SHSR.
- [ ] **Feedback Loop** is mandatory — without retraining, GenAI in testing degrades due to model drift.
- [ ] **Industry Target Benchmarks**: SHSR $\geq 90\%$; CEPS $\geq 0.90$; MTTA $\leq 5$ min; DPP $\geq 0.70$ for high-stakes systems.
- [ ] **Exam Cue Words**: *Anticipatory* (predictive), *Adaptive / Self-healing* (responsive), *Environment-aware / Parity* (cross-env).
- [ ] **Always pair** a metric with a *justification* in 14-mark answers — examiners reward the "why" as much as the "what."
- [ ] **Never** present predictive or responsive in isolation in a real-world answer — they are *synergistic* layers of a single AI-driven test architecture.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study - Implementation of black-box, grey-box, and responsive testing using PEX and AI-driven tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST631 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study - Implementation of black-box, grey-box, and responsive testing using PEX and AI-driven tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Black-Box Testing (Definition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Black-Box Testing** is a software testing methodology in which the tester evaluates the functionality of a software application **without knowledge of its internal code structure, implementation details, or internal pathways**. Test cases are derived solely from the **Software Requirement Specification (SRS)**, and the system is treated as an opaque "black box" where inputs are provided and outputs are observed for conformance to expected behavior.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)
Imagine you have a sealed **microwave oven**. You, as a user, press buttons (provide inputs like time, power level) and observe whether food comes out hot. You do **not** open the microwave to inspect the magnetron, the waveguide, or the circuitry. If the food heats up correctly, you accept the appliance. That is **black-box testing**. In software, we feed data to the GUI/API and compare the output against the specification — without ever looking at the source code.

### 1.1.2 Black-Box Sub-Techniques Relevant to PEX
| Technique | Purpose | Key Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Equivalence Partitioning (EP)** | Divides inputs into valid/invalid partitions | Partition coverage |
| **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** | Tests at partition edges | Boundary defects |
| **Decision Table Testing** | Models logical combinations | Rule coverage |
| **State Transition Testing** | Validates state-driven behavior | State coverage |
| **Pairwise Testing** | Tests all discrete pairs | Pair coverage |

---

## 1.2 Grey-Box Testing (Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grey-Box Testing** is a hybrid testing technique where the tester possesses **partial knowledge** of the internal structure (e.g., database schemas, architecture diagrams, algorithms) while still treating the application primarily as a black box. The tester designs test cases using both **functional specifications** and **limited structural insight** to target code paths more intelligently.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **car mechanic who knows the engine layout but does not disassemble it**. They use the OBD-II port (a limited structural window) to read fault codes while driving the car (functional behavior). They combine what they know about the engine (structure) with what the dashboard tells them (function) to find bugs. Grey-box testing follows the same philosophy.

### 1.2.2 Why Grey-Box Matters in Case Studies
In the PEX-driven case study, grey-box testing emerges because **PEX uses code-coverage feedback (a structural signal) to dynamically guide black-box input generation**. The tool *behaves* like a black-box generator externally, yet uses *internal coverage counters* internally — making it a textbook example of grey-box automation.

---

## 1.3 Responsive Testing (Definition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Responsive Testing** (also called **Responsive Design Testing** or **Cross-Device Testing**) verifies that a web/mobile application renders and behaves correctly across a variety of **viewports, devices, browsers, and operating systems**. It validates layout fluidity, element visibility, touch ergonomics, media-query breakpoints, and performance under varying screen resolutions.

### 1.3.1 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine publishing a **billboard advertisement**. Before printing, you preview it on paper cutouts sized for highway signs, bus stops, and subway walls to ensure the message is legible everywhere. Responsive testing does the same for software: it confirms the digital "billboard" (your application) reads correctly on a 4-inch phone, a 13-inch laptop, and a 65-inch smart TV.

---

## 1.4 PEX — Parameterized Exploration (Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PEX** (now evolved into **IntelliTest** within Visual Studio Enterprise) is a Microsoft Research–developed automated test-generation tool for the **.NET framework**. It employs **Dynamic Symbolic Execution (DSE)**, also known as **Concolic Testing**, to systematically explore execution paths and produce a minimal suite of high-coverage parameterized unit tests.

### 1.4.1 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **robot exploring a maze**. At every fork, instead of guessing randomly, the robot solves a small math puzzle that tells it *exactly* which input would turn left, right, or go straight. PEX does the same with code: it solves path conditions symbolically and generates the precise inputs that drive execution down each unexplored branch.

### 1.4.2 Key Terminology for PEX
- **Concrete Execution**: Running the program with actual numeric/string values.
- **Symbolic Execution**: Replacing inputs with symbolic variables and tracking constraints along each path.
- **Constraint Solver**: An SMT (Satisfiability Modulo Theories) engine (commonly **Z3**) that solves path constraints.
- **Path Condition**: The accumulated boolean predicate that must hold for an execution to follow a specific path.
- **Parameterized Unit Test (PUT)**: A unit test with parameters, where PEX supplies multiple inputs across runs.

---

## 1.5 AI-Driven Testing Tools (Definition)

> [!NOTE]
> **AI-Driven Testing Tools** are software applications that leverage **Machine Learning (ML), Natural Language Processing (NLP), Computer Vision (CV), and heuristic search algorithms** to autonomously generate test cases, identify defects, optimize regression suites, or self-heal brittle locators in UI tests.

### 1.5.1 Categories of AI in Software Testing
| Category | Function | Example Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Test Generation AI** | Creates test inputs/cases from requirements | **Testim.io**, **Mabl**, **Functionize** |
| **Visual AI** | Detects UI anomalies via CV | **Applitools Eyes**, **Percy** |
| **Predictive AI** | Forecasts high-risk modules | **Launchable**, **SeaLights** |
| **Self-Healing AI** | Repairs broken locators | **Healenium**, **Testim** |
| **Defect Triage AI** | Auto-classifies bug reports | **Ericsson AI Triage** |

---

Understanding *which tool implements which technique* is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in End Semester Examinations (ESE).

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept: How PEX Implements Black/Grey-Box Testing

PEX operates through a **five-stage concolic pipeline**. Understanding each stage is critical for KTU derivations and viva questions.

### Stage 1 — Concrete Execution (Initial Run)
PEX begins by running the **Parameterized Unit Test (PUT)** with **default/random concrete inputs**. It records the executed branch sequence and the **concrete state** of all symbolic variables.

### Stage 2 — Instrumentation & Symbolic Tracking
PEX instruments the .NET Intermediate Language (IL) using **Runtime Code Instrumentation**. Each input parameter is replaced with a symbolic variable `α`, `β`, `γ`, … and a **path condition (PC)** is accumulated as the program executes.

$$ PC = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} C_i $$

where $C_i$ is the boolean branch predicate at decision node $i$.

### Stage 3 — Branch Negation (Concolic Step)
PEX systematically **negates one branch predicate** at a time (depth-first or heuristic search) to construct a new PC for an unexplored path:

$$ PC_{new} = (PC_{old} \setminus \{C_k\}) \cup \{\neg C_k\} $$

### Stage 4 — Constraint Solving (SMT via Z3)
The new PC is dispatched to the **Z3 SMT solver**, which returns a satisfying assignment. If **SAT**, that assignment becomes the next concrete input; if **UNSAT**, the path is infeasible and is discarded.

### Stage 5 — Test Suite Minimization
PEX retains only inputs that **uniquely cover a new branch** (using the *minimization-by-uniqueness* heuristic) and emits them as **MSTest / xUnit** parameterized test cases.

---

## 2.2 Grey-Box Realization in PEX

PEX is a **grey-box tool by construction**:

| Black-Box Aspect | Grey-Box Aspect | White-Box Aspect |
|---|---|---|
| Tester writes only PUT signatures | Uses **branch-coverage feedback** internally | Instruments IL bytecode |
| No manual path enumeration | Uses **PC mutation** to target gaps | Full symbolic trace available |
| Inputs remain opaque | **Coverage counters** steer search | Constraint solver sees internals |

This makes PEX a textbook **grey-box automation framework**, satisfying the KTU Module 4 case-study requirement perfectly.

## 2.3 Responsive Testing Operational Pipeline

### 2.3.1 The Five Pillars of Responsive Validation
1. **Viewport Emulation** — Simulate device pixel ratios (DPR) and CSS viewport widths.
2. **Media Query Coverage** — Verify breakpoints at common widths: **320 px, 480 px, 768 px, 1024 px, 1280 px, 1920 px**.
3. **Touch vs. Mouse Affordance** — Validate that tap targets ≥ **44 × 44 CSS pixels** (Apple HIG) and ≥ **48 × 48 dp** (Material Design).
4. **Orientation Testing** — Verify portrait ↔ landscape transitions.
5. **Network Throttling** — Simulate 3G/4G/5G conditions to verify responsive performance budgets.

### 2.4.2 Self-Healing Locator Algorithm (Simplified)
$$ S_{final} = \arg\max_{s_i \in S} \Big( w_1 \cdot \text{sim}(s_i, d_{old}) + w_2 \cdot \text{sim}(s_i, d_{new}) + w_3 \cdot \text{rank}(s_i) \Big) $$

where $s_i$ is a candidate selector, $d_{old}$ and $d_{new}$ are DOM snapshots, and $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric / Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle \text{Line Coverage} = \frac{L_{executed}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Lines exercised |
| 2 | $\displaystyle \text{Branch Coverage} = \frac{B_{executed}}{2 \cdot B_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Decision outcomes tested |

| 6 | $\displaystyle \text{Path Condition: } PC = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} C_i$ | Symbolic execution predicate |
| 7 | $\displaystyle PC_{new} = PC_{old} \setminus \{C_k\} \cup \{\neg C_k\}$ | Concolic branch negation |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \text{Equivalence Partition} \rightarrow \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\}$ | BVA partitions |
| 9 | $\displaystyle \text{Off-by-one bounds} = \{ \min - 1,\ \min,\ \min + 1,\ \max - 1,\ \max,\ \max + 1 \}$ | BVA test points |

| 12 | $\displaystyle \text{Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)} = \frac{\sum t_{detect}}{N_{defects}}$ | AI defect-triage efficiency |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Case Study — Derivation: PEX Concolic Path Exploration

Consider the following C# (illustrative pseudo-code structure — not executed):

```
Function Divide(a : int, b : int) : int
    If b == 0 Then
        Throw New Exception
    End If
    If a > 100 Then
        Return a / b
    Else
        Return -1
    End If
End Function
```

### Step 1 — Symbolic Variables Assignment
Let the input domain be $a, b \in \mathbb{Z}$.

PEX assigns:
$$ a \mapsto \alpha, \quad b \mapsto \beta $$

### Step 2 — First Concrete Run
PEX starts with default/random values. Suppose it picks $a = 0,\ b = 1$.

Execution trace:

$$ \text{Path} : \neg(b == 0) \rightarrow \neg(a > 100) \rightarrow \text{Return } -1 $$

### Step 3 — Path Condition Construction

$$ PC_1 = (\beta \neq 0) \land (\alpha \leq 100) $$

### Step 4 — Negate First Predicate
Negating $C_1 = (\beta \neq 0)$ yields $\beta = 0$.

$$ PC_2 = (\beta = 0) \land (\alpha \leq 100) $$

Solving via Z3:
$$ \beta = 0,\ \alpha = 0 $$

This input ($a = 0,\ b = 0$) triggers the exception branch → new coverage.

### Step 5 — Negate Second Predicate (Keeping First Untaken)
Negating $C_2 = (\alpha \leq 100)$ yields $\alpha > 100$, while preserving $\beta \neq 0$:

$$ PC_3 = (\beta \neq 0) \land (\alpha > 100) $$

Solving via Z3:
$$ \beta = 1,\ \alpha = 101 $$

This triggers the third path: division of 101 by 1 = 101.

### Step 6 — Resulting Minimal Test Suite

| Test Case | $a$ | $b$ | Path Covered |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | 0 | 1 | Normal else-branch (return -1) |
| T2 | 0 | 0 | Exception branch |
| T3 | 101 | 1 | True-then-branch (return 101) |

**Coverage Achieved:** 100 % Branch Coverage (3 of 3 outcomes).

---

## 3.3 Derivative Computation — Mutation Score for AI-Generated Test Suite

Suppose an AI tool (Testim) generates **N = 50** test cases against a module with **M = 120** injected mutants. Of these, **M_k = 96** mutants are detected (killed).

Applying Formula 4 from the cheat sheet:

$$ \text{Mutation Score} = \frac{M_{killed}}{M_{total}} \times 100\% $$

Substituting:

$$ \text{Mutation Score} = \frac{96}{120} \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{Mutation Score} = 0.8 \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{Mutation Score} = 80\% $$

> **Interpretation:** An 80 % mutation score is considered **industry-acceptable** (the threshold is typically ≥ 75 %). AI-generated suites meeting this benchmark rival hand-crafted suites for routine CRUD modules.

---

## 3.4 Derivative Computation — Branch Coverage from PEX Output

Let PEX produce **5 parameterized tests** for a method containing **8 branches**. The Branch Coverage formula (Formula 2):

$$ \text{Branch Coverage} = \frac{B_{executed}}{2 \cdot B_{total}} \times 100\% $$

Substituting $B_{executed} = 7$ (one branch outcome unreachable due to infeasible path):

$$ \text{Branch Coverage} = \frac{7}{2 \cdot 8} \times 100\% $$

$$ = \frac{7}{16} \times 100\% $$

$$ = 43.75\% $$

> **KTU Note:** When PEX cannot reach a branch, it reports it as **"infeasible path"** — students must distinguish this from *uncovered* in their answers.

---

## 3.5 Responsive Testing — Cross-Viewport Defect Rate

A web application is tested across **6 viewport profiles**. Defects observed: $D = 14$, with $N = 6$ profiles.

$$ \text{Defects per Profile} = \frac{D}{N} = \frac{14}{6} \approx 2.33 $$

If the responsive baseline viewport is at 1280 px and defects scale linearly with DPR:

$$ D_{device} = D_{base} \times \left( \frac{DPR_{device}}{DPR_{base}} \right) $$

For a 3× DPR phone:

$$ D_{phone} = 14 \times \frac{3.0}{1.0} = 42\ \text{potential defects} $$

This shows why **DPR-aware responsive testing** is mandatory for mobile-first applications.

---

## 3.6 AI Self-Healing Locator — Numerical Walkthrough

Suppose a Selenium locator fails because the `id` attribute changed. Healenium ranks three candidates:

| Candidate | DOM Similarity | Visual Similarity | Historical Rank | Weights |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0.92 | 0.88 | 0.70 | $w_1 = 0.4, w_2 = 0.4, w_3 = 0.2$ |
| $s_2$ | 0.81 | 0.85 | 0.60 | Same |
| $s_3$ | 0.75 | 0.70 | 0.90 | Same |

Score for $s_1$:

$$ S_1 = 0.4 \cdot 0.92 + 0.4 \cdot 0.88 + 0.2 \cdot 0.70 $$

$$ = 0.368 + 0.352 + 0.14 $$

$$ = 0.860 $$

Score for $s_2$:

$$ S_2 = 0.4 \cdot 0.81 + 0.4 \cdot 0.85 + 0.2 \cdot 0.60 = 0.324 + 0.34 + 0.12 = 0.784 $$

Score for $s_3$:

$$ S_3 = 0.4 \cdot 0.75 + 0.4 \cdot 0.70 + 0.2 \cdot 0.90 = 0.30 + 0.28 + 0.18 = 0.760 $$

**Winner:** $s_1$ with score **0.860**. Healenium auto-substitutes $s_1$ in the broken test, achieving **self-healing**.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PEX Concolic Execution Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parameterized Unit Test PUT] --> B[Concrete Execution Engine]
    B --> C[IL Instrumentation Layer]
    C --> D[Symbolic Variable Tracker]
    D --> E[Path Condition Builder]
    E --> F{Branch Untaken?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Negate Predicate PC Negation]
    F -- No --> H[Test Suite Minimization]
    G --> I[Z3 SMT Constraint Solver]
    I --> J{SAT?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Generate New Concrete Input]
    J -- No --> L[Mark Path Infeasible]
    K --> B
    L --> F
    H --> M[Emit MSTest or xUnit Suite]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The loop continues until branch coverage saturates. Each iteration either discovers a new branch (via SAT) or discards an unreachable path (via UNSAT).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is PEX? List any two advantages of using PEX for unit testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**PEX (Parameterized Exploration)** is a Microsoft .NET-based automated unit-test generation tool that uses **Dynamic Symbolic Execution (Concolic Testing)** combined with the **Z3 SMT solver** to produce high-coverage parameterized test suites with minimal human intervention.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **High Coverage Automation** — Achieves near 100 % branch coverage without manually writing edge-case tests.
2. **Fault Detection at Boundaries** — Systematically discovers off-by-one and boundary defects through symbolic constraint solving.
3. **Reduced Maintenance** — Generated tests are stored as parameterized MSTest/xUnit cases that can be regression-run indefinitely.

> **Mark Allocation:** Definition 1 + Advantages 2 × 1 = 3.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the architecture of PEX with a neat block diagram. Describe how PEX implements **concolic testing** to achieve branch coverage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a function `AbsoluteDiff(x, y)` that returns `|x − y|` but throws an exception if both inputs are negative. Apply **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** and **Equivalence Partitioning (EP)** to derive the complete test suite. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — PEX Architecture & Concolic Testing (7 marks)

**Architecture (3 marks):**

The PEX pipeline consists of five stages:

1. **Instrumentation Layer** — PEX intercepts .NET Intermediate Language (IL) at runtime, replacing concrete parameters with **symbolic variables** $\alpha, \beta, \gamma \dots$
2. **Execution Monitor** — Records every branch decision and accumulates the **Path Condition (PC)**:

$$ PC = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} C_i $$

3. **Branch Selector** — Picks the next untaken branch predicate $C_k$ and negates it:

$$ PC_{new} = (PC_{old} \setminus \{C_k\}) \cup \{\neg C_k\} $$

4. **Constraint Solver (Z3 SMT)** — Determines whether $PC_{new}$ is **SAT** (satisfiable) or **UNSAT** (infeasible path).
5. **Test Emitter** — Converts satisfiable assignments into MSTest/xUnit parameterized unit tests and minimizes the suite using the *uniqueness heuristic*.

**Concolic Mechanism (4 marks):**

- **Concrete Phase:** PEX first runs the PUT with concrete values to obtain an initial trace.
- **Symbolic Phase:** Concurrently, the symbolic tracker builds the PC.
- **Negation Phase:** Each untaken branch is systematically negated to force exploration of new paths.
- **Solving Phase:** Z3 solves the modified PC; if SAT, the resulting inputs drive a new concrete run.
- **Loop Closure:** The process repeats until **branch coverage saturation** or a configurable time limit.

> **Diagram Required:** Students should reproduce the Mermaid flowchart from Section 4.1 (or equivalent) for full 7 marks.

---

#### Part (b) — BVA + EP for `AbsoluteDiff(x, y)` (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Input Domain Analysis (1 mark):**
Inputs $x$ and $y$ are integers. The function throws an exception when **both** $x < 0$ **and** $y < 0$.

**Step 2 — Equivalence Partitions (2 marks):**

| Partition ID | Description | Validity |
|---|---|---|
| EP1 | $x \geq 0, y \geq 0$ | Valid |
| EP2 | $x < 0, y \geq 0$ | Valid |
| EP3 | $x \geq 0, y < 0$ | Valid |
| EP4 | $x < 0, y < 0$ | **Invalid (Exception)** |

**Step 3 — Boundary Values (2 marks):**

| Boundary | Min | Min+1 | Max−1 | Max |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Value | $-2^{31}$ | $-2^{31}+1$ | $2^{31}-2$ | $2^{31}-1$ |
| Significance | INT_MIN | Just above MIN | Just below MAX | INT_MAX |

For the exception partition (EP4), the boundary is at $x = -1$ and $y = -1$.

**Step 4 — Final Test Suite (2 marks):**

| TC | $x$ | $y$ | Expected Output | Partition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | EP1 (BVA: 0,0) |
| T2 | 100 | 50 | 50 | EP1 |
| T3 | -5 | 10 | 15 | EP2 |
| T4 | 10 | -5 | 15 | EP3 |
| T5 | -1 | -1 | Exception thrown | EP4 |
| T6 | INT_MIN | 0 | $2^{31}$ | EP2 BVA |
| T7 | 0 | INT_MAX | $2^{31}-1$ | EP3 BVA |

**Coverage:** All four partitions + critical boundary points.

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss how **AI-driven testing tools** implement self-healing locators in UI test automation. Provide a numerical example of the self-healing score computation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the operational pipeline of **responsive testing** using AI-driven visual validation. List any four cross-viewport defects commonly detected by such tools. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — AI Self-Healing Locators (7 marks)

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**
When UI element attributes (`id`, `xpath`, `css`) change due to refactoring, traditional Selenium tests fail. AI-driven tools like **Healenium** and **Testim** compute a **multi-factor similarity score** combining:

1. **DOM Structural Similarity** — Cosine similarity between old and new DOM trees.
2. **Visual Similarity** — CNN-based image embedding distance.
3. **Historical Rank** — Frequency of successful past usage of the selector.

The candidate selector with the **highest weighted score** replaces the broken one.

**Scoring Formula (1 mark):**

$$ S_{final} = w_1 \cdot \text{sim}_{DOM} + w_2 \cdot \text{sim}_{visual} + w_3 \cdot \text{rank}_{hist} $$

subject to $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$.

**Numerical Example (3 marks):**
Given weights $w_1 = 0.4,\ w_2 = 0.4,\ w_3 = 0.2$ and three candidates:

| Candidate | DOM Sim | Visual Sim | Hist Rank |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0.92 | 0.88 | 0.70 |
| $s_2$ | 0.81 | 0.85 | 0.60 |
| $s_3$ | 0.75 | 0.70 | 0.90 |

Compute scores (as derived in Section 3.6):

$$ S_1 = 0.4 \cdot 0.92 + 0.4 \cdot 0.88 + 0.2 \cdot 0.70 = 0.860 $$

$$ S_2 = 0.4 \cdot 0.81 + 0.4 \cdot 0.85 + 0.2 \cdot 0.60 = 0.784 $$

$$ S_3 = 0.4 \cdot 0.75 + 0.4 \cdot 0.70 + 0.2 \cdot 0.90 = 0.760 $$

**Healenium selects $s_1$** (score 0.860) and continues execution without test failure.

---

#### Part (b) — Responsive Testing Pipeline & Common Defects (7 marks)

**Pipeline Stages (3 marks):**

1. **Test Authoring** — Define flows in BrowserStack or Applitools.
2. **Parallel Multi-Viewport Execution** — Run the same flow across 320 px, 414 px, 768 px, 1024 px, 1280 px, 1920 px, 3840 px.
3. **Visual AI Baseline Comparison** — Applitools' CNN compares each screenshot to a baseline pixel-by-pixel, ignoring anti-aliasing noise.
4. **DOM/Element Assertion** — Verifies tap-target sizes ≥ 44 × 44 px and breakpoint transitions.
5. **Network Throttling** — Simulates 3G/4G/5G to validate performance budgets.
6. **Reporting** — Generates unified cross-device defect dashboard.

**Four Common Cross-Viewport Defects (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Text Overflow / Truncation** — Long strings breaking card layouts on narrow phones.
2. **Misaligned Tap Targets** — Buttons shrinking below the 44 × 44 px minimum, causing usability failures.
3. **Image Distortion** — Non-responsive images stretching disproportionately on 4K displays.
4. **Hidden Navigation** — Hamburger menus failing to expand on certain DPR profiles.
5. *(Bonus)* **Font Legibility** — Body text below 14 px on high-DPR phones violating WCAG accessibility.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Calling PEX a "black-box tool" alone** — PEX is **grey-box by design**. Writing only "black-box" loses at least **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing symbolic execution with random testing** — PEX is **deterministic**, not random. State this clearly.
> 3. **Forgetting the Z3 solver** — Any PEX answer without mentioning **SMT/Z3** is incomplete (-1 mark).
> 4. **Skipping the negation step formula** $PC_{new} = PC_{old} \setminus \{C_k\} \cup \{\neg C_k\}$ — this is the **single most-tested derivation**.
> 5. **Treating responsive testing as only "resize the browser"** — must mention **DPR, touch targets, network throttling, and AI visual validation**.
> 6. **Mixing up coverage formulas** — Branch coverage denominator is $2 \cdot B_{total}$, not $B_{total}$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **PEX** = Parameterized Exploration; .NET-based concolic test generator using **Z3 SMT solver**.
- PEX = **grey-box** tool (externally black, internally white).
- **Concolic** = concrete + symbolic execution combined.
- **Path Condition (PC)** = $\bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} C_i$.
- **Negation rule** = $PC_{new} = PC_{old} \setminus \{C_k\} \cup \{\neg C_k\}$.
- **Black-box** techniques: EP, BVA, Decision Tables, State Transition, Pairwise.
- **Grey-box** = partial structural insight + functional testing.
- **Responsive** breakpoints to memorize: **320, 414, 768, 1024, 1280, 1920, 3840 px**.
- **Tap-target minimum** = **44 × 44 px** (Apple HIG) / **48 × 48 dp** (Material).
- **AI self-healing** combines DOM + visual + rank similarities.
- **Applitools** = Visual AI CV-based regression.
- **Testim / Mabl / Functionize** = AI-driven test generators.
- **Mutation score** ≥ **75 %** is industry-acceptable.
- **Branch coverage** denominator = $2 \cdot B_{total}$.
- **Infeasible path** ≠ uncovered path (PEX distinguishes them).
- **DPR-aware** responsive testing scales defect rate linearly with pixel density.
- **PEX emits MSTest/xUnit** parameterized unit tests as final output.
- **Visual AI** uses CNNs to compare screenshots while tolerating anti-aliasing noise.
- **NLP-based test synthesis** uses BERT/Transformer models to parse requirements into test cases.
- Always cite **Z3** whenever discussing PEX; examiners expect this name.
